stem: An architect is designing an assistant that must answer from a large internal knowledge base whose articles are edited daily. They are weighing fine-tuning against retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Which choice best fits the requirement, and why?
options: Fine-tuning, because baking the corpus into the model weights removes the need to operate a retrieval layer and keeps answers current automatically
RAG, because relevant passages are retrieved at query time, keeping answers grounded in the current corpus and updatable without retraining
Fine-tuning, because a task-specialized model answers with lower latency and avoids the cost of maintaining a vector index
RAG, but only after the knowledge base is frozen so a single stable index never has to be rebuilt
rationale: RAG retrieves relevant content at query time, so a frequently-updated corpus stays current by re-indexing rather than retraining, and answers can cite sources. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights that go stale and cannot reflect daily edits without repeated retraining; RAG does not require freezing the corpus.
referenceText: Retrieval-augmented generation grounds answers in retrievable, updatable sources.

stem: A team is hardening a production RAG system built on Claude. Which two practices most improve efficiency and answer quality? (Select TWO.)
options: Chunk and index the sources, then retrieve only the most relevant passages per query to pass alongside the question
Cache large, stable instruction and context prefixes with prompt caching to cut repeated cost and latency
Retrieve only the single highest-scoring chunk and discard all others to minimize token usage
Concatenate the entire corpus into every request to guarantee no relevant passage is ever missed
Skip building an evaluation harness initially and add one only after users report wrong answers
rationale: Retrieving only the most relevant chunks per query and caching stable prefixes are the core efficiency/quality levers. Forcing a single chunk often drops needed context; sending the whole corpus is costly and dilutes relevance; deferring evals leaves quality unmeasured.
referenceText: RAG retrieves relevant context per query; prompt caching reduces cost and latency for stable prefixes.

stem: A workload involves a difficult multi-step analytical task where correctness matters most and higher latency is acceptable. Which model strategy best fits?
options: Choose a smaller, faster model and raise temperature to broaden the reasoning paths it explores
Use a mid-tier model with a low max_tokens cap to keep the reasoning concise
Select the most capable model and enable extended thinking for the hardest reasoning steps
Route each step to a different small model and majority-vote across their answers
rationale: Matching model capability to task difficulty is the principle: the most capable model, optionally with extended thinking, suits hard reasoning when latency is tolerable. Downsizing plus higher temperature trades away reliability, capping tokens truncates reasoning, and ensembling weak models does not reliably recover frontier reasoning.
referenceText: Match model capability to task difficulty; extended thinking aids hard reasoning.

stem: A long-context feature sends the same large system prompt on every call across thousands of daily requests. Which technique most reduces cost and latency without dropping needed context?
options: Enable prompt caching on the stable system-prompt prefix so it is reused across calls
Lower the temperature so the model generates fewer output tokens per response
Truncate the system prompt on each call, removing context the task depends on
Disable response streaming so the full response returns in a single payload
rationale: Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix across requests, cutting both cost and latency while preserving the context. Temperature affects randomness, not input cost; truncation drops needed context; streaming affects perceived latency of delivery, not compute cost.
referenceText: Prompt caching reuses stable prefixes to reduce cost and latency.

stem: A nightly job scores millions of records; it is not latency-sensitive and can complete over several hours. Which integration pattern is most cost-effective?
options: Issue synchronous real-time requests one record at a time to keep the pipeline simple
Submit the records through the Message Batches API for asynchronous bulk processing at reduced per-token cost
Stream each request individually so partial results become available sooner
Fine-tune a dedicated model on the records and run inference on it locally
rationale: Large, delay-tolerant workloads are the ideal case for the Message Batches API, which processes asynchronously (within 24 hours) at roughly 50% lower cost. Synchronous or streamed one-at-a-time calls forgo the discount and strain rate limits; fine-tuning is unrelated to bulk scoring economics.
referenceText: Message Batches API processes large asynchronous jobs at reduced cost.

stem: An enterprise on AWS wants to run Claude so that inference stays within its existing cloud and governance boundary. Which option supports this?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, which serves the models inside the customer's AWS environment and governance boundary
Use only the first-party Anthropic API, which can be pinned to a chosen AWS region on request
Download the Claude model weights and self-host them on the customer's own EC2 instances
Proxy first-party API calls through AWS PrivateLink, since Claude has no native AWS availability
rationale: Claude is offered through Amazon Bedrock (and Google Vertex AI), letting teams deploy within their existing cloud and governance boundary. The first-party API is Anthropic-hosted, weights are not distributed for self-hosting, and a PrivateLink proxy is unnecessary because native AWS availability exists.
referenceText: Claude is available on Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI for in-cloud deployment.

stem: Before rolling out a prompt-and-model change to production, a team wants a rigorous, objective read on whether quality actually improved. What should they do?
options: Run a versioned, representative evaluation set with graded success criteria and compare scores across the two versions
Review a handful of sample outputs from each version and judge which reads better overall
Ship the change and treat the volume of subsequent user complaints as the quality signal
Compare the per-request token cost of each version and keep whichever is cheaper
rationale: A representative, versioned eval with graded criteria yields an objective before/after comparison. Reading a few samples is anecdotal, complaint-driven QA is lagging and biased, and token cost measures spend rather than quality.
referenceText: Develop empirical, graded evaluations to measure quality changes.

stem: A team wants to use an LLM-as-judge to scale grading in their evaluation pipeline. Which use is appropriate?
options: Score outputs against an explicit rubric to scale grading, calibrated against a set of human-labeled examples
Permanently replace all human review with an uncalibrated judge to eliminate grading labor entirely
Grade outputs holistically with no rubric so the judge is not constrained by predefined criteria
Reuse the exact prompt that generated the output as the judge prompt so scoring matches the generator's intent
rationale: An LLM judge scales grading only when driven by an explicit rubric and calibrated against human labels. Fully replacing human review without calibration removes ground truth, rubric-free grading is inconsistent, and reusing the generation prompt bakes in the generator's biases rather than independently assessing quality.
referenceText: LLM-as-judge should use an explicit rubric calibrated to human labels.

stem: A solution processes regulated personal and health data subject to GDPR and HIPAA. Which two governance practices are appropriate? (Select TWO.)
options: Minimize and control which sensitive fields are sent, and select a deployment and data-handling posture that meets the applicable compliance regime
Maintain human oversight and audit logging for consequential automated decisions
Forward complete raw records including all PII by default to avoid building a redaction step
Disable request and decision logging entirely so no sensitive data is ever retained anywhere
Rely on the model's built-in safety training as the sole control for handling regulated data
rationale: Data minimization with a compliant deployment posture, plus human oversight and audit trails, are core governance controls for regulated data. Defaulting to sending all PII violates minimization, disabling all logging destroys the auditability compliance requires, and model safety training alone is not a governance control.
referenceText: Governance for regulated data: minimization, compliant posture, oversight, and audit logging.

stem: An agent feature ingests untrusted external content and can call tools that take real actions. The team is worried about prompt injection. Which mitigation approach is most appropriate?
options: Treat untrusted content as data behind clear delimiters, constrain tools to least privilege, and add guardrails and monitoring
Grant the agent broad tool permissions but instruct it in the system prompt to ignore any malicious requests
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to reason its way past any injected instructions
Depend on the model refusing harmful instructions, since refusal training already blocks injection attempts
rationale: Defense-in-depth is required: isolate untrusted content as data, scope tools to least privilege, and layer guardrails and monitoring. A broad-permission agent plus a hopeful instruction is fragile, extra tokens do not prevent injection, and refusal training alone is not a sufficient control against tool misuse.
referenceText: Mitigate prompt injection with isolation, least-privilege tools, and guardrails.

stem: An architect must brief non-technical executives on whether a Claude solution is ready for launch. Which communication approach is most effective?
options: Frame business outcomes, key risks and their mitigations, evaluation evidence, and a cost and rollout plan in stakeholder terms
Walk through the model's benchmark token throughput and context-window figures in technical detail
Present the API request parameters and prompt structure that were tuned during development
Assure the executives the system is fully accurate and will require no further oversight after launch
rationale: Executive communication should translate technical work into business outcomes, risks and mitigations, evaluation evidence, and a cost/rollout plan. Benchmark internals and API parameters are not decision-relevant to executives, and promising perfection misrepresents residual risk.
referenceText: Communicate outcomes, risks, evidence, and rollout plans to stakeholders.

stem: After launch, usage patterns and source content continue to evolve. Which lifecycle practice best sustains quality over time?
options: Continuously monitor outputs and cost, re-run evaluations on every prompt or model change, and iterate with versioned prompts and content
Lock the prompt after launch so behavior stays reproducible and can never regress
Rely on the passing launch test suite indefinitely, since production inputs rarely change materially
Remove monitoring once the system has produced stable output for a full release cycle
rationale: Ongoing monitoring, regression evals on changes, and versioned iteration keep a deployed system healthy as inputs drift. Freezing the prompt, trusting one-time launch tests, or removing monitoring all let quality decay silently as content and usage change.
referenceText: Sustain quality with monitoring, regression evals, and versioned iteration.

stem: An organization has many teams independently adopting Claude and wants to raise baseline quality while reducing duplicated effort. Which enablement step is most effective?
options: Provide shared, reusable assets such as prompt libraries, evaluation suites, MCP servers, and CLAUDE.md conventions, backed by enablement documentation
Have each team independently develop its own prompts and tooling so solutions fit its exact context
Centralize all Claude development in a single specialist team to enforce consistency across the organization
Defer building shared evaluation suites until teams have shipped, to preserve early development velocity
rationale: Operational enablement scales through shared, documented, reusable assets that cut duplication and lift the baseline. Full decentralization reinvents work and diverges quality, funneling everything through one team creates a bottleneck, and deferring evals ships unmeasured quality.
referenceText: Scale enablement with shared reusable assets and documented conventions.

stem: A design must degrade gracefully when the model API or a downstream tool is temporarily unavailable. Which architectural practice best supports this?
options: Wrap model and tool calls with timeouts, bounded retries using exponential backoff, fallbacks, and structured error handling
Retry every failed call immediately and indefinitely until it eventually succeeds
Remove error handling so failures surface immediately and are easier to trace
Assume calls succeed and let the caller's outer process deal with any exceptions
rationale: Resilient designs use timeouts, bounded backoff retries, fallbacks, and structured error handling. Unbounded immediate retries amplify outages, removing error handling creates fragility, and assuming success guarantees cascading failures under partial outages.
referenceText: Handle transient failures with timeouts, bounded retries, and fallbacks.

stem: An integration must let Claude call several internal systems as tools. Which approach keeps the integration standardized and maintainable as systems are added?
options: Expose each internal system through an MCP server with well-scoped tools and resources the agent can reuse
Write bespoke integration glue for each system, optimizing every connection independently
Embed each system's API credentials directly in the system prompt so the model can call them
Fine-tune a separate model per internal system so each one learns that system's interface
rationale: MCP provides a standardized way to expose internal systems as reusable, well-scoped tools and resources, improving maintainability as systems grow. Bespoke per-system glue does not scale, credentials in the prompt are a security anti-pattern, and per-system fine-tuning is costly and unnecessary for tool access.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol standardizes reusable tool and resource integration.

stem: A user-facing feature has unacceptably high latency. Which optimization is most likely to reduce latency without lowering answer quality?
options: Cache stable prompt prefixes and right-size the model to the task, confirming quality holds with an evaluation
Raise the temperature so the model commits to an answer with less deliberation
Strip the system instructions to shorten the prompt the model has to process
Route every request to the largest available model to reduce reasoning retries
rationale: Caching stable prefixes and choosing an appropriately sized model, verified by an eval, cut latency while holding quality. Temperature governs randomness, not speed; removing instructions degrades quality; and always using the largest model typically increases latency.
referenceText: Reduce latency with prompt caching and right-sized model selection, verified by evals.

stem: A customer-support assistant prepends the same 30,000-token knowledge base and policy document to every request; the prefix is identical across thousands of daily conversations. The architect enables prompt caching. Which cost impact should they communicate to stakeholders?
options: Cache-write tokens cost more than standard input tokens (about 1.25x for the 5-minute TTL), while subsequent cache-read tokens are billed at a small fraction (about 0.1x) of standard input pricing
Cached input tokens are billed identically to uncached input tokens, but request latency drops to zero
After the first request the cached prefix incurs no input-token charge at all, so only output tokens are billed
Caching raises output-token cost by 25% in exchange for storing the prefix indefinitely at no additional charge
rationale: Prompt caching charges a premium to write the cache (roughly 1.25x base input for the default 5-minute TTL) and then bills cache reads at roughly 0.1x base input, which is what drives the savings on a large stable prefix. It never makes cached tokens free (they are read at a reduced rate, not zero), latency is reduced but not to zero, and caching does not increase output-token pricing or store data indefinitely (the default TTL refreshes on use).
referenceText: Prompt caching pricing: cache writes are billed above base input token rates and cache reads at a reduced fraction.

stem: A data team must classify 2 million archived documents with Claude. The job is not time-sensitive and can finish overnight. Which approach best optimizes cost while respecting platform constraints?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly 50% lower cost than standard requests
Send all 2 million requests synchronously in a tight loop and rely on automatic retries to absorb rate-limit errors
Use the standard Messages API with the largest model to finish as fast as possible, since batching costs more per token
Distribute the workload across many API keys to bypass per-account rate limits and lower cost
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large, non-urgent workloads: asynchronous processing within a 24-hour window at about half the per-token cost. A synchronous tight loop wastes cost and hammers rate limits; forcing the largest model synchronously ignores the batch discount; distributing across keys to evade rate limits violates usage policies and does not reduce cost.
referenceText: Message Batches API processes large asynchronous jobs within 24 hours at a 50% discount.

stem: While designing a RAG pipeline, a developer asks which Anthropic endpoint generates the vector embeddings for the document index. What is the most accurate guidance?
options: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embeddings model; the documentation recommends a third-party provider such as Voyage AI to create the vectors
The Messages API returns an embedding vector in its response metadata when embedding_mode is set to true
Claude generates embeddings automatically whenever documents are passed in the request's documents field
The Token Counting endpoint returns embeddings alongside the token count for any submitted text
rationale: Anthropic explicitly states it does not provide its own embedding model and points customers to embedding vendors such as Voyage AI. The Messages API has no embedding_mode, passing documents (for example for citations) does not return vectors, and token counting only returns token counts.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model and recommends providers like Voyage AI.

stem: A RAG system frequently retrieves chunks that cut sentences in half and lose surrounding context, hurting answer quality. Which chunking change most directly addresses this without simply maximizing chunk size?
options: Introduce overlap between adjacent chunks and align boundaries to semantic units such as paragraphs or sentences
Reduce every chunk to a single token so retrieval granularity is maximized
Store the entire corpus as one chunk so context is never split
Disable retrieval entirely and rely solely on the model's parametric knowledge
rationale: Overlapping chunks and boundaries aligned to natural semantic units preserve context across chunk edges without ballooning chunk size. Single-token chunks destroy meaning, one giant chunk defeats retrieval precision and blows the context budget, and disabling retrieval abandons the grounding the system needs.
referenceText: Structuring context by semantic units improves how models use retrieved content.

stem: A regulated-industry client requires that every factual claim in the assistant's answer be traceable to a specific passage in the source documents it was given. Which Claude capability most directly supports this requirement?
options: Prompt caching, which stores the source documents so they can later be audited for provenance
The Citations feature, which returns references to the exact source passages that support each part of the response
Extended thinking, which surfaces the model's reasoning trace as the origin of each stated claim
The Token Counting endpoint, which maps output tokens back to the corresponding input passages
rationale: The Citations feature grounds responses in the supplied documents and returns structured references to the specific supporting passages, giving verifiable provenance. Prompt caching only reduces the cost/latency of resending documents; extended thinking exposes reasoning, not source attribution; token counting does not map claims to sources.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude ground its answers in provided documents and reference the exact supporting passages.

stem: Before building an evaluation suite for a new summarization feature, a team wants to follow Anthropic's recommended first step. What should they do first?
options: Draft and freeze the production prompt so the evaluation targets the final wording
Define specific, measurable success criteria—such as target accuracy, latency, and cost thresholds—that the system must meet
Select the most capable available model so the feature clears evaluation by default
Release to a limited production cohort and let observed user complaints establish the quality bar
rationale: Anthropic's guidance is to establish clear, measurable success criteria before designing evals, so the tests measure what actually matters. Freezing the prompt, picking the biggest model, or shipping first all place implementation ahead of defining what 'good' means.
referenceText: Establish measurable success criteria before designing evaluations.

stem: An architect proposes an LLM-as-judge (model-graded) evaluation for open-ended responses. Which two practices most improve the reliability of this approach? (Select 2.)
options: Provide the judge model with a detailed rubric and concrete, criterion-level grading guidance
Have the judge emit a structured verdict—such as a numeric score or pass/fail label—that can be aggregated across the test set
Evaluate against a single fixed example on every run so scores stay directly comparable over time
Withhold any reference or expected answer so the judge cannot be anchored by it
Weight the grade toward response length, treating more thorough answers as higher quality
rationale: Model-graded evals are far more reliable when the judge is given an explicit rubric and asked to emit a structured, aggregatable verdict. A single example gives no coverage, withholding a reference answer removes useful grounding rather than reducing bias, and length is not a valid quality proxy.
referenceText: Model-graded evaluations should use clear rubrics and structured, machine-readable outputs.

stem: When contacting Anthropic support about a single failed Claude request and correlating it with internal logs, which value is most useful to capture per request in the observability pipeline?
options: The client host's local wall-clock timestamp captured when the call was issued
The unique request ID returned in the API response headers
The retry count recorded by the CDN edge node that fronted the request
The stop_reason value from the response body
rationale: Each API response carries a unique request ID that Anthropic support and your own logs use to identify a specific call, so logging it is the key to traceability. A local timestamp is ambiguous across concurrent requests, CDN retry counts are not diagnostic for a specific call, and stop_reason only explains why generation ended.
referenceText: API responses include a request-id used to identify and troubleshoot individual requests.

stem: To make a Claude API integration resilient, which two HTTP status codes should trigger an automatic retry with exponential backoff rather than an immediate hard failure? (Select 2.)
options: 400 (invalid request / malformed input)
429 (rate limit exceeded)
401 (authentication error)
529 (API temporarily overloaded)
403 (permission denied)
rationale: 429 (rate limited) and 529 (overloaded) are transient conditions that typically succeed on a later attempt, so backoff-and-retry is appropriate. 400, 401, and 403 are deterministic client errors that will fail identically on retry and should surface immediately.
referenceText: 429 and 529 are transient errors suited to retry with backoff; 4xx auth/validation errors are not.

stem: A feature generates long-form reports that can take well over a minute to produce, and non-streaming requests occasionally hit network timeouts. Which design change best mitigates this while keeping the interaction responsive?
options: Raise max_tokens so the full report is produced in fewer, larger chunks
Enable streaming so output tokens are delivered incrementally, keeping the connection active and reducing timeout risk
Disable automatic retries so a stalled request fails fast instead of waiting
Route every long request to a smaller-context model to shorten generation time
rationale: Streaming returns partial output as it is generated, keeping the connection active so long generations do not trip idle/network timeouts. Raising max_tokens can lengthen generation, disabling retries removes resilience, and downgrading the model sacrifices quality without addressing the timeout mechanism.
referenceText: Use streaming for long-running requests to avoid timeouts on non-streaming calls.

stem: An architect wants the assistant to stay available when a specific Claude model in one region is temporarily overloaded and returns HTTP 529. Which resilience pattern best addresses this?
options: Serve the most recent cached answer for the duration of any outage, regardless of the new question
Extend the request timeout to several hours so the overloaded call eventually completes
Retry with exponential backoff and, if overload persists, fail over to an alternate region or a comparable model
Remove the surrounding error handling so overload conditions surface directly to end users
rationale: 529 signals transient overload, so bounded retry-with-backoff combined with failover to another region or comparable model preserves availability. Returning a stale answer to a different question is wrong, multi-hour timeouts degrade UX and tie up resources, and removing error handling makes the system less resilient.
referenceText: Handle 529 overloaded responses with retries and fallback routing.

stem: A healthcare app must send clinical notes to Claude but wants to limit exposure of directly identifying data. Applying the principle of data minimization, which design choice is most appropriate?
options: Send the complete unredacted record so the model has maximum clinical context for accuracy
Place the direct identifiers in the system prompt, treating it as a private channel the model will not expose
Redact or tokenize direct identifiers—names, MRNs, SSNs—before transmission, sending only the minimum data the task requires
Retain the full raw request and response payloads indefinitely to support future debugging
rationale: Data minimization means sending only the data required and stripping or tokenizing direct identifiers before transmission. Sending full records maximizes exposure, the system prompt is not a privacy boundary, and indefinite raw-payload logging contradicts minimization and retention limits.
referenceText: Minimize and de-identify data sent to and retained by the system.

stem: A covered entity plans to process protected health information (PHI) through the Claude API. What is the appropriate first governance step with Anthropic?
options: Rely on the standard commercial terms, which extend HIPAA coverage to PHI on every paid account
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and configure the account to the agreed data-handling terms before transmitting any PHI
Route PHI exclusively through the developer console UI, which sits outside HIPAA's scope
Encrypt the API key at rest, which by itself satisfies HIPAA's requirements for processing PHI
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement between the covered entity and Anthropic, plus corresponding account configuration, before PHI is processed. Default commercial terms do not cover PHI, the console UI is not exempt from HIPAA, and encrypting a key does not satisfy the BAA or data-handling requirements.
referenceText: Processing PHI requires a BAA and appropriate data-handling configuration with Anthropic.

stem: An EU customer subject to GDPR asks how to reduce retention of personal data submitted to the Claude API and support data-subject deletion requests. Which two architectural measures align best? (Select 2.)
options: Pseudonymize and minimize personal data before submission, keeping the re-identification mapping under the customer's sole control
Persist every prompt and completion permanently to preserve an immutable record for all data subjects
Evaluate zero-data-retention / no-training handling options and configure retention to the minimum the use case requires
Treat GDPR as inapplicable on the basis that the API is operated from the United States
Turn off all logging, including the security audit logs required for compliance
rationale: Pseudonymizing before submission (with the customer holding the mapping) and configuring minimal or zero data retention both reduce personal-data exposure and ease deletion. Permanent storage conflicts with data-subject rights, GDPR applies based on the data subjects rather than server location, and disabling required audit logs undermines compliance.
referenceText: Reduce personal-data retention and pseudonymize inputs to support GDPR obligations.

stem: An enterprise has standardized on AWS with IAM, VPC networking, and centralized billing, and wants to use Claude without managing a separate Anthropic account. Which deployment option fits best?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, reusing existing AWS IAM, VPC networking, and consolidated billing
Adopt the first-party Anthropic API, since Bedrock is limited to prototyping and cannot serve production traffic
Deploy the Claude model weights onto customer-managed EC2 instances inside the existing VPC
Configure Bedrock as a pass-through proxy that forwards requests to the Anthropic API using the customer's Anthropic key
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets the enterprise consume Claude through AWS-native IAM, VPC, and billing, matching their standardization. Bedrock is production-grade, Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting on EC2, and Bedrock is a first-class provider rather than a proxy to the Anthropic API.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock provides access via AWS IAM, networking, and billing.

stem: A team running Claude on Google Vertex AI gets model-not-found (404) errors when calling a model in a particular region. What is the most likely architectural cause?
options: Vertex AI does not support Claude models, so the workload must move to a different provider
The requested Claude model is not enabled in that Vertex AI region, so requests must target a region where it is offered
Claude on Vertex AI additionally requires an Anthropic API key alongside the Google Cloud credentials
The model has been globally deprecated and can no longer be invoked from any region
rationale: On Vertex AI, Claude models are offered in specific regions; calling a region where the model is not available yields model-not-found. Vertex AI does support Claude, it authenticates with Google Cloud credentials (not an Anthropic key), and a region-scoped 404 points to regional availability rather than global deprecation.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is region-specific; requests must target a region where the model is available.

stem: A solution uses the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to connect Claude to internal systems. Which description of MCP's architecture is accurate?
options: MCP is a proprietary Anthropic binary format that only Claude models are able to parse
MCP is a client-server protocol in which host applications run MCP clients that connect to MCP servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts
MCP moves application logic into the model weights so that tool calls execute inside the model itself
MCP is a database schema-migration standard unrelated to model tool integration
rationale: MCP defines an open client-server architecture: a host runs MCP clients that connect to MCP servers, which expose tools, resources, and prompts. It is an open standard (not an Anthropic-only binary format), does not run logic inside model weights, and is unrelated to database migrations.
referenceText: MCP uses a client-server architecture where servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to host applications.

stem: A pipeline runs a high-volume, latency-sensitive intent-classification step before a complex multi-step reasoning step. Applying model right-sizing to optimize cost and latency, how should the architect assign models?
options: Assign the largest available model to every step so the pipeline stays uniform and simple to operate
Assign the smallest available model to every step to minimize cost regardless of task difficulty
Use a smaller, faster model such as Claude Haiku for the high-volume classification step and reserve a larger model such as Sonnet or Opus for the complex reasoning step
Rotate models randomly across requests to distribute load evenly over the available tiers
rationale: Right-sizing matches model capability to task difficulty: a fast, inexpensive model handles the simple high-volume step while a more capable model handles the hard reasoning step, optimizing cost and latency together. Using the largest model everywhere wastes cost/latency, the smallest everywhere sacrifices quality on the hard step, and random assignment yields unpredictable quality.
referenceText: Select the smallest model that meets each task's requirements and reserve larger models for harder tasks.

stem: Anthropic announces a retirement date for a model version the client's production system depends on. Which lifecycle and stakeholder-communication action is most appropriate?
options: Wait until the model is fully retired and requests begin failing, then migrate reactively
Proactively migrate to a supported successor, validate it against the existing eval suite before the retirement date, and communicate the timeline to stakeholders
Pin the deprecated model version indefinitely on the assumption it will continue to serve traffic
Swap in the successor model in production without re-running the evaluation suite so the change ships sooner
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means migrating to a supported model ahead of the retirement date, validating it against your evals, and keeping stakeholders informed. Waiting for failures, pinning a retired model, or swapping without re-evaluating all risk outages or silent quality regressions.
referenceText: Plan and test migrations to successor models before deprecated models are retired.

stem: A compliance team requires an audit trail of who invoked the AI assistant, when, and what category of action was taken. Where should this audit logging be implemented in the architecture?
options: Inside the model weights, which retain a per-user record of every invocation automatically
In the application/orchestration layer the customer controls, recording actor, timestamp, request metadata, and outcome for each invocation
Nowhere additional, since the response's stop_reason field already serves as the audit trail
In the end user's browser local storage, close to where the action originates
rationale: Audit logging is a customer responsibility implemented in the application/orchestration layer, where actor identity, timestamps, request metadata, and outcomes are known and controllable. Model weights do not store audit logs, stop_reason only explains why generation ended, and browser local storage is neither authoritative nor tamper-resistant.
referenceText: Audit logging of invocations is implemented in the customer-controlled application layer.

stem: A team builds RAG over long technical manuals. Retrieval uses fixed 256-token chunks, and Claude's answers frequently omit steps because multi-step procedures and tables are split across chunk boundaries. Which change most directly improves answer completeness?
options: Increase the embedding model's output dimensionality to capture more meaning in each vector
Replace dense retrieval with keyword-only BM25 search to guarantee exact term matches
Adopt larger, structure-aware chunks with overlap that keep procedures, tables, and section boundaries intact
Stop chunking entirely and embed each full manual as a single vector
rationale: Structure-aware chunking with overlap keeps semantically coupled content (procedures, tables) together so retrieved passages are self-contained, directly fixing the split-context problem. Higher dimensionality does not stop content from being split, BM25 alone loses semantic recall and does not preserve structure, and embedding whole manuals produces one coarse vector that destroys retrieval precision.
referenceText: Preserve document structure and use meaningful, overlapping chunk boundaries so related content stays together for retrieval.

stem: An architect needs verifiable, fine-grained source attributions in a RAG assistant so each claim links back to the exact supporting text in the provided documents. What is the most robust way to implement this on the Claude API?
options: Instruct Claude in the system prompt to append a bracketed source number after each sentence
Lower temperature to 0 so the model does not fabricate any citations
Pass the sources as document content blocks and enable the Citations feature so Claude returns grounded citation objects
Have Claude echo the full source text alongside each answer for manual verification
rationale: The Citations feature lets you supply documents as content blocks and returns structured citation objects pointing to the exact supporting spans, which is auditable and resistant to fabrication. Prompt-only citation formatting is unverified and easily hallucinated, temperature 0 reduces randomness but does not produce grounded references, and echoing full source text is wasteful and still not a verified mapping.
referenceText: Citations returns fine-grained references to the source documents you provide rather than relying on the model to format citations itself.

stem: During RAG design a team asks which Anthropic endpoint to call to generate the vector embeddings for their document index. What is the correct architectural guidance?
options: Call the Messages API with an embeddings flag set so it returns vectors instead of text
The Claude API has no first-party embeddings endpoint; use a recommended third-party provider such as Voyage AI
Rely on embeddings emitted automatically as a side output of every completion request
Use the token-counting endpoint, whose token IDs double as semantic embeddings
rationale: Anthropic does not ship its own embeddings model and recommends third-party providers such as Voyage AI. The Messages API returns text/tool outputs rather than embeddings, completions do not emit embeddings as a side effect, and token IDs from token counting are not semantic embeddings.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model; the embeddings guide recommends third-party providers such as Voyage AI.

stem: A chat product resends a 40,000-token policy handbook and tool schema unchanged on every turn, driving high input-token cost and latency. Which content is the best candidate for prompt caching, and why?
options: The user's latest message, since it is the portion that changes on every turn
The large, stable handbook and tool definitions placed ahead of the variable conversation, because cached prefixes are reused across requests
The model's streamed output tokens, since output typically dominates total cost
The entire request each turn, because caching requires the full prompt to be resent as one cache unit
rationale: Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix across requests, so the large unchanging handbook and tool schema placed before variable content yield the biggest savings. The user's per-turn message changes and cannot be reused, output tokens are not cacheable input, and caching benefits a stable prefix even when the suffix differs—so it does not require an identical full request.
referenceText: Prompt caching stores and reuses stable prefix content across requests; put large, static context up front and mark cache breakpoints.

stem: An engineer wants to maximize prompt-cache hit rate across many similar requests. How should the prompt be structured?
options: Interleave dynamic user variables throughout the static instructions so the model sees them in context
Order content from most static to most dynamic, placing cache breakpoints after the stable prefix and keeping variable content last
Put the changing user question first so it is prioritized by the cache
Randomize block order per request to avoid stale cache entries
rationale: Caching keys on an identical prefix, so putting static content first with a breakpoint after it and variable content last maximizes reuse. Interleaving dynamic values breaks the shared prefix. Leading with the changing question prevents any stable prefix from forming. Randomizing order defeats caching entirely.
referenceText: Structure prompts with static content first and dynamic content last so the cacheable prefix is stable across requests.

stem: A nightly job scores 2 million archived reviews for sentiment. Results are not needed until the next morning, and cost is the primary concern. Which approach best fits the requirements?
options: Fan out 2 million individual synchronous Messages requests behind a high concurrency limit
Submit the work through the Message Batches API to process asynchronously at a reduced per-token cost within the batch window
Stream each request to reduce time-to-first-token across the job
Increase max_tokens so more reviews fit in a single request
rationale: The Message Batches API is built for large, latency-tolerant workloads, processing asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly half the cost, which matches an overnight, cost-sensitive job. Millions of synchronous calls are costlier and rate-limit constrained. Streaming optimizes perceived latency, irrelevant here. max_tokens governs output length, not batching multiple reviews.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a discount, ideal for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: A pipeline classifies millions of short support tickets into 12 categories; a smaller model already meets accuracy for the bulk, but a minority of tickets need nuanced multi-step reasoning. Which two choices best balance cost and quality? (Select 2.)
options: Route all traffic to the most capable model to guarantee uniform quality
Use a smaller, faster model (e.g., Haiku) for the high-volume, well-handled classifications
Escalate only ambiguous or low-confidence tickets to a more capable model (e.g., Sonnet or Opus)
Disable streaming everywhere to reduce token usage
Always request the maximum max_tokens to avoid any truncation
rationale: Right-sizing means serving the bulk with a cheaper model and escalating only the hard cases to a stronger model, capturing most of the savings while protecting quality where it matters. Sending everything to the top model wastes cost. Streaming does not change token counts. Maxing out max_tokens raises cost and latency without improving classification.
referenceText: Choosing a model: match model capability to task difficulty and use tiered routing to balance cost, speed, and quality.

stem: A production client intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses. What is the correct resilience pattern for these transient errors?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when present
Treat both as permanent failures and surface the error to the end user immediately
Lower the request's max_tokens and resend without any delay
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; exponential backoff with jitter (and respecting retry-after) spreads load and recovers gracefully. Tight-loop retries amplify congestion and worsen the problem. These are retryable, not permanent. Reducing max_tokens does not address rate limiting or overload and immediate resends still hammer the service.
referenceText: Errors: 429 and 529 are transient; clients should retry with exponential backoff and respect retry-after guidance.

stem: You are hardening a customer-facing Claude application against transient capacity errors without degrading user experience. Which two design measures are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Retry immediately and indefinitely with no delay until a response arrives
Apply exponential backoff with jitter and cap the number of retries
Provide a fallback path such as a secondary region, service tier, or model when retries are exhausted
Return the raw upstream error to every user and cache it for five minutes
Set the client timeout to infinite so no request is ever cancelled
rationale: Bounded backoff with jitter plus a fallback path (secondary region/tier/model) preserves availability and UX during transient overload. Infinite immediate retries cause retry storms. Broadcasting and caching errors degrades everyone. Infinite timeouts let requests hang, exhausting resources instead of failing fast to a fallback.
referenceText: Service tiers and capacity management support fallback strategies for handling load and transient errors gracefully.

stem: An architect is explaining the Model Context Protocol (MCP) topology to stakeholders. Which description correctly characterizes MCP's architecture?
options: A single monolithic server that embeds the model, the application, and all data sources in one process
A client-server model where a host application runs MCP clients that connect over a transport to MCP servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts
A peer-to-peer mesh in which each model instance directly queries every other model's memory
A REST-only specification in which the model polls a central registry for all available context
rationale: MCP defines a host application containing MCP clients that connect via a transport to MCP servers, which expose capabilities like tools, resources, and prompts. It is not a monolith embedding the model, not a model-to-model mesh, and not a single REST registry the model polls; it is transport-agnostic (e.g., stdio, HTTP).
referenceText: MCP architecture: hosts run clients that connect to servers over a transport; servers expose tools, resources, and prompts.

stem: In an MCP server design, the team must decide whether to expose a capability as a tool or as a resource. Which distinction should guide the decision?
options: Tools and resources are interchangeable; choose whichever name reads better
Tools are model-invoked actions that can have side effects, while resources are application/user-controlled context the host exposes to the model
Resources are the only way to let the model take actions, and tools are read-only
Both tools and resources are invoked automatically by the model with no host or user mediation
rationale: MCP tools are model-controlled, action-oriented capabilities (often with side effects) the model can invoke, while resources are application-controlled data/context exposed to the model. They are not interchangeable, the action/read-only mapping in option three is reversed, and resources are host/user-mediated rather than auto-invoked by the model.
referenceText: Resources are application-controlled context exposed to the model, distinct from tools, which are model-controlled actions.

stem: An enterprise standardized on AWS wants to run Claude while keeping inference within their AWS security and networking boundary and reusing existing IAM controls. Which deployment is the best fit?
options: Call the first-party Anthropic API directly from AWS Lambda using an Anthropic API key
Use Claude on Amazon Bedrock, invoking models via Bedrock APIs governed by AWS IAM within the account's region
Proxy all requests through a self-hosted copy of the model weights on EC2
Route traffic through Google Vertex AI to reach Claude from AWS
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock runs within the customer's AWS account and region, using IAM for auth and AWS-native controls, satisfying the boundary and governance requirements. The first-party API sits outside AWS's IAM/networking boundary. Anthropic does not distribute self-hostable weights. Vertex AI is Google Cloud, not AWS.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets you invoke Claude through Bedrock using AWS IAM and regional controls within your AWS account.

stem: A Google Cloud customer must call Claude while enforcing data residency in a specific European region and using their existing GCP identity controls. Which detail is essential to the design?
options: Vertex AI has a single global endpoint, so region cannot be controlled
Claude on Vertex AI is invoked through regional endpoints authenticated with GCP IAM, letting you pin requests to a chosen region
You must export an Anthropic API key into GCP because Vertex does not manage Claude auth
Vertex AI requires downloading Claude weights into the project bucket before use
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses regional endpoints and GCP IAM, so requests can be pinned to a specific region for residency and governed by native identity controls. Vertex is regional, not single-global. Auth is via GCP IAM, not an Anthropic key. No weight download is involved.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is accessed via regional endpoints and authenticated through Google Cloud IAM.

stem: Before building a summarization feature, an architect wants a defensible way to know whether a candidate prompt/model is good enough for release. What should the evaluation design include first?
options: A single hand-picked prompt that looks impressive in a demo
A representative, versioned test set with explicit success criteria and graders (code-based, LLM-based, or human) run consistently across changes
Only production A/B traffic, with no offline test set
A rule to ship whenever the model's output subjectively feels acceptable to the developer
rationale: A representative, versioned eval set tied to explicit success criteria and consistent graders enables objective, repeatable release decisions and regression detection. A single demo prompt is not representative. Relying solely on live A/B lacks a pre-release gate and controlled comparison. Subjective feel is not measurable or reproducible.
referenceText: Develop tests: build representative eval sets with clear success criteria and graders to measure quality across changes.

stem: To support production observability and efficient Anthropic support escalations, which per-request metadata is most important to capture in application logs?
options: Only the final rendered answer shown to the user
The response request_id together with token usage, latency, model, and stop_reason
A locally generated correlation UUID by itself, without the response request_id or token usage
Nothing, since Anthropic retains full request logs on the customer's behalf
rationale: Logging the response request_id plus usage, latency, model, and stop_reason gives traceability, cost/performance monitoring, and a handle Anthropic support can use to investigate specific calls. Storing only the answer loses diagnostic context. A local UUID alone cannot be correlated by support without the response request_id. Customers should not assume Anthropic retains their request logs for them.
referenceText: API responses include a request_id used to identify and troubleshoot individual requests with support.

stem: An EU-based application processes sensitive customer data through Claude and must uphold GDPR data-minimization and residency expectations. Which two design measures directly support this? (Select 2.)
options: Send each customer's complete record on every request so the model always has full context
Redact or omit personal data that is not required for the task before sending it to the model
Deploy through a regional endpoint (e.g., Bedrock or Vertex in an EU region) to keep processing in-region
Persist all raw prompts and completions indefinitely for future analytics
Embed customers' national ID numbers into the system prompt to personalize responses
rationale: Minimizing the personal data sent (redaction/omission) and pinning processing to an EU regional endpoint align with data-minimization and residency principles. Sending full records and embedding national IDs maximize rather than minimize exposure. Indefinite retention of raw data conflicts with storage-limitation and minimization goals.
referenceText: Regional endpoints on Vertex AI let you control where requests are processed, supporting data residency requirements.

stem: A US healthcare provider intends to process protected health information (PHI) with Claude. Which architectural prerequisite must be satisfied for a compliant design?
options: Any Anthropic API key automatically covers HIPAA, so no separate agreement is needed
PHI processing requires a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and a HIPAA-eligible deployment such as Claude on Amazon Bedrock under the AWS BAA
Enabling zero-data-retention on the first-party API is sufficient to process PHI without any signed agreement
HIPAA eligibility is satisfied as long as all traffic is encrypted with TLS 1.2 or higher
rationale: Handling PHI requires a signed BAA and a HIPAA-eligible deployment configuration (e.g., Claude on Amazon Bedrock covered under the AWS BAA). A default API key does not confer HIPAA coverage. Zero-data-retention and TLS encryption are useful controls but neither substitutes for the required BAA and eligible environment.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock can be used within AWS's HIPAA-eligible services under a Business Associate Agreement.

stem: A US federal agency requires a FedRAMP-authorized environment for a Claude workload. Which deployment approach is the appropriate architectural choice?
options: Call the public first-party Anthropic API and self-attest FedRAMP compliance
Deploy Claude on Amazon Bedrock within AWS GovCloud / FedRAMP-authorized boundaries that meet the required authorization level
Run the workload from any commercial cloud region, since FedRAMP applies only to storage
Skip FedRAMP because encryption in transit is sufficient for federal use
rationale: Meeting FedRAMP means running within an authorized boundary, and Claude on Amazon Bedrock in AWS GovCloud/FedRAMP-authorized environments provides that inheritance. Self-attesting on the public API does not confer authorization. FedRAMP governs the whole service, not just storage, and TLS alone does not satisfy it.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is available within AWS environments that carry FedRAMP authorizations, including GovCloud.

stem: A regulated enterprise running Claude on Amazon Bedrock must demonstrate an auditable trail of model usage. Which two measures provide traceability and audit evidence? (Select 2.)
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail to record Bedrock API invocations
Persist the request_id returned with each response alongside application audit logs
Disable all logging to reduce storage cost and simplify compliance
Log only a random one-percent sample of invocations to limit audit storage
Store only the final user-visible answer with no request metadata
rationale: CloudTrail captures Bedrock invocation events and persisting the request_id with app logs links each interaction to a traceable identifier, together forming an audit trail. Disabling logging destroys evidence. Sampling only one percent leaves most invocations unauditable. Answer-only storage omits the metadata auditors need.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock integrates with AWS logging services such as CloudTrail for auditing model invocations.

stem: An architect is setting expectations with business stakeholders for the lifecycle of a Claude-powered feature. Which practice best supports maintainability and clear communication over time?
options: Define success criteria and an evaluation suite up front, version prompts and models, and monitor quality against those criteria after launch
Freeze the initial prompt permanently and avoid re-evaluating once the feature ships
Rely on stakeholder anecdotes as the sole signal of ongoing quality
Upgrade to every new model the day it releases without re-running evaluations
rationale: Defining measurable success criteria and evals, versioning prompts/models, and monitoring against those criteria gives stakeholders a shared, objective view across the lifecycle and catches regression or drift. Freezing prompts ignores changing data and requirements. Anecdotes are not reliable metrics. Blind model upgrades without re-evaluation risk silent regressions.
referenceText: Define success: establish measurable success criteria and evaluations to guide development and ongoing monitoring.

stem: You are architecting a document-analysis service that sends the same 40-page policy manual plus a large tool-definition block to Claude on every request, followed by a short, per-user question. You enable prompt caching to cut cost and latency. How should the request be structured so the cache is actually reused across users?
options: Place the stable manual and tool definitions at the very beginning of the request and set the cache_control breakpoint on the last stable block, keeping the variable user question after it
Put the per-user question first with its own cache_control breakpoint so each user's turn is cached independently
Interleave the manual content with each user question so every block gets its own cache entry
Set cache_control on every content block in the request to maximize the number of cache hits
rationale: Prompt caching matches on the longest common prefix, so all static, reusable content (system prompt, tool definitions, the manual) must come first, with the cache breakpoint marking the end of the stable prefix; the variable user question goes after it and is never cached. Putting the question first breaks the shared prefix so nothing is reused. Interleaving destroys the contiguous prefix. Caching every block does not help because the cache still keys on prefix position and adds needless write overhead.
referenceText: Anthropic — Prompt caching (cache breakpoints and prefix matching)

stem: A team needs to run Claude over 2 million historical support tickets to generate summaries for an internal knowledge base. The job has no real-time requirement and must minimize cost. Which approach best fits this workload?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs within 24 hours at a reduced token cost
Send the requests through the standard streaming Messages API to get partial results as fast as possible
Fan the requests out across many concurrent standard synchronous calls and rely on rate-limit retries
Use the Token Counting endpoint to process the tickets in a single aggregated request
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads: requests are processed asynchronously (target within 24 hours) at roughly half the cost of standard calls, which directly matches a bulk offline summarization job. Streaming optimizes for perceived latency, not cost or throughput. Blasting synchronous calls fights rate limits and costs full price. Token Counting only estimates token usage and does not generate summaries.
referenceText: Anthropic — Batch processing (Message Batches API)

stem: A regulated financial RAG application must show, for every claim in Claude's answer, exactly which passage of the retrieved source documents supports it, so compliance can audit responses. Which built-in capability best satisfies this requirement?
options: Pass the retrieved sources as document content blocks and enable the Citations feature so Claude returns claims linked to the specific supporting spans
Lower the temperature to 0 so the model deterministically repeats the source text verbatim
Add a system-prompt instruction telling Claude to 'always be accurate and cite sources' and parse the free text afterward
Increase the retrieval top-k so more documents are in context, guaranteeing traceability
rationale: The Citations feature lets you supply sources as document blocks and returns structured citations that tie each generated statement back to the exact supporting passages, which is precisely what auditable grounding requires. Temperature 0 improves determinism but provides no source linkage. A prose instruction yields unstructured, unreliable citations you must brittle-parse. Raising top-k adds context but produces no per-claim traceability.
referenceText: Anthropic — Citations

stem: A high-throughput pipeline must classify each inbound email into one of 12 routing categories. The classification is simple, but volume is millions per day and each call must return in a few hundred milliseconds under a tight per-token budget. Which model-selection strategy is most appropriate?
options: Right-size to the fastest, lowest-cost model (a Haiku-class model) since the task is well-scoped and latency- and cost-sensitive
Default to the most capable Opus-class model for every request to guarantee the highest possible accuracy
Route each email to Opus first and fall back to Haiku only when Opus times out
Randomly alternate between models on each request to balance cost and quality
rationale: Model right-sizing means matching model capability to task complexity: a narrow, high-volume classification with strict latency and cost targets is the canonical fit for the smallest, fastest Haiku-class model. Always using Opus wastes cost and latency on an easy task. Trying Opus first then falling back inverts the priority and adds latency on the common path. Random alternation makes cost and quality unpredictable and untestable.
referenceText: Anthropic — Models overview (choosing the right model)

stem: You are designing a Claude-based intake assistant for a US healthcare provider that will process protected health information (PHI). Which design decisions most directly support HIPAA-aligned handling of that data? (Select 2.)
options: Embed patient records into the model's system prompt permanently so future requests never need to resend PHI
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and use an offering configured for PHI, applying data-minimization so only the PHI necessary for the task is sent
Rely solely on TLS in transit and assume no further contractual or configuration controls are required
Scrub or avoid PHI in application logs, traces, and error messages, and enable audit logging of access to the system
Disable all retries and fallbacks so PHI is never transmitted more than once under any circumstance
rationale: HIPAA-aligned handling for a covered entity's vendor requires a BAA plus a configuration approved for PHI, combined with data minimization, and keeping PHI out of logs/traces while enabling audit logging (confidentiality + accountability). Models do not permanently 'learn' data placed in a system prompt, so that option is technically false and merely resends PHI. Disabling retries harms resilience with no privacy benefit. TLS alone ignores the required contractual and operational controls.
referenceText: Anthropic — Trust & compliance (HIPAA / BAA) and data handling

stem: An enterprise already runs its workloads in Google Cloud, requires that model inference stay within a specific region for data-residency reasons, and wants to inherit its existing cloud IAM and VPC controls rather than manage a separate API key. Which deployment choice best fits?
options: Call the first-party Anthropic API directly and add a client-side region check before each request
Deploy on Amazon Bedrock so the workload can stay inside the existing Google Cloud environment
Access Claude through Google Vertex AI, using the customer's GCP project, region, and IAM/VPC controls for governance
Self-host the Claude model weights inside the customer's GCP project to control the region
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI lets a GCP-native customer run inference within their chosen project and region and govern access through native GCP IAM and networking, satisfying data-residency and existing-control requirements. The first-party API does not inherit GCP IAM/VPC, and a client-side check does not enforce where inference runs. Bedrock is AWS, not Google Cloud. Claude weights are not distributed for customer self-hosting.
referenceText: Anthropic — Claude on Google Vertex AI

stem: Your production service occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses from the API during traffic spikes. Which client-side strategy is the most resilient way to handle these?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop with no delay until the request succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent errors and fail the request without any retry
Retry on a fixed short interval indefinitely with no backoff or attempt cap
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring any retry-after header, and cap attempts before surfacing a graceful failure
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; the resilient pattern is bounded exponential backoff with jitter, respecting retry-after to avoid thundering-herd retries, and degrading gracefully once attempts are exhausted. Tight-loop and fixed-interval-forever retries amplify the overload and never back off. Treating transient errors as permanent needlessly loses recoverable requests.
referenceText: Anthropic — Errors and rate limits (retry guidance)

stem: An architect wants Claude-based agents to reach internal systems (a ticketing database, a wiki, and a filesystem) through a consistent, reusable integration layer instead of bespoke glue code for each data source. Which statement best describes what the Model Context Protocol (MCP) provides for this design?
options: A proprietary Anthropic-only wire format that replaces the Messages API for all model calls
An open standard in which MCP servers expose tools and resources that any MCP-compatible client can consume, decoupling integrations from any single application
A model fine-tuning technique that bakes each internal system's data directly into the model weights
A managed vector database that automatically embeds and retrieves the internal systems' content
rationale: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context: servers expose tools, resources, and prompts, and any MCP-compatible client can connect, so integrations are written once and reused rather than rebuilt per application. It is not a replacement for the Messages API, not a fine-tuning method, and not a vector store.
referenceText: Anthropic — Model Context Protocol (MCP)

stem: You are designing the retrieval layer of a RAG system over a large corpus of long technical manuals. Which chunking-and-retrieval design best balances retrieval precision with preserving enough context for accurate answers?
options: Embed each entire manual as a single vector and always retrieve the whole document into the context window
Chunk strictly on a fixed character count with no overlap and no regard for sentence or section boundaries
Split documents into semantically coherent chunks with modest overlap, embed each chunk, retrieve the top-k most similar chunks for a query, and pass them to Claude with source metadata for citations
Skip embeddings entirely and rely on the model's training knowledge to answer corpus-specific questions
rationale: Effective RAG uses semantically aware chunks sized for the embedding model with some overlap so boundary context is preserved, retrieves top-k by similarity, and passes chunks with metadata for grounded, citable answers. Embedding whole manuals gives coarse vectors and blows the context budget. Fixed-length no-overlap splitting fractures meaning across boundaries. Skipping retrieval defeats RAG and invites hallucination on corpus-specific facts.
referenceText: Anthropic — Embeddings and retrieval-augmented generation

stem: Before promoting a Claude-powered feature to production, the team wants a repeatable way to measure quality and catch regressions when prompts or models change. Which evaluation approach is the soundest foundation?
options: Have engineers manually spot-check a few outputs each release and approve by consensus
Rely on production user complaints as the primary signal that quality has regressed
Treat a strong result in a single demo as evidence the prompt will generalize, re-testing only if customers escalate
Build a version-controlled, representative held-out test set with defined grading criteria, and automate scoring with code-based checks and/or an LLM-as-judge grader run on every change
rationale: A durable eval strategy uses a representative, versioned test set with explicit success criteria and automated grading (deterministic checks where possible, an LLM judge for open-ended outputs) so every change is measured consistently and regressions are caught before release. Ad-hoc spot checks are not reproducible, waiting for complaints or escalations is reactive, and one-demo generalization is unreliable.
referenceText: Anthropic — Create strong empirical evaluations / develop tests

stem: A model version your production application depends on has been announced for deprecation with a retirement date. As the architect, which actions best manage this lifecycle transition while keeping stakeholders informed? (Select 2.)
options: Ignore the notice until the deprecated model stops responding, then react to the outage
Run your existing evaluation suite against the newer target model to compare quality, latency, and cost, and adjust prompts before cutting over ahead of the retirement date
Pin the application permanently to the deprecated model ID so behavior never changes
Communicate the timeline, expected behavior changes, and rollout plan to affected stakeholders and schedule the migration with a rollback path
Immediately switch every workload to the newest model in production with no evaluation, since newer is always better
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means testing the replacement against your own evals to quantify quality/latency/cost deltas and re-tuning prompts before migrating ahead of the deadline, plus proactively communicating the timeline and impact to stakeholders with a scheduled, reversible rollout. Waiting for an outage guarantees disruption. A deprecated model ID will be retired, so pinning forever is not viable. Cutting over untested wrongly assumes behavior is identical.
referenceText: Anthropic — Model deprecations and lifecycle

stem: An architect designs a customer-support assistant that sends the same 40,000-token policy manual and system prompt on every request, followed by a short, varying user question. They enable prompt caching by placing a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of the policy manual. Which statement about how this caching behaves is correct?
options: Because the user question changes each turn, the cache never produces a hit, so the breakpoint provides no cost benefit.
Caching applies to the entire request including the varying user question, so the whole prompt must be identical for a hit.

The cached prefix is reused only when the tokens up to and including the breakpoint are byte-for-byte identical to a prior request; any change to the system prompt or manual invalidates the cache from the point of difference onward.
The cached content is stored permanently until manually deleted, so stale policy text keeps being served even after the manual is updated.
rationale: Prompt caching matches on an exact prefix up to and including the cache_control breakpoint; identical static content yields a cache read while the trailing variable question is processed normally, so placing the breakpoint after the stable content is correct. 'Never hits' is wrong because the varying question comes AFTER the breakpoint, so the stable prefix still hits. 'Entire request' is wrong because only the prefix must match. 'Permanent' is wrong: cache entries are ephemeral (default 5-minute TTL, refreshed on use), and updating the manual changes the prefix and creates a new entry.
referenceText: Prompt caching references cached prefixes; the cache is invalidated at the first token that differs. Cache entries have a default 5-minute lifetime, refreshed each time the cached content is used.

stem: A data team must classify 2 million archived documents overnight. The workload is not latency-sensitive and results can be collected the next morning. Which approach best optimizes cost while meeting the requirement?
options: Send all requests synchronously in a tight loop and rely on prompt caching to bring the price down by 50%.
Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs at roughly half the standard token cost and returns results within 24 hours.
Use the streaming Messages API with very high concurrency to finish faster and qualify for volume discounts.
Split the job across the Batches API and extended thinking so each document gets a larger reasoning budget for free.
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for high-volume, non-latency-sensitive workloads: asynchronous processing with target completion within 24 hours at a 50% discount on input and output tokens, matching this overnight, cost-sensitive use case. Prompt caching discounts reused prefixes, not distinct documents. Streaming does not grant volume discounts. Extended thinking increases token usage and cost rather than being free.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously, completing within 24 hours, at a 50% discount compared to standard API pricing.

stem: An architect is choosing a Claude model for a real-time chat routing step that does simple intent classification at very high volume, and separately for a low-volume legal-analysis feature requiring deep multi-step reasoning. What is the most appropriate right-sizing decision?
options: Use the Opus tier for both to guarantee maximum accuracy everywhere and simplify operations.
Use the Haiku tier for both, adding extended thinking so the small model matches frontier reasoning on the legal feature.
Use the Sonnet tier for classification and the Haiku tier for legal analysis to balance cost across the two.
Use a fast, lower-cost model (Haiku tier) for the high-volume classification and a frontier model (Opus tier) for the complex legal reasoning.
rationale: Model right-sizing matches capability to task: a fast, economical Haiku-tier model handles simple, high-throughput classification cheaply, while a frontier Opus-tier model is justified for low-volume, high-stakes reasoning. Opus for both over-provisions simple tasks (cost/latency). Haiku for both wrongly assumes extended thinking makes a small model equivalent to a frontier model on genuinely hard reasoning. The Sonnet/Haiku split inverts the mapping, giving the harder task the weakest model.
referenceText: Anthropic offers a family of models balancing intelligence, speed, and cost; select the smallest, fastest model that meets your quality bar and reserve the most capable models for the hardest tasks.

stem: A team is designing the chunking strategy for a RAG pipeline over long technical manuals. Which practices improve retrieval quality? (Select 2.)
options: Use the largest possible fixed-character chunks with no overlap to minimize the total number of embeddings stored.
Preserve semantic boundaries (e.g., split on sections/paragraphs) and add modest overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not severed mid-idea.
Strip all headings and structure before embedding so chunks contain only raw prose.
Attach metadata such as source, section heading, and document title to each chunk so retrieved passages can be filtered and cited.
Embed each full document as a single vector to guarantee complete context is always retrieved.
rationale: Semantically aware chunking with light overlap keeps related ideas intact, and per-chunk metadata enables filtering, ranking, and grounded citations. Huge fixed chunks with no overlap dilute relevance and sever context. Stripping headings discards structural signal that aids retrieval and citation. One vector per whole document destroys retrieval granularity, returning far more irrelevant text than needed.
referenceText: Effective retrieval chunks content along semantic boundaries with some overlap and enriches chunks with metadata, improving relevance and the ability to produce grounded, citable answers.

stem: During a design review, a stakeholder asks which Anthropic-native model call generates the vector embeddings for the RAG system. What is the accurate response?
options: The Messages API returns an embeddings array alongside every completion, so no separate service is required.
Claude's tokenizer endpoint doubles as the embedding model when called with an embed=true flag.

Anthropic does not offer a first-party embedding model; the documentation recommends using a third-party embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) to create vectors, then passing retrieved text to Claude.
Extended thinking mode outputs the document embeddings as part of its thinking blocks.
rationale: Anthropic does not provide its own embedding model; its guidance points customers to embedding providers such as Voyage AI to generate vectors for retrieval, after which retrieved passages are supplied to Claude for generation. The other options describe capabilities that do not exist: the Messages API returns text/tool output (not embeddings), there is no embed flag on token counting, and extended thinking emits reasoning text, not vectors.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model. Anthropic recommends embedding providers such as Voyage AI for generating text embeddings used in retrieval workflows.

stem: A compliance-sensitive knowledge assistant must show exactly which source sentences support each claim in Claude's answer, with verifiable references rather than free-text 'Source: ...' strings that the model might fabricate. Which capability best meets this requirement?
options: Instruct Claude in the system prompt to append footnote numbers, trusting the model to keep them accurate.
Provide the source documents and enable the Citations feature, which returns cited passages tied to specific locations in the supplied documents.
Lower the temperature to 0 so that any citations the model writes become guaranteed factual.
Enable prompt caching on the documents so the model memorizes and reliably reproduces their source identifiers.
