rationale: The Citations feature lets you pass documents and have Claude return structured citations that reference exact locations in those source documents, giving verifiable grounding rather than model-invented references. Prompting alone can hallucinate footnotes. Temperature 0 confuses determinism with factual accuracy and does not prevent fabricated citations. Caching reduces cost/latency and has nothing to do with citation correctness.
referenceText: The Citations feature enables Claude to provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages it uses from source documents, improving verifiability and reducing hallucinated sourcing.

stem: An architect is building an evaluation harness before shipping a prompt change to production. Which principles reflect strong empirical evaluation practice? (Select 3.)
options: Define task-specific success criteria and grade outputs against them (using code-based checks or an LLM-as-judge) rather than eyeballing a few samples.
Build a representative test set that includes edge cases and hard examples, not only easy happy-path inputs.
Rely on a single manual spot-check of the newest example, since if the latest output looks good the change is safe to ship.
Run the eval automatically in CI so prompt or model changes are measured against the same benchmark over time.
Tune the prompt directly on the held-out test set until the score is maximized, then report that score as the expected production accuracy.
rationale: Robust evaluation defines measurable success criteria, grades many cases automatically (code or LLM-judge), uses a representative set that stresses edge cases, and runs repeatedly (e.g., in CI) to catch regressions. A single spot-check gives no statistical confidence and misses regressions. Tuning on the held-out test set is overfitting, which inflates the reported metric and destroys its value as an unbiased estimate of production performance.
referenceText: Create strong empirical evaluations: define clear success criteria, develop test cases that cover edge cases, grade outputs consistently with code or model-based grading, and run evaluations systematically to detect regressions.

stem: A team enables extended thinking to improve accuracy on multi-step math and planning tasks. Which configuration statement is correct?
options: Extended thinking is free of token charges because thinking blocks are internal and never billed.
budget_tokens can exceed max_tokens because thinking happens outside the output budget entirely.
The thinking budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens, and a larger budget generally allows deeper internal reasoning at higher latency and token cost.
Extended thinking must be enabled on every request or tool use will not function.
rationale: With extended thinking you set a budget_tokens value that must be smaller than max_tokens; increasing it permits deeper reasoning but raises latency and cost because thinking tokens are billed as output. Thinking tokens are billed, so 'free' is wrong. The budget must be within max_tokens, so 'can exceed' is wrong. Extended thinking is optional and unrelated to whether tool use functions.
referenceText: When using extended thinking, you set a token budget for reasoning; the thinking budget must be less than max_tokens, and thinking tokens count toward billing and increase latency.

stem: An architect wants Claude to reliably distinguish instructions, a lengthy reference document, and the end-user's question within a single prompt so the model does not confuse them. Which prompt-engineering technique does Anthropic specifically recommend for this?
options: Rely on the model to infer the boundaries from natural-language transitions between the sections.
Wrap each distinct part in descriptive XML tags (e.g., <instructions>, <document>, <question>) to give the prompt clear, unambiguous structure.
Separate the sections with rows of dashes and uppercase labels acting as visual delimiters.
Place each section in a separate user message across multiple turns so they remain distinct.
rationale: Anthropic recommends XML tags to delineate parts of a prompt (instructions, context, examples, input), which reduces ambiguity and lets you reference sections precisely. Relying on natural-language transitions removes the explicit structure the model needs. Dashes and uppercase labels are weaker, less machine-parseable delimiters. Splitting sections across turns fragments a single task's context rather than structuring one prompt.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure your prompts; tags like <instructions>, <example>, and <document> help Claude clearly separate different parts of the input and reduce misinterpretation.

stem: To force Claude to return only a JSON object with no preamble like 'Here is the JSON:', which technique is most direct and reliable?
options: Append 'Do not include any preamble' to the system prompt and rely on instruction-following alone.
Lower the temperature to 0 so the model deterministically omits conversational text.
Prefill the assistant turn with the opening '{' so the model continues the structured output from that point.
Provide several few-shot examples but let the model open its response however it chooses.
rationale: Prefilling the assistant response with the initial token(s) of the desired format (e.g., an opening brace) constrains Claude to continue in that structure, eliminating preamble deterministically. An instruction alone is less reliable than prefill. Temperature 0 controls determinism, not whether conversational text appears. Few-shot examples help but, without prefill, still let the model add a preamble.
referenceText: Prefilling Claude's response by providing the beginning of the assistant turn steers output format and can eliminate unwanted preamble, forcing the model to continue from your provided prefix.

stem: A long-context prompt places a 150,000-token document together with a specific question about it. To maximize answer quality on Claude, how should the content be ordered?
options: Put the question first and the document last so the model reads the question before the context.
Put the long document(s) near the top of the prompt and place the query and instructions after the document content.
Interleave the question every few thousand tokens throughout the document to keep it salient.
Split the document across multiple system prompts so each stays under 8,000 tokens.
rationale: For long-context tasks, Anthropic's guidance is to place lengthy documents and data near the top of the prompt, above the query and instructions, which improves response quality. Putting the question first inverts the recommended order. Interleaving the question clutters the context and wastes tokens. There is no requirement to fragment documents into multiple small system prompts.
referenceText: For long context prompts, place longform data and documents near the top of the prompt, above the query and instructions, to improve the quality of Claude's responses.

stem: A production client calls the Claude API and must handle transient failures resiliently. Which HTTP responses should trigger an automatic retry with exponential backoff? (Select 2.)
options: 400 Invalid Request (malformed parameters in the request body).
429 Too Many Requests (rate limit reached).
401 Authentication Error (missing or invalid API key).
529 Overloaded (the API is temporarily overloaded).
403 Permission Error (the key lacks access to the resource).
rationale: 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) are transient conditions that typically resolve on retry, so retrying with exponential backoff and jitter is appropriate. 400, 401, and 403 are deterministic client-side errors: the request is malformed, unauthenticated, or unauthorized, and retrying without changing the request or credentials will fail identically, so retries only add load.
referenceText: 429 rate_limit_error and 529 overloaded_error are transient and should be retried with exponential backoff, whereas 400, 401, and 403 indicate client errors that will not succeed on retry without changes.

stem: An application generates long completions and occasionally hits client or gateway timeouts before the full response arrives. Beyond raising timeout limits, which design change most directly mitigates this for long-running generations?
options: Lower max_tokens so responses are shorter and less likely to time out, truncating output when necessary.
Split the generation into several smaller sequential requests and concatenate the partial outputs client-side.
Use streaming (Server-Sent Events) so tokens are delivered incrementally and the connection stays active as output is produced.
Move the call to a background worker with a longer socket timeout and poll periodically for completion.
rationale: Streaming delivers partial output as it is generated, keeping the connection alive and letting the client render progressively, which is Anthropic's recommended approach for long generations that risk timing out. Lowering max_tokens sacrifices completeness rather than fixing the transport. Splitting into sequential requests adds complexity and coordination overhead without addressing a single long response. A background worker with a longer timeout mitigates the symptom but does not keep the connection active the way streaming does.
referenceText: Use streaming with server-sent events to receive partial responses as they are generated; this is recommended for long-running requests to avoid network timeouts.

stem: A user-facing feature must stay available even when the primary Claude deployment returns overloaded (529) errors or times out. Which fallback design is the most resilient?
options: On repeated 529/timeout failures, trip a circuit breaker and fail over to an alternate model or cloud region (for example, a secondary Bedrock or Vertex region) while backing off the failing endpoint
Retry the same endpoint on a tight loop with no backoff until it eventually succeeds
Serve the most recent cached response from a different user's prior session so the caller never sees an error
Return a hard error to the user on the first failure to keep the fallback path simple
rationale: Resilient design pairs retries with exponential backoff, a circuit breaker to stop overwhelming a failing endpoint, and failover to an alternate model or region to preserve availability. Backoff-free retries amplify the outage. Serving another user's cached response is a correctness and privacy failure. Failing hard on the first error abandons the availability goal.
referenceText: Design resilient integrations with retries and exponential backoff, circuit breakers, and failover across regions or model deployments to maintain availability during transient outages.

stem: An EU-facing application sends user content to Claude and must align with the GDPR data-minimization principle. Which two measures most directly support that principle? (Select 2.)
options: Send only the fields the task requires and redact or tokenize personal identifiers the model does not need
Constrain retention so prompts and outputs are not stored longer than necessary, using zero- or limited-retention arrangements where available
Include the full customer record on every request so the model always has complete context
Persist all raw PII-bearing prompts in application logs indefinitely to aid future debugging
Move the API key into client-side code so requests reach the model with lower latency
rationale: Data minimization means processing and retaining only what the task requires: redacting or tokenizing unnecessary PII and limiting retention both reduce exposure. Sending full records regardless of need violates minimization; indefinite PII logging violates storage limitation; client-side API keys are a credential-exposure anti-pattern unrelated to minimization.
referenceText: Follow data minimization by sending only necessary data, redacting personal information where possible, and limiting retention; Anthropic offers data-retention controls to support privacy obligations.

stem: A US healthcare provider intends to process protected health information (PHI) through Claude in a HIPAA-aligned way. What is the foundational prerequisite before any PHI is sent?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and use a service configuration covered by that agreement
Set temperature to 0 and add a HIPAA notice to the system prompt so outputs are compliant
Rely on the standard commercial API terms, which already cover HIPAA obligations for all traffic
Base64-encode the PHI in each prompt to satisfy HIPAA de-identification requirements
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed BAA and use of a covered/eligible configuration before PHI is processed. Temperature and prompt notices are unrelated to the legal safeguard. Standard commercial terms are not automatically HIPAA-covered. Base64 is reversible encoding, not de-identification.
referenceText: To handle PHI, a covered entity must have a Business Associate Agreement in place and use HIPAA-eligible offerings covered under that agreement.

stem: A US federal agency must run Claude workloads inside a FedRAMP-authorized environment. Which deployment path should the architect evaluate?
options: Claude through a cloud environment carrying the required government authorization, such as a FedRAMP-authorized Bedrock configuration in AWS GovCloud
The public claude.ai consumer product, which is authorized at FedRAMP High for all tenants by default
The standard commercial API endpoint with a signed enterprise contract and IP allowlisting
A self-managed deployment of the Claude model weights on agency-controlled hardware
rationale: FedRAMP compliance comes from running within an authorized environment such as Bedrock in AWS GovCloud, not the commercial endpoint. The consumer product is not a FedRAMP authorization vehicle; a contract plus IP allowlist does not confer an authorization boundary; and Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting.
referenceText: Claude is available through cloud platforms such as Amazon Bedrock, including government-authorized environments like AWS GovCloud, enabling deployments that meet federal compliance requirements.

stem: A team running Claude on Amazon Bedrock wants requests routed automatically across multiple AWS regions for higher throughput and resilience. Which mechanism enables this, and how does it change the model identifier used for invocation?
options: A cross-region inference profile, invoked with a region-prefixed inference-profile ID (for example, a 'us.'-prefixed identifier) instead of a single-region foundation-model ID
A single-region foundation-model ID that Bedrock silently reroutes to other regions under load
Cross-region inference enabled by appending '?multiregion=true' to the invocation endpoint URL
A provisioned-throughput commitment, after which the standard model ID load-balances across regions
rationale: Bedrock cross-region inference profiles distribute traffic across regions and are invoked with an inference-profile ID, commonly carrying a geographic prefix such as 'us.'. A plain single-region model ID does not auto-route, the query-string flag is fabricated, and provisioned throughput governs capacity within a region rather than cross-region routing.
referenceText: On Amazon Bedrock, cross-region inference profiles route requests across regions to increase throughput and resilience, and are invoked using inference-profile IDs (often with a regional prefix) rather than single-region model IDs.

stem: An organization standardized on Google Cloud wants to call Claude from Vertex AI. Which statement accurately describes a prerequisite of that integration?
options: Enable the specific Claude model in Vertex AI Model Garden and call the regional Vertex endpoint using Google Cloud credentials (ADC or a service account) with a Vertex-format model identifier
Supply an Anthropic first-party API key in the x-api-key header on each Vertex request
Call the same global endpoint and model IDs as the Anthropic first-party API with no changes
Access Claude on Vertex AI only through scheduled nightly batch exports, since synchronous calls are unsupported
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI requires enabling the model in Model Garden and invoking a regional Vertex endpoint with Google Cloud IAM authentication and Vertex-style model IDs. Vertex uses Google Cloud auth rather than an Anthropic x-api-key, its endpoints and ID formats differ from the first-party API, and it serves synchronous requests.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is accessed by enabling the model in Model Garden and calling regional Vertex endpoints authenticated with Google Cloud credentials, using Vertex-specific model identifiers.

stem: An architect is briefing stakeholders on the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as a way to connect Claude to external systems. Which two statements about MCP are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: MCP is an open protocol with a client-server architecture: a client in the host application connects to MCP servers that expose capabilities such as tools and resources
Because MCP standardizes how context and tools are provided, a server built once can be reused across different MCP-compatible hosts
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic wire format that only Claude models are able to consume
MCP removes the need for authorization, since MCP servers are trusted and run with full system privileges by default
MCP servers can expose only static text resources and cannot define callable tools
rationale: MCP is an open standard with a client-server design where hosts embed clients that connect to servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts, and its standardization enables reuse across compatible hosts. It is open rather than Claude-exclusive, defines authorization rather than granting blanket privileges, and servers can expose callable tools in addition to resources.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context to LLMs, using a client-server architecture where clients connect to servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts.

stem: A support assistant sends the same large system prompt and a 40K-token policy document on every request, followed by a short, variable user question. The team wants to cut input cost and time-to-first-token. How should the request be structured to best leverage prompt caching?
options: Place the static system prompt and policy document at the start and set a cache_control breakpoint at the end of that static content, before the variable user message
Put the user question first, then the policy document, and set cache_control immediately after the question
Set temperature to 0 so identical outputs can be reused from cache across requests
Split the policy document into many separate messages so each paragraph is cached independently
rationale: Prompt caching keys on a stable prefix, so the reusable content must come first with a cache_control breakpoint marking the end of the cacheable prefix. Placing the variable question inside the prefix breaks reuse, temperature governs response randomness rather than caching, and fragmenting the document adds overhead without improving reuse.
referenceText: Place cache breakpoints at the end of static, reusable content that precedes variable content so the stable prefix can be reused across requests.

stem: A pipeline has three stages: (a) high-volume intent classification, (b) short FAQ answers, and (c) multi-step legal reasoning over long documents. The architect must control cost and latency without degrading the hardest task. Which model-routing strategy is best?
options: Route (a) and (b) to a fast, economical model such as Haiku and reserve a frontier model such as Opus for (c)
Use Opus for all three stages to keep the architecture uniform and easier to operate
Use Haiku for all three stages and accept reduced reasoning quality on (c)
Route by request size, sending the longest inputs to Haiku and the shortest to Opus
rationale: Right-sizing matches each task's difficulty to the smallest capable model, using economical models for simple high-volume work and a frontier model only where deep reasoning is required. Opus everywhere overpays and adds latency, Haiku everywhere sacrifices quality on the hardest stage, and routing purely by input length ignores task complexity.
referenceText: Choose the model that balances capability, speed, and cost for each task; use smaller models for simpler, high-throughput tasks and larger models for complex reasoning.

stem: A nightly job must score 500,000 archived support tickets with Claude. Results are not needed in real time, and reducing cost is the priority. Which approach fits best?
options: Submit the workload through the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing at a reduced price, with results returned within the batch window
Fan the tickets out as parallel synchronous Messages calls during peak hours to finish fastest
Enable response streaming, which lowers the per-token price on large jobs
Rely on prompt caching alone, which discounts output tokens for repeated jobs
rationale: The Message Batches API targets large, latency-tolerant workloads at a substantial discount with asynchronous delivery. Parallel synchronous calls cost more and risk rate limits, streaming changes delivery rather than price, and prompt caching discounts cached input tokens (not output tokens) and does not fit this pattern.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a reduced cost compared with standard synchronous requests.

stem: In a RAG system, retrieval frequently returns fragments that lack surrounding context, and answers miss key details. Which chunking change is most likely to improve retrieval quality?
options: Chunk on semantic or section boundaries at a moderate size with slight overlap, preserving context and attaching metadata for filtering
Chunk into fixed 5-token windows so retrieval is maximally granular
Store the entire corpus as a single chunk so all context is always retrieved together
Chunk at random character offsets to increase the diversity of retrieved passages
rationale: Effective retrieval uses semantically coherent, moderately sized chunks with modest overlap and useful metadata so each unit is self-contained and filterable. Tiny fixed windows destroy context, a single giant chunk defeats retrieval and blows the context budget, and random boundaries fragment meaning and hurt relevance.
referenceText: Split documents into coherent chunks with appropriate size and overlap and attach metadata so retrieval returns contextually complete passages.

stem: A team is building the retrieval layer for a Claude-based RAG application and needs to generate vector embeddings for their documents. What does Anthropic recommend?
options: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embedding model; use a dedicated third-party embeddings provider such as Voyage AI for the retrieval step
Call the Messages API and read the embedding vectors returned in the response's usage object
Use Claude's tokenizer token IDs directly as the embedding vectors
Fine-tune a Claude model to emit fixed-length embedding vectors for each document
rationale: Anthropic does not provide its own embedding model and points customers to third-party providers such as Voyage AI. The Messages API returns generated text and token usage rather than embeddings, token IDs are not semantic vectors, and Claude fine-tuning does not produce embeddings.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model; a third-party embeddings provider such as Voyage AI is recommended for generating embeddings.

stem: A RAG application must show, for each claim in an answer, exactly which source sentences support it, in a verifiable way that reduces unsupported statements. Which Claude capability best meets this requirement?
options: The Citations feature, which returns structured references to the provided source documents that ground each part of the response
Setting temperature to 0 so the model only emits statements it can support
Adding a 'cite your sources' instruction to the system prompt with no document structure
Using stop sequences to cut off any sentence that lacks a citation
rationale: The Citations feature returns structured, verifiable references to the supplied source documents, directly satisfying the traceability requirement. Temperature controls randomness rather than grounding, a bare prompt instruction is unreliable and unstructured, and stop sequences only truncate text and cannot detect missing support.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude provide detailed references to the source documents it used, grounding responses in provided material.

stem: A team is designing an offline evaluation suite for a Claude-based contract-analysis feature before production rollout. Which two practices most strengthen the evaluation? (Select 2.)
options: Build a held-out, representative test set with graded examples and define task-specific success metrics before running the model
Score outputs at scale with an LLM-as-judge or code-based grader aligned to the rubric
Evaluate only on a handful of hand-picked prompts already known to produce good outputs
Tune prompts against the same examples later used to report final accuracy
Defer measurement to occasional manual spot checks performed after launch
rationale: Strong evaluation defines success criteria up front, uses a representative held-out set, and scores at scale with graders aligned to the rubric. Cherry-picked prompts overstate quality, tuning and reporting on the same data leaks and inflates results, and post-launch spot checks provide no pre-release signal and do not scale.
referenceText: Define success criteria, build representative graded test cases, and use graders such as code-based checks or an LLM judge to evaluate at scale before deployment.

stem: An SRE team wants production observability for a Claude workload to track cost and detect quality regressions across prompt versions. Which logging approach is most directly actionable from the Messages API response?
options: Log usage.input_tokens and output_tokens (including cache read/creation tokens), stop_reason, latency, and request IDs per call, then aggregate by prompt version
Log only the final completion text and infer cost from character counts
Record client wall-clock time only and omit token accounting
Capture HTTP status codes alone, without correlating to model or prompt version
rationale: The Messages response includes a usage object with input/output and cache token counts plus stop_reason; logging these with latency, request IDs, and a prompt-version tag yields precise cost and regression signals. Character-count estimates are inaccurate for tokenized billing, wall-clock time alone omits cost and quality signals, and status codes without model/prompt correlation cannot localize regressions.
referenceText: Each Messages response includes a usage object reporting input and output token counts (and cache tokens) plus a stop_reason, enabling cost and behavior tracking.

stem: Users say a chat UI feels slow because they wait for the entire response before anything appears. Without changing the model, which change most improves perceived latency?
options: Stream the response so tokens appear incrementally as they are generated
Increase max_tokens so the model has more room to finish sooner
Raise temperature to speed up token sampling
Send each request twice and display whichever completes first
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens as they are produced, improving perceived latency and time-to-first-token without changing the model. Raising max_tokens can increase total generation time, temperature affects randomness rather than speed, and duplicate requests waste cost and quota while doing little for a single stream's responsiveness.
referenceText: Streaming responses lets users see output as it is generated, reducing perceived latency and improving time to first token.

stem: An EU-facing application sends customer records to Claude. To align with GDPR data minimization and privacy-by-design, which two practices are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Redact or omit personal-data fields that are not needed for the model's task before building the prompt
Establish a lawful basis and limit personal data in prompts to what is necessary for the stated purpose
Retain full raw prompts and completions indefinitely in logs for possible future tuning
Rely on the model provider to automatically anonymize any personal data in requests
Include every available field so the model always has maximum context
rationale: Data minimization means processing only the personal data necessary for a defined, lawful purpose, so redacting unneeded fields and constraining prompt contents are both correct. Indefinite retention contradicts storage limitation, providers do not automatically anonymize inputs (that is the controller's responsibility), and sending all fields regardless of need is the opposite of minimization.
referenceText: Send only the data necessary for the task; minimize and where possible redact personal data in prompts, consistent with data-usage and privacy obligations.

stem: A healthcare provider wants to process protected health information (PHI) with Claude. Which prerequisite is required to do this appropriately?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and use a HIPAA-eligible configuration before sending PHI
Encrypt the API key at rest, which by itself makes any endpoint HIPAA compliant
Send PHI freely because API traffic is already TLS-encrypted in transit
Rely on automatic de-identification performed by the model on every request
rationale: Processing PHI requires a signed BAA and use of a HIPAA-eligible configuration. Encrypting the API key does not establish HIPAA eligibility, TLS protects data in transit but does not satisfy HIPAA's contractual and safeguards requirements, and the model does not automatically de-identify inputs.
referenceText: Handling PHI requires a Business Associate Agreement and use of HIPAA-eligible configurations before such data is processed.

stem: A US federal agency must run its Claude workload inside a FedRAMP-authorized environment at the required impact level. Which deployment path is most appropriate?
options: Access Claude through a FedRAMP-authorized cloud, such as Claude on Amazon Bedrock in AWS GovCloud, that meets the agency's authorization level
Call the public commercial Anthropic API directly with an IP allowlist on the agency network
Self-host the Claude model weights on agency-owned hardware
Use any commercial region as long as traffic is encrypted in transit
rationale: FedRAMP requirements are met by consuming Claude through an authorized environment such as Bedrock in AWS GovCloud at the appropriate impact level. An IP allowlist on the commercial API does not confer authorization, Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting, and encryption in transit alone does not satisfy FedRAMP's authorization boundary.
referenceText: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock, including environments such as AWS GovCloud, enabling deployments that meet federal authorization requirements.

stem: An enterprise needs to attribute Claude API usage to individual teams and support security audits. Which practice best supports this governance goal?
options: Use separate workspaces and per-team API keys with usage and admin/audit logging to attribute and review activity
Share a single organization-wide root API key across all teams for simplicity
Embed the API key in client-side code so each end user authenticates individually
Rotate API keys only after a confirmed security breach
rationale: Separating usage into workspaces with per-team keys plus usage and admin/audit logs enables attribution, least privilege, and auditability. A shared root key destroys attribution and widens blast radius, client-side keys expose secrets to end users, and rotating keys only after a breach is reactive and violates key hygiene.
referenceText: Organizations can use workspaces and scoped API keys with administrative and usage logging to manage, attribute, and audit access.

stem: A team standardized on AWS wants to invoke Claude through Amazon Bedrock. Which statement most accurately describes how that integration authenticates and where inference runs?
options: You authenticate with AWS IAM credentials and invoke a Bedrock model ID; inference runs within your AWS account and selected region
You authenticate with an Anthropic Console API key while AWS IAM governs only network access to the endpoint
Prompt caching and other Claude API features are disabled when Claude is accessed through Bedrock
Bedrock proxies each request to Anthropic-operated inference servers and returns the result to your account
rationale: On Bedrock you use AWS IAM authentication and a Bedrock model identifier, with inference served inside your AWS account and chosen region under the AWS shared-responsibility model. No separate Anthropic API key is required. Feature support is available on Bedrock rather than disabled, and inference is served within AWS, not proxied to Anthropic.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is accessed using AWS credentials and Bedrock model IDs, with requests handled within the customer's AWS environment.

stem: An organization runs on Google Cloud and wants to adopt Claude while keeping its existing GCP IAM, billing, and regional controls. Which statement about Claude on Vertex AI is accurate?
options: You invoke Claude through Vertex AI using GCP project authentication and a supported region, so IAM and billing stay within Google Cloud
Billing for Vertex AI requests is settled through a separate Anthropic Console account rather than Google Cloud
Claude on Vertex AI supports only batch requests and cannot stream responses token by token
You must translate prompts into Gemini's request schema before Vertex AI will route them to Claude
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses standard GCP project authentication and supported regions, so IAM and billing remain within Google Cloud. Billing flows through GCP, not a separate Anthropic account; streaming is supported; and requests use Claude's Messages format on the Vertex endpoint, not Gemini's schema.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is accessed through Google Cloud project authentication in supported regions, using Google Cloud IAM and billing.

stem: A production Claude service must remain reliable when it intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses under load. Which two measures are appropriate design choices? (Select 2.)
options: Retry idempotent requests using exponential backoff with jitter on 429 and 529 responses
Provide a fallback path (such as a smaller model or a degraded response) and set bounded client timeouts
Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request eventually succeeds
Treat every 529 response as a permanent failure and surface an error without retrying
Configure client timeouts as unlimited so no in-flight request is ever cut off
rationale: Resilient design retries transient 429/529 errors with exponential backoff and jitter and adds graceful fallbacks plus bounded timeouts. Tight-loop retries amplify load and worsen overload; 529 is transient, so treating it as permanent needlessly fails recoverable requests; and unlimited timeouts hang clients and exhaust resources instead of failing fast.
referenceText: Handle 429 and 529 responses with exponential backoff and retries, and design timeouts and fallbacks for transient errors.

stem: Anthropic announces that a model your production system depends on is deprecated with a scheduled retirement date. As the architect, which lifecycle response should you communicate to stakeholders?
options: Migrate to the recommended successor before the retirement date, re-run the evaluation suite for quality, latency, and cost, and pin explicit model versions
Take no action, since deprecated models continue to serve traffic indefinitely after the announced date
Wait until requests begin failing in production, then swap models without re-running evaluations
Route all calls through a 'latest' alias so future model upgrades apply automatically without an evaluation gate
rationale: Sound lifecycle management migrates to the successor ahead of retirement, re-validates with evals across quality/latency/cost, and pins explicit versions for predictability. Deprecated models are retired on the published date, so inaction or waiting for failures causes outages, and silent 'latest' aliasing removes the evaluation gate and risks unreviewed behavior changes.
referenceText: Deprecated models are retired on announced dates; migrate to recommended replacements and validate before the retirement date, using pinned model versions.

stem: A team wants Claude-based agents to reach internal tools and data sources through a standard interface instead of building a bespoke integration for each system. What does the Model Context Protocol (MCP) provide?
options: An open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLM clients through MCP servers
A managed vector database service for storing and querying document embeddings
A replacement for the Messages API that lets clients call tools without sending a prompt
A proprietary Anthropic-only plugin format that cannot be self-hosted by third parties
rationale: MCP is an open standard defining how servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLM clients, replacing one-off integrations with a common protocol. It is not a vector database, does not replace the Messages API, and is open and self-hostable rather than proprietary or Anthropic-only.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard for connecting AI applications to external tools, data, and prompts through MCP servers.

stem: You must send a 150K-token prompt containing many reference documents plus a single question. Following Anthropic's long-context guidance, how should you structure the prompt to maximize answer quality?
options: Place the documents near the top, wrap each in XML tags with source metadata, and put the query at the end of the prompt
Put the query at the very top ahead of the documents and omit structural tags to save tokens
Repeat the question between every document so it remains salient throughout the context
Concatenate all documents into one unlabeled block and append the query with no delimiters
rationale: Anthropic's long-context guidance recommends placing large documents near the top, delimiting each with XML tags and metadata, and putting the query at the end, which improves grounding and recall. Leading with the query, dropping structure, or collapsing sources into an unlabeled blob removes the boundaries and metadata the model uses to locate and cite relevant content.
referenceText: For long context, place documents near the top of the prompt, structure them with XML tags and metadata, and put the query at the end.

stem: A customer-support assistant prepends a 30,000-token product knowledge base and detailed system instructions to every request, followed by a short user question. Traffic is interactive (sub-second responses expected) and the same static context is reused across many turns in a session. Which optimization best reduces both cost and time-to-first-token for the repeated static portion?
options: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static knowledge base and system instructions
Move all requests to the Message Batches API to obtain the batch token discount
Lower max_tokens on each request so the model processes fewer tokens overall
Fine-tune a Claude model on the knowledge base so the context no longer needs to be sent
rationale: Prompt caching stores the large static prefix so subsequent requests read it at a fraction of base input cost and skip re-processing, cutting both cost and time-to-first-token for interactive reuse. The Batches API is asynchronous and unsuitable for sub-second latency; lowering max_tokens affects only output tokens, not the 30K input; and fine-tuning does not eliminate the need to supply grounding context and is not the offered path here.
referenceText: Prompt caching – Anthropic docs

stem: An architect is modeling the cost impact of prompt caching before rollout. Which statement about the default 5-minute cache pricing is correct?
options: A cache write costs more than a base input token, while a cache read costs a fraction of a base input token
Both cache writes and cache reads are billed at the same rate as base input tokens
Cache writes are free, and only cache reads incur an added charge
Once a cache entry is created, tokens read from it are no longer billed at all
rationale: With the default 5-minute TTL, a cache write is billed at 1.25x the base input token price and a cache read (hit) at 0.1x, so caching pays off once the cached prefix is reused enough to amortize the write premium. Writes are not free, reads are not full price, and cache hits are still billed at the reduced read rate.
referenceText: Prompt caching – pricing

stem: A nightly pipeline must classify roughly 400,000 archived support tickets. No user is waiting on any individual result, and completion within 24 hours is acceptable. Which integration approach best fits the cost and throughput goals?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing at a discounted token rate
Issue synchronous Messages API calls in a tight loop with no concurrency limit to finish as fast as possible
Use the streaming Messages API so partial results arrive sooner for each ticket
Request a large rate-limit increase and run all 400,000 calls concurrently on the standard tier
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large asynchronous jobs with no interactive latency requirement, returning results within 24 hours at a 50% discount on input and output tokens. Tight synchronous loops and mass concurrency waste cost and invite rate limiting, and streaming only improves per-response latency, which is irrelevant when no user is waiting.
referenceText: Message Batches API

stem: A high-volume moderation service performs simple binary classification (allowed vs. flagged) on millions of short messages per day, where per-request cost and latency dominate the design. Which model-selection strategy reflects correct right-sizing?
options: Select the fastest, lowest-cost model in the Claude family and confirm it meets the accuracy bar against an eval set
Default to the most capable Opus-tier model to guarantee the highest possible accuracy on every message
Route requests randomly across all model tiers to average out cost and quality
Choose the model with the largest context window regardless of task complexity
rationale: Right-sizing matches model capability to task difficulty; a simple, high-volume classification task typically runs well on the fastest, cheapest tier, validated against an eval set. Defaulting to the largest model wastes cost and latency, random routing yields inconsistent quality, and context-window size is irrelevant for short-message classification.
referenceText: Choosing a model / models overview

stem: An architect is designing the chunking stage of a RAG pipeline over long policy documents. Which chunking approach best preserves retrieval quality while keeping chunks usable as grounding context?
options: Split on semantic and structural boundaries (sections, paragraphs) at a moderate size with some overlap between adjacent chunks
Split strictly every N characters with no overlap so every chunk is exactly the same length
Use one chunk per entire document so no relevant context can ever be excluded
Create single-sentence chunks so retrieval always returns the most granular unit
rationale: Chunking on semantic and structural boundaries with modest overlap keeps related ideas together and avoids severing context at chunk edges, improving retrieval relevance and grounding. Fixed character splits with no overlap cut sentences and lose context; whole-document chunks defeat retrieval precision and bloat context; single-sentence chunks fragment meaning and hurt recall.
referenceText: Retrieval augmented generation guidance

stem: While designing the retrieval layer for a RAG system on Claude, a team asks which embedding model to call from the Anthropic API to vectorize their corpus. What is the correct guidance?
options: Anthropic does not provide a first-party embeddings endpoint; use a recommended third-party provider such as Voyage AI
Call the Anthropic /v1/embeddings endpoint with a Claude model id to generate the vectors
Reuse the Messages API and treat the assistant's text output as the embedding vector
Skip embeddings entirely, since Claude performs managed vector search over uploaded files
rationale: Anthropic does not offer its own embeddings model; the documentation directs customers to third-party providers such as Voyage AI for vectorization. There is no Anthropic /v1/embeddings endpoint, chat text output is not an embedding, and Claude does not silently run managed vector search over your corpus.
referenceText: Embeddings – Anthropic docs

stem: A regulated knowledge assistant must show exactly which source passages support each claim in Claude's answer, using verifiable spans rather than model-invented references. Which native capability should the architect use?
options: The Citations feature, passing source material as document content blocks so Claude returns cited passages tied to its claims
A system-prompt instruction telling Claude to append a bibliography, relying on it to quote sources accurately
A post-processing regex that guesses which retrieved chunk each sentence most likely came from
Prompt caching on the source documents, which attaches source citations to responses automatically
rationale: The Citations feature accepts documents as structured content blocks and returns grounded citations (cited_text and source location) attached to the parts of the answer they support, reducing fabricated references. Prompting for a bibliography or regex-matching cannot guarantee verifiable spans, and prompt caching is a cost/latency feature unrelated to citations.
referenceText: Citations – Anthropic docs

stem: A team is standing up an evaluation practice before launching a Claude-powered feature. Which approach best reflects sound eval design for measuring task quality?
options: Build a representative, versioned test set with clear grading criteria and run it automatically on every prompt or model change
Have the prompt author perform ad-hoc manual spot checks whenever an output happens to look wrong
Track only average token latency, treating output quality as acceptable as long as responses are fast
Defer evaluation to production user complaints collected after the feature ships
rationale: Effective evaluation uses a representative, versioned test set with defined grading criteria run automatically, so regressions from prompt or model changes are caught before release. Ad-hoc spot checks are non-reproducible and biased, latency is not a quality measure, and waiting for production complaints is a reactive failure mode.
referenceText: Create strong empirical evaluations

stem: An SRE needs to give Anthropic support the precise identifier for a failed API call so it can be traced. Which artifact from each API response should the application capture in its structured logs for this purpose?
options: The request-id value returned in the HTTP response headers of each API call
The user's raw prompt text, stored verbatim so the exact call can be reconstructed
The wall-clock timestamp of the request, which uniquely identifies it to Anthropic
The max_tokens value sent on the request
rationale: Each API response includes a request-id header that uniquely identifies the call; logging it lets support and the team correlate and troubleshoot specific requests. Timestamps are not unique identifiers, max_tokens is a parameter rather than an identifier, and storing raw prompt text verbatim is a data-minimization concern, not a trace ID.
referenceText: Errors and request IDs

stem: Under GDPR, an EU-facing application built on Claude includes far more personal data in its prompts than the task requires. Which principle should most directly reshape what the integration sends to the model?
options: Data minimization: send only the personal data strictly necessary for the task and redact or omit the rest before the API call
Send all available data for maximum context, then delete it after the response returns
Encrypt the payload in transit, which on its own satisfies the obligation to limit data collection
Rely on Anthropic to automatically discard any unnecessary personal data it receives
rationale: Data minimization requires processing only the personal data necessary for the stated purpose, so the integration should redact or omit unneeded personal data before sending prompts. Sending everything violates the principle, transport encryption addresses security rather than minimization, and the controller (the application), not the provider, is responsible for limiting what is collected and sent.
referenceText: Anthropic privacy and data handling

stem: A healthcare provider wants to process protected health information (PHI) through Claude in a HIPAA-compliant manner. Which control is required before PHI may flow through the integration?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and configure the account for the required data-handling terms before sending PHI
Add a system-prompt instruction stating that the data is PHI and must be handled securely
Rely on the default consumer terms, since API traffic is already encrypted in transit
Base64-encode the PHI before including it in prompts to de-identify it
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed BAA with the vendor and the associated account data-handling configuration before PHI is transmitted; this is a contractual and account control, not a prompt instruction. A system-prompt note, default consumer terms, and base64 encoding (trivially reversible and not de-identification) do not satisfy HIPAA obligations.
referenceText: Anthropic HIPAA compliance / trust

stem: A US federal agency workload requires a FedRAMP-authorized path to run Claude. Which deployment option is the appropriate architecture to pursue?
options: Access Claude through a compliant cloud platform such as Amazon Bedrock in an environment carrying the required FedRAMP authorization
Call the standard public Anthropic API directly, since all Anthropic API traffic is inherently FedRAMP authorized
Self-host the Claude model weights inside the agency's own data center
Use the consumer Claude.ai web application with accounts on a government email domain
rationale: A FedRAMP-authorized path to Claude is achieved through compliant cloud platforms such as Claude on Amazon Bedrock in a suitably authorized environment. The public API is not automatically FedRAMP authorized, Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting, and the consumer web app is not a FedRAMP path.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock

stem: A team already running its stack on AWS wants to call Claude while keeping data within their AWS security and networking boundary and reusing existing IAM controls. Which integration is the natural fit?
options: Claude on Amazon Bedrock, authenticating with AWS IAM credentials and invoking Bedrock model identifiers
The direct Anthropic API with an Anthropic API key, routed outbound through a NAT gateway
Google Vertex AI, since it federates transparently with AWS IAM roles
A shared consumer Claude.ai subscription used across the engineering team
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets AWS customers invoke Claude via Bedrock model IDs using native AWS IAM authentication and keep traffic within their AWS boundary. The direct Anthropic API uses Anthropic keys rather than IAM, Vertex AI is Google Cloud's platform and does not federate AWS IAM, and the consumer app is not an enterprise API integration.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock

stem: An organization standardized on Google Cloud wants to call Claude with GCP-native authentication and regional model endpoints. Which detail correctly describes the Claude on Vertex AI integration?
options: You authenticate with Google Cloud credentials and target a specific GCP project and region when invoking Claude models on Vertex AI
You must supply an Anthropic-issued API key because Vertex AI cannot accept Google Cloud credentials
Vertex AI offers Claude only through a single global endpoint with no region selection
The workload must also hold active AWS Bedrock credentials to call Claude on Vertex AI
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication (application default credentials or service accounts) and requires specifying a GCP project and region for the model endpoint. It does not use Anthropic API keys, does support regional endpoints, and has no dependency on AWS Bedrock credentials.
referenceText: Claude on Google Vertex AI

stem: An architect is hardening a Claude client for transient failures. Which HTTP status conditions should trigger an automatic retry with exponential backoff and jitter? (Select 2.)
options: 429 Too Many Requests (rate limited)
529 (service temporarily overloaded)
400 Invalid Request (malformed parameters)
401 Authentication error (invalid API key)
403 Permission denied
rationale: 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) are transient conditions where backing off and retrying is appropriate, which is what the SDKs do automatically. 400, 401, and 403 are deterministic client-side errors (bad parameters, invalid credentials, lack of permission) that fail identically on retry and must be fixed rather than retried.
referenceText: Errors - HTTP status codes

stem: A latency-sensitive service calls Claude synchronously. The team wants to prevent a slow or stalled upstream call from cascading into user-facing hangs. Which resilience pattern best addresses this?
options: Set a bounded client request timeout and define a fallback (e.g., degraded response or smaller model) when the timeout or repeated failures occur
Remove all client timeouts so no in-flight request is ever cancelled prematurely
Retry the same request in an unbounded loop until it eventually succeeds
Increase max_tokens so the model has more room and is less likely to fail
rationale: Bounded timeouts plus a defined fallback path contain latency and keep the system responsive when the upstream call is slow or failing. Removing timeouts lets requests hang indefinitely, unbounded retries amplify load and can worsen an outage, and raising max_tokens increases (not decreases) potential latency and is unrelated to fault containment.
referenceText: Handling errors and timeouts

stem: Anthropic has announced a deprecation date for the model version a production application pins. Which lifecycle-management action best protects continuity while communicating responsibly with stakeholders?
options: Test the recommended successor model against the existing eval suite, plan a migration before the retirement date, and communicate the timeline to stakeholders
Wait until the model returns errors in production, then swap the model id under incident response
Pin to the deprecated model indefinitely and assume Anthropic will keep serving it
Silently switch to the newest model in production without re-running evaluations
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means validating the successor model against your evals, scheduling migration ahead of the retirement date, and communicating the plan to stakeholders. Waiting for production errors is a preventable incident, indefinite pinning ignores the announced retirement, and switching models without re-evaluating risks silent quality regressions.
referenceText: Model deprecations

stem: An architect is explaining the Model Context Protocol (MCP) roles to a team. Which description correctly maps the MCP architecture?
options: A host application embeds MCP clients, each of which maintains a connection to an MCP server that exposes capabilities like tools and resources
An MCP server embeds the LLM and calls out to client applications for tool definitions
MCP replaces the Messages API by streaming model weights directly to connected clients
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic-only wire format that cannot be implemented by third parties
rationale: In MCP, a host application runs one or more clients, and each client connects to an MCP server that exposes capabilities (tools, resources, prompts). The server does not embed the LLM, MCP is a context/capability protocol rather than a replacement for the Messages API, and it is an open standard implementable by anyone.
referenceText: MCP architecture

stem: A team is building an MCP server to expose their internal systems to Claude. Which primitives can an MCP server expose to clients? (Select 3.)
options: Tools (model-invokable actions/functions)
Resources (contextual data the client can read)
Prompts (reusable prompt templates the server offers)
Model weights the client downloads and runs locally
The client's billing credentials for the underlying LLM provider
rationale: MCP servers expose tools (callable actions), resources (readable contextual data), and prompts (reusable templates) to connected clients. Servers do not distribute model weights, and they never hand out the client's billing credentials - MCP is about exposing capabilities and context, not model hosting or credential sharing.
referenceText: MCP core concepts (tools, resources, prompts)

stem: A real-time application needs more consistent, predictable latency for its Claude traffic and is willing to commit to capacity for it. Which platform capability directly targets this latency-consistency goal?
options: Use a priority/service tier that offers more consistent latency for committed traffic
Switch every request to the asynchronous Message Batches API
Set temperature to 0 so responses are generated faster
Disable streaming so the full response is computed in a single burst
rationale: Service (priority) tiers are designed to provide more consistent, predictable latency for committed traffic, directly addressing the requirement. The Batches API is asynchronous and higher-latency, temperature affects sampling not speed, and disabling streaming does not reduce total generation time (it delays perceived responsiveness).
referenceText: Service tiers

stem: A production support assistant sends an identical 30 KB block of tool definitions and policy documents as the system prompt on every request, followed by short, varying user turns. Both per-request input cost and time-to-first-token are concerns. Which change most directly reduces both without altering the model's answers?
options: Add a cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static tools/policy content so it is written once and served from the prompt cache on later requests
Move the policy documents into every user message so the model re-reads them in fresh context each turn
Lower max_tokens so the request is billed for fewer input tokens
Split the system prompt into several smaller system blocks so the platform can process them in parallel
rationale: Prompt caching lets you mark a stable prefix with cache_control; subsequent requests that reuse that prefix read it from cache at roughly 10% of base input cost and skip re-processing, cutting both cost and latency. Moving docs into user turns defeats caching and increases tokens. max_tokens caps output, not input billing. Splitting system blocks does not create parallel processing and can break the cacheable prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching caches a prefix marked with cache_control; cache reads are billed at a fraction of base input tokens and reduce latency by skipping reprocessing of the cached content.

stem: An analytics team must classify roughly 80,000 archived documents overnight. There is no interactive user waiting, and cost is the primary constraint. Which approach best fits these requirements?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API and collect results asynchronously
Fan out synchronous Messages API calls at maximum concurrency until the rate limit is hit
Send every request on the Priority Tier to guarantee capacity
Stream each request and buffer the token deltas to reduce cost
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large, non-time-sensitive workloads: requests are processed asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at a 50% discount, which directly serves the cost goal. High-concurrency synchronous calls cost full price and fight rate limits. Priority Tier optimizes latency at higher cost, the opposite of the goal. Streaming affects delivery of tokens, not price.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a 50% cost discount, with results generally available within 24 hours.

stem: An engineer enables prompt caching but sees very low cache-hit rates. Investigation shows the prompt is assembled as: a per-user personalization line, then a large unchanging knowledge base, then the user question. What is the most likely fix?
options: Reorder the prompt so the large unchanging content comes first and the per-user, variable content comes after it
Increase max_tokens so the cache has room to store the response
Add a second cache_control breakpoint inside the user question
Disable caching for the knowledge base since it is too large to cache
rationale: Caching matches on an exact prefix. Placing a variable per-user line before the static knowledge base changes the prefix on every request, so nothing is reused. Moving stable content to the front and variable content to the back preserves a long cacheable prefix. max_tokens is unrelated to caching. Caching the question (which always changes) does not help, and large static content is exactly what caching is designed for.
referenceText: Cache hits require an identical prefix; place static content at the beginning of the prompt and variable content after the cache breakpoint.

stem: A legal-research application built on retrieval must show auditors exactly which sentence in a source document supports each statement Claude makes. Which native capability satisfies this most reliably?
options: Enable Citations on the document content blocks so Claude returns cited_text with precise source locations
Append an instruction asking Claude to 'list your sources at the end of the answer'
Request token logprobs and map high-probability spans back to documents
Post-process the answer with regular expressions to find quoted phrases
rationale: The Citations feature makes Claude ground claims in provided documents and return structured references (including cited_text and location) that map answer segments to source spans, which is exactly what auditors need. A free-text 'list sources' instruction is unverifiable and prone to fabrication. Logprobs do not identify source spans. Regex post-processing cannot establish which source actually supports a claim.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages in source documents that support its claims.

stem: A team is designing a RAG pipeline and asks which model to call to convert their document chunks into vector embeddings for their vector store. What is the correct guidance per Anthropic's documentation?
options: Anthropic does not provide a first-party embeddings endpoint; use a recommended third-party embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) for the vectors
Call the Messages API with an 'embedding' response format to return vectors instead of text
Use the token-counting endpoint, whose token IDs serve as embeddings
Enable the embeddings flag on a Claude model to have it return dense vectors
rationale: Anthropic does not ship its own embedding model; the documentation points customers to third-party embedding providers such as Voyage AI. The Messages API returns generated text, not embeddings, and has no 'embedding' response format. Token counts and token IDs are not semantic embeddings, and there is no embeddings flag on Claude models.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model; the docs recommend using a third-party embeddings provider such as Voyage AI.

stem: In a RAG system over long policy manuals, retrieval frequently returns fragments that begin mid-sentence and drop the section heading, so Claude loses context. Which chunking adjustment best addresses this?
options: Use structure-aware chunking that respects section/paragraph boundaries and adds overlap plus the parent heading to each chunk
Reduce every chunk to a single sentence so each vector is maximally specific
Remove all overlap between chunks to avoid duplicate content in the index
Increase the number of chunks returned (top-k) to a very large value regardless of the context window
rationale: Chunking on semantic/structural boundaries with modest overlap and retained headings preserves the surrounding context a passage needs to be interpreted correctly. One-sentence chunks strip context and fragment meaning. Removing overlap increases the chance of cutting off ideas at boundaries. Blindly inflating top-k adds noise, raises cost, and can overflow the context window without fixing the root cause.
referenceText: Effective chunking preserves semantic boundaries and context; overlap and surrounding structure help retrieved passages remain interpretable.

stem: A retrieval system returns semantically similar passages but misses queries that hinge on exact identifiers like error codes and part numbers. Which retrieval design change is most appropriate?
options: Combine dense (embedding) search with a lexical/keyword (e.g., BM25) index and merge the ranked results
Switch entirely to keyword search and remove the embedding index
Raise the vector-similarity threshold so only near-duplicate matches are returned
Re-embed the corpus with a larger dimension to capture exact strings
rationale: Hybrid retrieval pairs dense embeddings (semantic recall) with lexical search (exact-term precision), so identifiers and codes are matched by the keyword index while meaning-based queries still work. Dropping embeddings loses semantic recall. A stricter similarity threshold shrinks recall further. Larger embedding dimensions do not make dense vectors reliably match exact tokens.
referenceText: Hybrid search blends semantic embedding retrieval with keyword-based retrieval to capture both conceptual matches and exact-term matches.

stem: Before wiring generation into a RAG product, an architect wants to evaluate the retrieval stage in isolation using a labeled set of query-to-relevant-document pairs. Which metrics directly measure retrieval quality? (Select 2.)
options: Recall@k (fraction of relevant documents appearing in the top k results)
Precision@k (fraction of the top k results that are relevant)
Output tokens per second of the generation model
The dollar cost of the final answer generation call
The system prompt's cache-hit ratio
rationale: Recall@k and precision@k are the standard measures of whether retrieval surfaces the right documents in the top results, and they can be computed from labeled query/document pairs without invoking generation. Generation token throughput, generation cost, and cache-hit ratio are latency/cost/efficiency signals of other components and say nothing about retrieval relevance.
referenceText: Evaluate retrieval quality with relevance metrics such as precision@k and recall@k against a labeled evaluation set before assessing end-to-end generation.

stem: A workflow has two stages: (1) route each incoming ticket into one of eight categories, a simple, high-volume classification, and (2) draft a nuanced legal-risk memo from the routed ticket. The team wants to control cost while keeping quality high. What is the best model strategy?
options: Right-size per stage: use a faster, cheaper model (e.g., Haiku) for the classification and a more capable model (e.g., Opus) for the complex memo
Use the single most capable model for every stage to keep the architecture uniform
Use the cheapest model for every stage and add more prompt instructions to compensate on the memo
Alternate models randomly across stages to average out cost and quality
rationale: Model right-sizing matches each task to the smallest model that meets its quality bar: cheap/fast for routine classification, more capable for the reasoning-heavy memo. Using the top model everywhere overpays on the easy stage; using the cheapest everywhere underdelivers on the memo regardless of prompt length; random selection makes quality and cost unpredictable.
referenceText: Choose the model that best fits each task's complexity, latency, and cost needs; use faster models for simple tasks and more capable models for complex reasoning.

stem: An architect must set up automated regression testing so a prompt change cannot silently degrade quality before release. Which approach is the most robust and scalable?
options: Build a versioned evaluation set of representative inputs with graded criteria, and score outputs automatically (code-based checks plus an LLM-as-judge for open-ended cases) in CI
Have a reviewer read a handful of outputs by hand before each deploy and approve based on overall impression
Rely on user complaints in production as the signal that a prompt regressed
Assert that each response exactly equals a single golden string for every test case
rationale: A versioned eval set with explicit grading, combining deterministic checks and an LLM judge for subjective quality, run automatically in CI, catches regressions consistently and scales. Ad hoc manual spot checks are inconsistent and unscalable. Waiting for user complaints detects regressions only after customers are harmed. Exact-match assertions are brittle for generative outputs, which vary in wording while remaining correct.
referenceText: Create empirical, automated evaluations with representative test cases and grading (including model-based grading for open-ended outputs) to detect regressions.

stem: A platform team wants production observability so they can correlate a specific failed generation with Anthropic support and trace token usage per feature. Which practice best supports this?
options: Log the response's request-id along with model, stop_reason, and input/output token usage for every call
Log only the final user-facing text so storage stays small
Store the full plaintext prompt and response for every request indefinitely with no field structure
Disable logging in production to minimize latency overhead
rationale: Capturing the request-id lets you reference an exact call when contacting support, and recording model, stop_reason, and usage tokens enables per-feature cost tracing and debugging of truncation/refusals. Logging only the final text discards the diagnostic fields. Storing full plaintext forever with no structure is a governance and privacy liability and is not queryable. Disabling logging removes observability entirely.
referenceText: Each API response includes a unique request-id and usage token counts; capture these for debugging, support correlation, and cost tracking.

stem: A client integration must remain resilient when the API returns HTTP 429 (rate limit) or 529 (overloaded). Which behaviors constitute a correct retry strategy? (Select 2.)
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter rather than immediately re-sending at a fixed interval
Honor the retry-after header when the response provides one
Retry a 400 invalid_request_error the same way, since all errors are transient
Remove client-side timeouts so slow requests always have unlimited time to complete
Escalate every 429 immediately to a human operator instead of retrying
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; exponential backoff with jitter avoids synchronized retry storms, and honoring retry-after respects the server's guidance on when to try again. A 400 invalid_request_error is a client bug that will fail identically on retry. Removing timeouts lets a stuck request hang the caller indefinitely. Paging a human on every rate-limit event does not recover the request and does not scale.
referenceText: 429 and 529 are retryable; use exponential backoff and respect the retry-after header, while 4xx request errors should not be blindly retried.

stem: A latency-sensitive chat service occasionally sees a single upstream generation hang far longer than usual. The team wants to bound user-visible latency without dropping the feature entirely. Which design is most appropriate?
options: Set a request timeout and, on timeout, fall back to a faster/smaller model or a cached/canned response, surfacing a graceful degraded result
Remove the timeout and show a spinner until the request eventually completes
Retry the same request against the same model dozens of times with no delay
Fail the entire user session whenever any single call exceeds the timeout
rationale: A bounded timeout plus a fallback path (smaller model, cached answer, or graceful message) caps user-visible latency while preserving functionality. Removing the timeout lets one slow call hang the experience. Tight retry loops with no backoff amplify load and can trigger rate limiting. Killing the whole session on one slow call is a poor experience and unnecessary when a degraded fallback exists.
referenceText: Resilient designs set timeouts and provide fallbacks (such as a smaller model or cached response) so a single slow or failed call degrades gracefully.

stem: Users of a chat product complain that answers 'take forever to appear,' even though total generation time is acceptable. The team cannot change the model. Which change most improves perceived latency?
options: Stream the response so tokens render incrementally as they are generated
Increase max_tokens so the full answer is guaranteed to fit
Disable prompt caching so each answer is computed fresh
Batch several users' messages into one request to share overhead
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens as they are produced, so users see output begin almost immediately, which sharply improves perceived latency even when total time is unchanged. Raising max_tokens can lengthen responses and time-to-completion. Disabling caching increases latency. Batching unrelated user messages into one request is not how the Batches API works and would harm interactivity.
referenceText: Streaming returns partial output as it is generated, improving perceived responsiveness by reducing time-to-first-token for the user.

stem: An enterprise has standardized on AWS and needs to call Claude while keeping network traffic, IAM identity, and data governance inside its AWS account. Which integration path fits this requirement, and what changes for the developer?
options: Route the Anthropic API key through an AWS PrivateLink endpoint, which reclassifies the traffic as an AWS-native managed service
Adopt Claude on Amazon Bedrock, authenticating with AWS IAM credentials and invoking Bedrock model IDs in a chosen region instead of an Anthropic API key
Adopt Claude on Amazon Bedrock, but rewrite all prompts because Bedrock exposes materially different model behavior than the first-party API
Adopt Claude on Amazon Bedrock, accepting that it supports only single-turn synchronous calls without system prompts or tool use
rationale: On Bedrock, Claude is invoked through AWS: the developer authenticates with IAM/AWS credentials and references Bedrock model IDs in a region, keeping identity, billing, and governance inside AWS. Routing an Anthropic key through a private endpoint does not confer IAM-based authorization or make it AWS-native. The Messages-style request format is largely portable, so prompts do not need wholesale rewriting, and Bedrock fully supports system prompts and tool use.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock uses AWS authentication and Bedrock model identifiers within an AWS region, and supports the standard Messages capabilities.

stem: A team that has standardized on Google Cloud wants to run Claude within its existing GCP project. Which statement correctly describes using Claude on Google Vertex AI?
options: Authenticate with Google Cloud credentials and invoke Claude through Vertex AI in a supported region and project, rather than with an Anthropic API key
Download and deploy the Claude model weights into a Vertex AI custom container before serving requests
Expect model and region availability to be uniform, since Vertex AI exposes every Claude model in every region without configuration
Use Vertex AI's endpoint, which returns only embedding vectors for Claude and requires a separate service for text generation
rationale: On Vertex AI, Claude is a managed endpoint accessed with Google Cloud authentication against a supported GCP project and region, keeping identity and billing in GCP. Customers do not host or upload model weights. Model and region availability must be configured and is not universal. Vertex returns Claude chat/message completions, not just embeddings.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication and is available in specific regions/projects through Vertex's managed endpoints.

stem: An architect is briefing stakeholders on the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and must correctly characterize its component roles. Which description is accurate?
options: MCP is a proprietary Anthropic wire format that only Claude-family models are able to interpret
In MCP the server invokes the model directly, while the client's only responsibility is to persist returned results
MCP servers execute the model themselves, so a host application no longer needs its own orchestration code
An MCP host embeds an MCP client that connects over the protocol to one or more MCP servers, which expose tools, resources, and prompts to the model
rationale: MCP is an open client-server protocol: a host application embeds an MCP client that connects to MCP servers, which expose capabilities (tools, resources, prompts). It is an open standard, not Anthropic-proprietary. The host drives the model and orchestration; servers provide context and capabilities rather than executing the model, and application code still coordinates the flow.
referenceText: MCP follows a client-server architecture: a host with an MCP client connects to MCP servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts.

stem: A team building an MCP server must expose two capabilities: (a) a read-only 'account balance' lookup that the application supplies as context, and (b) a 'submit refund' action the model can decide to invoke. Which MCP primitives fit each capability? (Select 2.)
options: Expose the read-only account balance as a resource the host supplies as context
Expose 'submit refund' as a tool the model can choose to invoke
Expose the account balance as a tool the model is required to call at the start of every turn
Expose 'submit refund' as a resource that the host reads and applies automatically
Expose both capabilities as prompts the end user selects from a menu
rationale: In MCP, resources are application-controlled, typically read-only context supplied by the host, which fits the account-balance lookup; tools are model-invoked actions with side effects, which fits 'submit refund.' Forcing the balance to be a model-called tool every turn misuses the primitive, and modeling a state-changing refund as a passively read resource is wrong.
