Prompts are reusable templated interactions, not the right fit for either capability.
referenceText: MCP resources provide application-controlled, typically read-only context, while tools are model-controlled actions the model can invoke.

stem: A regulated company preparing a Claude deployment must align with GDPR data-minimization and sound compliance practice. Which two measures are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Redact or strip personal data not required for the task, transmitting only the minimum necessary in each prompt
Establish the appropriate data-handling terms for the regime in scope, such as a zero-data-retention arrangement or a BAA for PHI
Retain complete prompts and responses containing personal data indefinitely to preserve a full audit trail
Include each end user's full identifier set in the system prompt so the model always has complete context
Grant the logging store broad read access so investigators can retrieve records without approval delays
rationale: Data minimization means sending only the personal data a task requires (redacting the rest), and regulated workloads should be covered by the correct data-handling terms such as zero-data-retention or a BAA for PHI. Indefinite retention of full personal data conflicts with minimization and retention limits. Injecting full identifier sets into every prompt is the opposite of minimization, and removing access controls on logs is a serious security and compliance failure.
referenceText: Apply data minimization by sending only necessary data, and use appropriate data-handling terms (such as zero data retention or a BAA) for regulated workloads.

stem: A model version an application depends on has been placed on a deprecation schedule with a fixed retirement date. What is the most responsible lifecycle-management action for the architect?
options: Continue on the deprecated version and switch only after production calls begin returning retirement errors
Test the successor model against the application's evaluation suite, schedule migration ahead of the retirement date, and communicate the timeline and expected changes to stakeholders
Pin the application to the deprecated version indefinitely, since retirement dates are advisory and traffic will keep being served
Cut all production traffic to the successor immediately without evaluation, on the assumption that a newer model is strictly better
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means evaluating the successor against your own tests, scheduling migration ahead of the retirement date, and keeping stakeholders informed of timing and behavioral changes. Waiting for failures guarantees an outage, pinning to a retired model is impossible once it is shut down, and cutting over with no evaluation risks silent quality regressions because a newer model can behave differently for a given prompt.
referenceText: Deprecated models have retirement dates; migrate to recommended replacements ahead of retirement and validate behavior against your evaluations before cutover.

stem: A support assistant sends a 28,000-token knowledge base plus fixed policy instructions on every request, followed by a short, unique user question. The team wants to maximize prompt-cache hit rate and cost savings. How should the prompt be structured?
options: Place the unique user question at the very beginning so Claude processes it before the knowledge base
Set the cache_control breakpoint immediately after the user question so each turn's content is cached
Insert multiple cache_control breakpoints after the variable question so each segment is cached independently
Place the static knowledge base and instructions first with a cache_control breakpoint, then append the variable user question after the cached prefix
rationale: Prompt caching keys on an exact-match prefix, so the largest static content (knowledge base + instructions) must come first with the cache breakpoint, and only the variable question should follow. Putting the unique question first, or placing breakpoints after it, invalidates or misplaces the prefix so nothing reusable is cached, defeating the purpose since the question changes each turn.
referenceText: Cache the largest, most static portions of your prompt (system instructions, context, documents) at the beginning; place dynamic, user-specific content after the cached prefix.

stem: An architect must classify 500,000 archived product reviews overnight. No user is waiting on the result, and cost reduction is a priority. Which Anthropic API capability best fits this workload?
options: Open one long-lived streaming Messages connection per review to process them concurrently
Call the token-counting endpoint to price and process each review before classification
Submit the classifications through the Message Batches API as a single asynchronous batch job
Upload each review through the Files API and poll for a synchronous completion per file
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads and bills at roughly half the standard price, which directly matches an overnight bulk-classification job. Streaming targets low-latency interactive UX, token counting only estimates size and performs no classification, and the Files API stores documents but does not itself run or discount bulk inference.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a 50% discount, ideal for workloads that are not latency-sensitive.

stem: Which two statements accurately describe the Message Batches API? (Select 2.)
options: Batches are billed at a 50% discount relative to standard Messages API pricing
Batches are processed asynchronously and typically complete within 24 hours
Every batch is guaranteed to return complete results in under one minute for time-sensitive workloads
Each request in a batch streams its output token-by-token to the caller as it is generated
Batched requests bypass standard rate limits and are billed at the same rate as real-time requests
rationale: Batch requests receive a 50% pricing discount and are processed asynchronously with a target completion window of up to 24 hours. There is no sub-minute guarantee; batching explicitly trades latency for cost. Batched requests do not stream token-by-token, and they are discounted rather than billed at the standard real-time rate.
referenceText: Message Batches are processed asynchronously, complete within 24 hours, and cost 50% less than standard API requests.

stem: A product embeds three Claude-powered features: (a) high-volume real-time routing of inbound messages into eight categories, (b) drafting nuanced legal-risk analyses of contracts, and (c) simple extraction of dates from receipts at scale. To right-size for cost and capability, which model assignment is most appropriate?
options: Use a fast, low-cost model such as Haiku for routing and receipt extraction, and a frontier model such as Opus for the legal-risk analysis
Use the largest frontier model for all three features to guarantee uniform output quality
Use a single fast, low-cost model for all three features to minimize total spend
Use a frontier model for routing and receipt extraction, and a fast, low-cost model for the legal-risk analysis
rationale: Right-sizing matches task complexity to model tier: high-volume, low-complexity tasks (routing, date extraction) run well on a fast, inexpensive model, while deep multi-step reasoning (legal-risk analysis) warrants a frontier model. Using the largest model everywhere wastes cost on trivial tasks, using the smallest everywhere under-serves the reasoning-heavy task, and the last option inverts the correct mapping entirely.
referenceText: Choose the model tier that matches task complexity, latency, and cost requirements; use smaller models for simple high-volume tasks and larger models for complex reasoning.

stem: A team is building a RAG pipeline over a 400-page technical manual. Retrieval quality is poor because answers frequently span section boundaries and lose surrounding context. Which chunking strategy most directly addresses this?
options: Reduce chunks to a fixed 200 characters each to maximize retrieval granularity
Split on semantic boundaries such as headings and paragraphs, and add overlap between adjacent chunks so boundary-spanning context is preserved
Index the entire manual as a single embedding so context is never separated across chunks
Cut chunks at fixed token offsets with no overlap to keep chunk sizes perfectly uniform
rationale: Chunking on semantic boundaries with overlap keeps related content together and preserves context that straddles boundaries, improving retrieval relevance. Very small fixed-size chunks destroy meaning and inflate the index, a single giant chunk defeats retrieval, and fixed-offset cuts with no overlap are exactly the boundary-splitting behavior causing the reported failure.
referenceText: Effective retrieval chunks respect semantic boundaries and use overlap so that context spanning chunk edges is not lost.

stem: An architect wants to add semantic vector search to a RAG system that uses Claude for generation. Which approach aligns with Anthropic's guidance for producing the embeddings?
options: Read the embedding vector from the metadata field of a standard Claude Messages API response
Set an embeddings flag on the Messages request so Claude returns vectors instead of generated text
Average the per-token log-probabilities Claude returns to construct a sentence embedding
Generate the vectors with a dedicated embeddings provider such as Voyage AI, since Claude models do not expose an embeddings API
rationale: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embeddings model and recommends a dedicated embeddings provider such as Voyage AI for vector generation. The Messages API returns generated text, not embedding vectors in metadata; there is no embeddings request flag; and averaging log-probabilities is not a supported or meaningful way to obtain sentence embeddings.
referenceText: Anthropic recommends using a specialized embeddings model provider such as Voyage AI; Claude itself does not generate embeddings.

stem: A regulated-industry customer requires that every claim in Claude's answers be traceable to specific source passages to reduce unsupported statements and enable auditing. Which native platform capability should the architect use?
options: Raise the temperature so the model quotes source documents more frequently
Use stop_sequences to force the model to terminate after each cited sentence
Use the Citations feature, passing source material as document content blocks so responses reference the exact supporting text
Enable extended thinking mode, which appends source URLs to every generated answer
rationale: The Citations feature lets Claude ground responses in provided documents and return references to the exact supporting passages, which is precisely what an auditable, traceable answer requires. Higher temperature increases randomness and would reduce faithfulness, stop_sequences only halt generation and produce no citations, and extended thinking exposes reasoning but does not attach verified source references.
referenceText: Citations enables Claude to ground its answers in source documents and return references to the specific text that supports each claim.

stem: Before scaling a Claude feature to production, a team wants to determine whether prompt changes actually improve output quality. Which practice best supports this?
options: Build a representative evaluation set of graded test cases and measure each prompt version against it empirically
Release each prompt change straight to production and treat user complaints as the primary quality signal
Review three outputs from the latest prompt by hand and ship if they look acceptable
Treat any longer, more detailed prompt as an improvement without separate measurement
rationale: A held-out evaluation set with graded cases gives an empirical, repeatable measure of whether a change helps or regresses, the recommended way to validate prompt changes. Relying on production complaints is slow and harms users, eyeballing three outputs is not statistically meaningful, and assuming longer prompts are better ignores measurement entirely and often backfires.
referenceText: Develop empirical, test-driven evaluations with representative graded cases to measure whether prompt or model changes improve quality.

stem: An SRE team needs production observability so that when a user reports a bad Claude response, engineers can locate and investigate that exact API call, escalating to Anthropic support if needed. Which telemetry is most important to capture per request?
options: The aggregate daily count of successful and failed requests, broken down by endpoint
The per-response request-id header, together with token usage, latency, model, and stop_reason for each call
A rolling p95 latency metric per model, without retaining any per-request identifiers
The end-user session ID and UI event stream, which Anthropic support can use to locate the call
rationale: Logging the per-request request-id (plus usage, latency, model, and stop_reason) lets engineers correlate a specific complaint to a specific API call and reference it in support escalations, which is core to LLM observability. Aggregate daily counts and p95 latency give no per-call traceability, and an internal session ID is meaningless to Anthropic support without the request-id that identifies the call on Anthropic's side.
referenceText: Each API response includes a request-id; log it together with usage and latency to trace and debug individual requests.

stem: To comply with GDPR data-minimization when integrating Claude into an EU-facing application, which design choice is most aligned with the principle?
options: Forward each user's complete profile and history on every request so the model always has maximum context
Persist every raw prompt and completion indefinitely in case the data proves useful later
Embed users' government ID numbers in the system prompt to personalize each response
Send only the fields strictly necessary for the task and pseudonymize personal identifiers before they reach the API
rationale: Data minimization requires processing only the personal data necessary for the purpose, so sending just the required fields and pseudonymizing identifiers is the aligned choice. Forwarding full profiles, retaining all data indefinitely, and embedding sensitive IDs each expand personal-data exposure beyond what the task needs, directly violating the principle.
referenceText: Follow data-minimization practices: send only data necessary for the task and avoid transmitting or retaining unnecessary personal information.

stem: A healthcare provider wants to process protected health information (PHI) through Claude. What is the appropriate first step to support HIPAA compliance for this integration?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and configure the account for the agreed data-handling terms before transmitting any PHI
Rely on standard commercial terms, since API traffic is encrypted in transit by default
Publish a HIPAA-compliance statement in the application footer before processing PHI
Hash patient names within the prompt, which de-identifies the record sufficiently for HIPAA
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement with any vendor that processes PHI on the covered entity's behalf, so executing a BAA and configuring the agreed terms is the correct prerequisite. Transport encryption alone does not satisfy HIPAA's contractual requirements, a footer disclaimer has no legal effect, and hashing one field does not de-identify a record or replace the BAA obligation.
referenceText: Customers handling regulated data such as PHI must have appropriate agreements (e.g., a BAA) and data-handling configuration in place before processing that data.

stem: Which two statements about Anthropic's enterprise compliance and data-handling posture are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Anthropic maintains SOC 2 Type II compliance for its services
Zero-data-retention arrangements are available for qualifying enterprise use cases
Anthropic trains its foundation models on all commercial API inputs and outputs by default
Every standard API account is automatically authorized at FedRAMP High
Commercial API inputs are retained and used for model training unless customers opt out on each request
rationale: Anthropic holds SOC 2 Type II compliance and offers zero-data-retention arrangements for eligible enterprise customers. Anthropic does not train foundation models on commercial API inputs/outputs by default (so neither the 'by default' nor the 'opt out per request' framing is correct), and FedRAMP authorization applies to specific government-authorized offerings, not automatically to every standard account.
referenceText: Anthropic does not train its models on commercial API data by default, maintains SOC 2 Type II, and offers zero-data-retention options for qualifying customers.

stem: A company already runs its infrastructure in AWS and wants to call Claude without managing a separate Anthropic API key, keeping traffic and access control inside AWS. Which integration path fits best?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, but continue to authenticate with an Anthropic API key supplied in the request header
Call the first-party Anthropic API and tunnel responses into AWS through a VPC endpoint to obtain IAM-based access control
Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, authenticating with AWS IAM and invoking Bedrock model IDs
Use Google Vertex AI, which federates automatically with AWS IAM roles
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock uses native AWS IAM for authentication and Bedrock model identifiers for invocation, keeping auth and governance within AWS. Bedrock does not use an Anthropic API key, tunneling the first-party API does not confer IAM-based model authorization, and Vertex AI is Google Cloud's platform and does not federate with AWS IAM.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is invoked using AWS credentials/IAM and Bedrock model IDs rather than an Anthropic API key.

stem: A team that standardized on Google Cloud wants to invoke Claude using its existing GCP identity and regional controls. Which statement about using Claude on Vertex AI is correct?
options: Vertex AI requires supplying an Anthropic-issued API key in place of Google Cloud credentials
Requests authenticate with Google Cloud credentials and target a specific GCP project and region where the model is available
Vertex AI ignores region selection and routes every Claude request to a single global endpoint
Vertex AI can be used only after proxying each request through Amazon Bedrock
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication and is invoked against a specific GCP project and region where the model is offered. It does not use an Anthropic API key, region selection matters because model availability is region-scoped, and Vertex AI is independent of Amazon Bedrock.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication and is called within a chosen GCP project and supported region.

stem: A production integration intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses under load. Which resilience pattern is the most appropriate response?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop with no delay until each request eventually succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as fatal and surface a hard error to the user on the first occurrence
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honor retry-after guidance when present, and fall back to an alternate model or degraded path if failures persist
Open a circuit breaker permanently after the first 429 and route no further requests to the API
rationale: Transient 429/529 responses should be handled with exponential backoff plus jitter, respecting retry-after, with a fallback for sustained failures; this is standard resilient-client design. Tight no-delay retries worsen congestion and prolong throttling. Failing hard on the first transient error needlessly degrades reliability. A permanently open circuit breaker after a single 429 blocks all traffic and never recovers, which is a misuse of the pattern.
referenceText: Handle 429 and 529 responses by retrying with exponential backoff (and jitter), respecting retry timing, rather than retrying immediately or failing hard.

stem: An architect wants Claude-based agents to reach internal tools and data sources (a ticketing system, a document store) through a standardized, reusable interface instead of bespoke per-integration code. Which technology is purpose-built for this?
options: Duplicating hard-coded HTTP client code for each backend inside every agent application
The Model Context Protocol (MCP), where clients connect to servers that expose tools and resources over a standard interface
A single system prompt that inlines the full documentation for every tool on each request
The Message Batches API, which standardizes agent access to external systems
rationale: MCP defines a standard client-server protocol for exposing tools and resources, enabling reusable, interoperable integrations across applications. Duplicating per-backend HTTP code is exactly the fragmentation MCP eliminates. A giant inline system prompt does not connect to systems or standardize access. The Batches API is for asynchronous bulk inference, not standardized tool integration.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard in which clients connect to servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts through a common interface.

stem: Anthropic announces that a Claude model version powering a live product will be deprecated on a future date. As the architect responsible for lifecycle and stakeholder management, what is the best course of action?
options: Wait until the model begins returning errors in production, then swap models reactively during the incident
Pin to the deprecated model version and assume it will remain available indefinitely
Migrate to a successor model immediately with no regression testing so the change ships quickly
Track the retirement timeline, validate a successor against the existing evaluation suite, and communicate the migration plan to stakeholders before the deprecation date
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means monitoring published deprecation dates, validating a successor against your eval suite, and proactively communicating the migration plan ahead of retirement. Waiting for production errors guarantees an avoidable outage. Pinning indefinitely ignores the announced end-of-life. Migrating with no regression testing risks silent quality regressions and erodes stakeholder trust.
referenceText: Anthropic publishes model deprecation timelines; plan migrations, validate replacements, and notify stakeholders before retirement dates.

stem: A production chatbot sends the same 9,000-token system prompt (product policies and few-shot examples) on every turn, followed by a short, varying user message. Using Claude Sonnet, the architect wants to cut input cost and time-to-first-token. What is the most effective way to apply prompt caching?
options: Place a cache_control breakpoint on the final user message so the entire request body is cached each turn
Place a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static system-prompt content, leaving the varying user message after it
Rely on Claude to automatically cache any prompt longer than 1,024 tokens with no cache_control markers
Place cache_control breakpoints on every message, including each user turn, to maximize the number of cache hits
rationale: Prompt caching caches the contiguous prefix up to a cache_control breakpoint, so the large static content must come first with the breakpoint at its end, before the varying user text; this maximizes prefix reuse. Caching the varying user message yields no reuse because its content changes each turn. There is no automatic caching—cache_control must be set explicitly. Marking every user turn wastes more-expensive cache writes on content that will never be reused.
referenceText: Prompt caching: cache breakpoints cache the prefix up to the marker; place static content before variable content.

stem: An enterprise must classify 500,000 archived support tickets overnight. The job is not latency-sensitive, and the team wants the lowest per-token cost while keeping code simple. Which approach best fits?
options: Send all requests to the standard synchronous endpoint on the priority service tier for guaranteed throughput
Stream each request individually to reduce time-to-first-token across the whole run
Rely on prompt caching alone to make the 500,000 synchronous requests cost-effective
Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes asynchronously within 24 hours at roughly 50% lower token cost
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large, latency-tolerant workloads: asynchronous processing (target within 24 hours) at about a 50% discount on input and output tokens. The priority tier optimizes for low latency at higher cost. Streaming reduces perceived latency for interactive use but does not lower cost. Prompt caching helps only when a shared prefix is reused and does not address bulk asynchronous throughput or pricing on its own.
referenceText: Message Batches API: asynchronous processing within 24 hours at 50% cost savings for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: A team runs a high-volume intent-classification step where inputs are short and outputs are a single label. The current pipeline uses Claude Opus and is too expensive and slow. Following model right-sizing guidance, what is the best first optimization to evaluate?
options: Move the classification step to a smaller, faster model such as Claude Haiku and validate accuracy against a held-out evaluation set
Keep Opus but set max_tokens to 1 so the model is forced to answer faster and cheaper
Keep Opus but raise temperature to speed up sampling of the single label
Switch every step of the pipeline to Haiku without measuring, since Haiku is sufficient for any classification
rationale: Right-sizing matches task complexity to model capability: a bounded classification task is a strong candidate for a smaller, faster, cheaper model like Haiku, validated against a held-out eval set to confirm accuracy holds. Lowering max_tokens caps output length but does not reduce the cost of an expensive model on input and cannot force a valid label. Temperature changes sampling randomness, not speed or cost. Blindly downsizing the whole pipeline unmeasured is unsafe—some steps may need a larger model.
referenceText: Choosing a model: balance intelligence, speed, and cost; validate downsizing with evaluations.

stem: During a traffic spike, an application intermittently receives HTTP 529 responses from the Claude API. What is the appropriate resilient-design response?
options: Treat 529 as an authentication failure and rotate the API key before retrying
Treat 529 as a malformed-request error and stop sending the request permanently
Treat 529 as a transient overloaded condition and retry with exponential backoff and jitter
Retry the same request immediately in a tight loop with no delay until it succeeds
rationale: A 529 indicates the API is temporarily overloaded, so the correct pattern is retry with exponential backoff plus jitter to avoid synchronized retry storms. It is not an auth problem (that surfaces as 401), so key rotation is irrelevant. It is not a client validation error (400/422), so permanently dropping the request is wrong. Tight, delay-free retry loops amplify the overload.
referenceText: API errors: 529 overloaded is transient; retry with exponential backoff.

stem: An architect is designing the ingestion pipeline for a RAG system over long technical manuals. Retrieval quality is poor because chunks frequently split sentences and lose surrounding context. Which chunking adjustment most directly addresses this?
options: Increase chunk size to the full document so no context is ever lost
Use semantically aware chunking that respects sentence and section boundaries, with a modest overlap between adjacent chunks
Reduce every chunk to a single sentence to maximize retrieval precision
Remove all overlap between chunks to eliminate duplicate tokens in the index
rationale: Structure-aware chunking that respects sentence and section boundaries, combined with a small overlap, preserves context that spans chunk edges and improves retrieval relevance. Making each chunk the whole document defeats retrieval granularity and inflates token cost. Single-sentence chunks strip the surrounding context needed to answer. Removing overlap reintroduces the exact boundary-context loss the team is trying to fix.
referenceText: RAG/embeddings guidance: chunk on semantic boundaries with overlap to preserve context.

stem: A regulated customer requires that every answer produced by a document-Q&A assistant be traceable to the exact source text, and wants to reduce unsupported claims. Which Claude API capability most directly satisfies this?
options: Raise temperature so the model explores more of the source document
Increase max_tokens so the model can restate the entire document in each answer
Disable streaming so the full answer is returned before any source is shown
Enable the Citations feature so responses reference and quote the specific source passages used
rationale: The Citations feature grounds responses in provided documents and returns the specific cited source passages, giving verifiable traceability and reducing unsupported claims. Higher temperature increases variability, working against faithfulness. Larger max_tokens lets the model output more text but creates no verifiable source links. Streaming settings affect delivery timing, not source attribution.
referenceText: Citations: ground responses in source documents and return the exact cited text.

stem: A team wants to measure whether a prompt change improved answer quality on an open-ended summarization task where exact-match scoring is impossible. Which evaluation approach is most appropriate?
options: Ship the change to all production traffic and treat customer complaints as the primary quality signal
Have one engineer read a few outputs once and approve the change by intuition
Build a graded evaluation set and use an LLM-as-judge with a clear rubric to score outputs at scale
Use average output token count as a proxy for summary quality
rationale: For open-ended tasks where deterministic scoring fails, an LLM-as-judge with a well-defined rubric over a representative graded eval set gives a scalable, repeatable signal you can compare across prompt versions. Shipping straight to all users makes the change itself the experiment and harms customers. A one-off ad hoc read is neither representative nor repeatable. Token count measures length, not quality.
referenceText: Develop tests: use eval sets and model-based grading for open-ended tasks.

stem: An application invoking Claude on Amazon Bedrock experiences throttling in a single region during peak hours, causing failures. Which Bedrock-native capability best improves resilience and throughput without the team building failover logic themselves?
options: Use a cross-region inference profile so requests are automatically distributed across multiple regions
Hard-code a secondary region and switch manually by editing configuration during each incident
Request a higher rate limit and continue routing all traffic to the single region
Disable client retries so failed requests surface to end users immediately
rationale: Bedrock cross-region inference profiles automatically route and distribute traffic across multiple regions, increasing available throughput and resilience with no customer-built failover. Manually editing config per incident is slow, error-prone, and not resilient. A single-region quota bump adds no redundancy against a regional capacity event. Disabling retries removes a resilience mechanism and worsens user-visible failures.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock: cross-region inference distributes traffic across regions for throughput and resilience.

stem: A healthcare provider will send protected health information (PHI) to Claude and must remain HIPAA-compliant. Which control is required before processing PHI?
options: Enable a PII-detection guardrail and rely on it to block PHI, which removes the need for a contractual agreement
Configure zero-data-retention alone, since a Business Associate Agreement is not required when PHI is not retained
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and process PHI only on an account configured for the appropriate data-handling controls
Add a system-prompt instruction marking the data as HIPAA-protected so the platform treats it as regulated
rationale: HIPAA compliance is a contractual and configuration matter: a signed BAA with Anthropic must be in place and the account configured for appropriate data handling (including retention controls) before PHI is processed. A detection guardrail cannot reliably catch all PHI and is not a substitute for the BAA. Zero-data-retention configuration alone does not remove the BAA requirement. A system-prompt instruction has no legal or platform-governance effect.
referenceText: Anthropic compliance: HIPAA support requires a signed BAA and appropriate data-handling configuration.

stem: Under GDPR data-minimization principles, a team is deciding what to send in each prompt for a customer-support assistant. Which practice best aligns with data minimization?
options: Send the customer's full account record on every request so the model always has maximum context
Send only the fields strictly necessary for the task and redact or tokenize identifiers that are not needed
Attach the entire customer database to the system prompt to avoid retrieval calls
Include raw personal identifiers in application logs so support staff can debug faster
rationale: Data minimization requires processing only the personal data necessary for the specific purpose, so sending just the needed fields and redacting or tokenizing unnecessary identifiers is correct. Sending the full record, attaching the whole database, or logging raw identifiers all expand the personal data processed and retained beyond what the task requires, conflicting with minimization and increasing breach and compliance exposure.
referenceText: Governance and data minimization: process only the personal data necessary for the task.

stem: An SRE team wants reliable correlation between their internal logs and Anthropic support when investigating a failed API call. Which observability practice should they build into their client?
options: Log the full prompt and completion text of every request in plaintext to a shared channel
Disable client-side logging and rely on Anthropic to retain full request bodies for later lookup
Generate a random client-side UUID per request and provide it to support for server-side lookup
Capture and log the request-id returned in each API response so it can be given to support for tracing
rationale: Each Claude API response includes a request-id; persisting it lets the team correlate their telemetry with Anthropic-side traces when escalating. Logging full prompts and completions in plaintext to a broadly accessible location creates PII and governance risk and is not the tracing key. Disabling logging removes observability entirely. A client-generated UUID is not the identifier Anthropic indexes by, so it enables no server-side lookup.
referenceText: API observability: responses include a request-id used to trace and debug requests.

stem: A team standardizing on Google Cloud wants to call Claude while keeping data governance and billing within their existing GCP project. Which integration is appropriate?
options: Access Claude through the Vertex AI integration, authenticating with Google Cloud credentials in their project
Call the first-party Anthropic API and manually re-bill usage to their GCP project each month
Route Claude generation through a personal Anthropic API key stored in the project's source repository
Use Vertex AI for embeddings only and send all Claude generation to a separate consumer account
rationale: Claude is available through Google Cloud's Vertex AI, where authentication and billing flow through the customer's GCP project, satisfying the governance and consolidation requirement. Using the first-party API and reconciling billing by hand defeats the reason to consolidate on GCP. Storing an Anthropic key in a source repo is a severe security failure. Splitting generation onto a separate consumer account fragments governance and billing, the opposite of the goal.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI: access Claude via Google Cloud with GCP authentication and billing.

stem: An architect is deciding how to let a Claude-based agent access several internal systems (a ticketing tool, a search index, and a database) in a standardized, reusable way across multiple applications. Which approach best fits the Model Context Protocol (MCP) design intent?
options: Write bespoke function-calling glue for each system inside every application separately
Embed each system's credentials in the system prompt so the model can call it directly
Expose each system as an MCP server so any MCP-compatible client can discover and call its tools through a common protocol
Fine-tune a separate model per system so no external tool interface is required
rationale: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, resources, and prompts to models; wrapping each system as an MCP server lets any compatible client reuse those integrations through one protocol. Bespoke per-application glue is exactly the duplication MCP eliminates. Credentials in the prompt is a security anti-pattern that provides no callable interface. Per-system fine-tuning gives the model no live, governed access to those systems.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol: an open standard for connecting models to tools and data via servers and clients.

stem: An architect is hardening a customer-facing Claude integration for reliability. Which practices improve resilience against transient upstream failures? (Select 3.)
options: Retry retryable errors (429, 529, 5xx) using exponential backoff with jitter
Set sensible client-side request timeouts and handle a timeout as a distinct failure mode
Define a graceful fallback, such as a smaller model or a cached/default response, when the primary path is unavailable
Retry every error, including 400 validation errors, indefinitely until it succeeds
Remove client-side timeouts so slow requests are never cut off
rationale: Resilient design combines backoff-with-jitter retries for genuinely transient errors, explicit timeouts with distinct handling, and a defined fallback so the system degrades gracefully. Retrying non-retryable 400 validation errors indefinitely wastes resources and never succeeds because the request is malformed. Removing all timeouts lets a single stalled request hang the caller and exhaust resources, reducing rather than improving resilience.
referenceText: Rate limits and error handling: backoff on retryable errors; use timeouts and fallbacks.

stem: Anthropic has announced a deprecation date for the model version an application pins in production. What is the best lifecycle-management response for the architect?
options: Treat the notice as informational, since pinned model versions remain supported indefinitely once deployed
Plan a migration to a supported model, run the existing eval suite against it to confirm quality, and cut over before the retirement date
Wait until the model returns errors in production, then swap models without re-evaluating
Switch all traffic to the successor model in production immediately with no evaluation to save time
rationale: Deprecation notices signal a firm retirement timeline, so the architect should migrate to a supported model, validate it against the existing eval suite to catch behavioral regressions, and cut over before retirement. Pinned versions are not supported indefinitely. Waiting for hard failures causes an avoidable outage. Switching everything with no evaluation risks silent quality regressions; the eval gate is what makes the migration safe.
referenceText: Model deprecations: migrate to supported models ahead of retirement and validate with evaluations.

stem: Before rolling out a new feature, a finance team needs to estimate per-request input cost accurately for prompts of varying length, without incurring generation charges. Which tool is most appropriate?
options: Send each prompt to the model and read the input usage from the completion, accepting the generation cost
Estimate cost by dividing each prompt's character count by four in application code, without verifying it
Assume every prompt costs the same flat amount regardless of length
Call the token-counting endpoint to measure input tokens per request, then multiply by the model's input price
rationale: The token-counting endpoint returns the exact input token count for a request without generating a completion, enabling precise cost estimation at no generation charge. Actually calling the model incurs the very cost the team wants to estimate ahead of time. A characters-divided-by-four heuristic is approximate and unverified, and can be materially off for code or non-English text. A flat per-prompt assumption ignores cost's strong dependence on prompt length.
referenceText: Token counting: measure input tokens before sending a request for accurate cost estimation.

stem: A user-facing assistant produces long answers, and users complain about the wait before anything appears. Total generation time is acceptable, but the initial delay feels slow. Which optimization most directly improves the experience?
options: Enable streaming so tokens are displayed incrementally, reducing perceived time-to-first-token
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to produce a longer, more complete answer
Switch to the Message Batches API to lower the cost of each long answer
Raise temperature so the model begins responding more creatively and therefore sooner
rationale: Streaming returns tokens as they are generated, so users see output almost immediately, addressing the perceived-latency (time-to-first-token) complaint even when total generation time is unchanged. Increasing max_tokens can make responses longer and slower. The Batches API is asynchronous and worsens interactive latency. Temperature affects randomness, not latency or first-token timing.
referenceText: Streaming and reduce-latency guidance: stream responses to improve perceived latency.

stem: A team building a RAG system asks the architect which embedding model to use, expecting a first-party Anthropic embeddings endpoint. What is the accurate architectural guidance?
options: Call the Messages API with an output type of 'embedding' to return vectors directly
Integrate a third-party embeddings provider such as Voyage AI for the retrieval step, since Anthropic does not offer a first-party embeddings model
Derive embeddings from the final hidden-state values returned by any Claude generative model
Use prompt caching to index the corpus, since cached prefixes act as semantic vectors
rationale: Anthropic does not offer its own embeddings model and documents integrating a third-party provider such as Voyage AI for the embedding/retrieval stage; the design separates the embedding provider from the generation model. The Messages API generates text and has no embedding output mode. Generative Claude models do not expose usable embedding vectors. Prompt caching optimizes repeated prefixes and performs no semantic retrieval.
referenceText: Anthropic recommends third-party embedding providers (e.g., Voyage AI) for RAG.

stem: An architect is defining an evaluation program for a Claude feature before and after launch. Which practices reflect sound evaluation and testing methodology? (Select 2.)
options: Build a representative eval set with defined success criteria and version it alongside the prompt
Integrate evaluations into CI so prompt or model changes are regression-tested automatically
Base the go/no-go decision on a single strong example captured during a stakeholder demo
Defer all measurement to production, monitoring only aggregate traffic with no offline test set
rationale: Sound practice uses a representative, criteria-defined eval set versioned with the prompt, and wires evaluations into a pipeline so changes are regression-tested automatically. A single demo example is neither representative nor statistically meaningful. Relying only on production aggregates with no offline set removes the ability to catch regressions before release and complicates root-causing.
referenceText: Develop representative, criteria-based eval sets integrated into a repeatable pipeline.

stem: A government contractor must meet FedRAMP requirements while using Claude and asks the architect for a compliant deployment path. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Deploy Claude through a cloud platform that provides a FedRAMP-authorized environment, such as Amazon Bedrock in an authorized region
Document in the system prompt that all processing occurs within a FedRAMP authorization boundary
Encrypt the API key at rest on the client, which brings the workload into FedRAMP scope
Route requests through the standard commercial API, which inherits FedRAMP authorization by default
rationale: FedRAMP authorizes a deployment environment, so a compliant path means consuming Claude through a cloud platform and region carrying the appropriate FedRAMP authorization, such as Bedrock in an authorized environment. A system-prompt statement has no bearing on authorization boundaries. The standard commercial API is not itself a FedRAMP-authorized boundary by default. Encrypting a key is hygiene, not authorization.
referenceText: Use an authorized cloud environment/region (e.g., Claude on Amazon Bedrock) to meet government compliance requirements.

stem: A customer-support application sends the same 8,000-token product manual and system instructions on every request, followed by a short, unique user question. Traffic is high and bursty, and both latency and per-request cost are concerns. Which optimization most directly reduces the cost and time spent re-processing the unchanging portion of each request?
options: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint after the static system instructions and manual
Submit each request through the Message Batches API so the manual is processed asynchronously
Downgrade every request from Claude Opus to Claude Haiku to cut per-token cost
Lower max_tokens so the model spends less time generating the response
rationale: Prompt caching marks a large, stable prefix (system instructions plus manual) with a cache_control breakpoint; subsequent requests reusing that prefix read it from cache at reduced cost and latency, exactly the repeated-static-context problem. The Batches API optimizes bulk asynchronous throughput, not repeated prefixes on interactive traffic. Downgrading to Haiku may hurt quality and still reprocesses the tokens. Lowering max_tokens affects only output tokens.
referenceText: Prompt caching caches large stable prefixes for reuse across requests at reduced cost and latency.

stem: A nightly job must classify 500,000 archived documents. No user is waiting on the results, which only need to be available within 24 hours, and the team wants the lowest possible cost. Which approach best fits these requirements?
options: Submit the workload through the Message Batches API
Issue synchronous requests in a loop and request a higher account rate limit
Enable prompt caching and stream each response as it completes
Use a priority service tier to guarantee fast processing
rationale: The Message Batches API targets large, latency-tolerant workloads at a substantial discount with a results window measured in hours, matching a 24-hour, no-user-waiting job. A synchronous loop is more expensive and fragile at that volume. Prompt caching helps repeated prefixes but gives no bulk discount, and streaming adds nothing offline. A priority tier optimizes latency (the opposite of the need) and costs more.
referenceText: Message Batches API processes large asynchronous workloads at reduced cost within a 24-hour window.

stem: During architecture review a stakeholder asks how to choose a Claude model for a new high-volume triage feature where most inputs are simple but a small fraction are complex. Which right-sizing strategy is most defensible?
options: Route the majority of simple traffic to a smaller, faster model and escalate only complex cases to a larger model, validating each tier against a shared eval set
Standardize on the largest available model so answer quality is never in question
Standardize on the smallest available model to guarantee the lowest cost per request
Select whichever model offers the largest context window, since context capacity drives answer quality
rationale: Right-sizing matches capability to task difficulty: serve common simple cases cheaply with a smaller model and escalate the hard minority to a more capable model, with an eval set confirming each tier meets its bar. Always using the largest wastes cost; always the smallest sacrifices accuracy on complex cases. Context-window size does not determine per-task reasoning quality.
referenceText: Choose the model that meets your quality bar at the lowest cost/latency, escalating harder cases as needed.

stem: A RAG system passes retrieved chunks to Claude, but reviewers cannot tell which source sentence supports each claim in the answer. The team wants Claude to return verifiable references to the exact supporting text with minimal custom parsing. What should they use?
options: Provide the source documents as content blocks and enable the Citations feature so responses return cited spans grounded in the source text
Lower the sampling temperature so the model stops fabricating its references
Adopt a higher-dimensional embedding model to raise retrieval recall
Extract quoted phrases from the answer with a regular expression in post-processing
rationale: The Citations feature accepts source documents as content blocks and returns answers with citation objects pointing at exact supporting spans, giving verifiable grounding without brittle parsing. Lowering temperature reduces variability but produces no structured citations. A better embedding model changes what is retrieved, not claim traceability. Regex post-processing is exactly the brittle parsing Citations replaces.
referenceText: Citations returns responses with references to the specific source spans that support each claim.

stem: A team is designing chunking for a RAG knowledge base of long technical articles. Retrieval quality is poor: relevant passages are often split so a chunk contains half of an idea and loses surrounding context. Which change most directly addresses this failure mode?
options: Add overlap between adjacent chunks and align chunk boundaries to semantic units such as sections or paragraphs
Return 100 chunks per query so the relevant passage is always somewhere in context
Index each article as a single large chunk so ideas are never split
Strip metadata from every chunk to lower token overhead
rationale: Overlap plus boundaries aligned to semantic units keeps each chunk self-contained so an idea is not severed across a boundary, the described defect. Returning 100 chunks floods context with noise and raises cost. A single giant chunk destroys retrieval granularity. Removing metadata harms filtering/ranking and does nothing for boundary placement.
referenceText: Effective chunking uses semantically coherent boundaries and overlap so retrieved chunks stay self-contained.

stem: Before launching a summarization feature, an architect wants to tell stakeholders objectively whether a prompt change makes quality better or worse. Which practice best supports this?
options: Grade candidate prompts against a representative eval set with defined success criteria and compare scores across versions
Ship the change and treat the absence of user complaints as confirmation of improvement
Have one engineer skim a handful of outputs and give a thumbs up or down
Compare only the token cost of the two prompts, favoring the cheaper one
rationale: A representative, held-out eval set with explicit success criteria lets you score each prompt version for an evidence-based, repeatable comparison. Absence of complaints is a lagging, biased signal that misses silent regressions. One engineer skimming is not representative. Cost alone ignores quality and can reward worse answers.
referenceText: Define measurable success criteria and use empirical evaluations to compare prompt/model versions.

stem: An evaluation must grade open-ended answers where exact-string matching fails because many phrasings are valid. The team wants a scalable, consistent scoring method beyond manual review. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Use an LLM-as-judge with a fixed rubric and grading prompt, validated against a set of human-labeled examples
Mark an answer correct only when it matches a single canonical reference string
Score answers by length, treating longer responses as higher quality
Have the model that generated the answer assign its own final score without a rubric
rationale: For open-ended outputs with many valid phrasings, an LLM-as-judge guided by a well-defined rubric scales grading consistently, and validating it against human labels calibrates reliability. Exact-string matching fails on paraphrase variation. Length is not a quality signal. Letting the generating model self-score without a rubric introduces bias and no consistent standard.
referenceText: For open-ended grading use rubric-based methods such as LLM-based grading, calibrated against human judgments.

stem: A production integration intermittently returns HTTP 429 responses during traffic spikes. The team wants a resilient client that maximizes successful completions without overwhelming the API. What is the recommended handling?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when present
Retry the failed request immediately in a tight loop until it succeeds
Treat the 429 as a permanent failure and surface an error without retrying
Increase max_tokens on the retry so the server prioritizes the request
rationale: 429 indicates rate limiting; the resilient pattern backs off exponentially with jitter and honors retry-after so retries are spaced rather than amplifying the spike. A tight loop worsens congestion. Treating a transient 429 as permanent drops recoverable requests. max_tokens does not affect scheduling priority.
referenceText: On 429 rate-limit responses, back off exponentially and honor retry-after before retrying.

stem: An architect is documenting resilience for a real-time chat product. Occasionally the API returns a 529 (overloaded) error. Which client behavior is the most appropriate design response?
options: Retry the request after a backoff delay and, if overload persists, fall back to a degraded response path
Terminate the user session permanently on the first 529 response
Immediately reissue the request several times in parallel to raise the chance one succeeds
Cache the last successful response and return it unchanged for every subsequent turn
rationale: A 529 signals temporary overload; the correct design retries after a backoff and, if it persists, degrades gracefully via a fallback so the user still gets a usable experience. Permanently failing the session over a transient condition is poor resilience. Parallel retries amplify load and worsen overload. Returning a stale cached response for every turn produces incorrect answers.
referenceText: 529 indicates temporary overload; retry with backoff and design graceful fallbacks.

stem: A chat UI feels slow because users stare at a blank screen until the full answer is generated. Total generation time cannot be reduced, but the team wants better perceived latency. What is the most effective change?
options: Stream the response so tokens render incrementally, reducing the perceived time-to-first-token
Raise max_tokens so the model has more room to complete its answer
Route interactive requests through the Message Batches API
Append more retrieved context to each prompt so answers are more complete
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens as produced, so users see output almost immediately and perceived latency drops even when total generation time is unchanged. Raising max_tokens can increase generation time. The Batches API is asynchronous and unsuitable for interactive chat. Adding context increases input processing and latency.
referenceText: Streaming improves perceived latency by returning output incrementally as it is generated.

stem: A healthcare provider will process protected health information (PHI) through Claude and must meet HIPAA obligations. Which measures are appropriate for a compliant, well-governed deployment? (Select 3.)
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider before transmitting PHI
Apply data minimization, sending only the PHI strictly required for the task
Maintain audit logs of access and requests that involve PHI
Treat the provider's SOC 2 Type II report as sufficient to authorize sending PHI without a BAA
De-identify records only after the prompt has already been transmitted to the API
rationale: HIPAA-aligned deployments require a signed BAA before PHI is processed, data minimization so only necessary PHI is transmitted, and audit logging to demonstrate controlled access. A SOC 2 report is not a substitute for a BAA covering PHI. De-identifying only after transmission means PHI has already left the boundary unprotected, defeating the safeguard.
referenceText: Handling PHI requires a BAA, data minimization, encryption, and audit logging.

stem: A European product must comply with GDPR. A user submits a valid right-to-erasure request. Which architectural practice best supports honoring such requests?
options: Design for data minimization and maintain a data map so stored personal data can be located and deleted on request
Retain all raw prompts indefinitely in case they are needed for future model training
Delegate erasure entirely to the model provider for data held in your own databases
Hash user identifiers once and treat the hashed data as fully exempt from GDPR
rationale: Meeting erasure depends on knowing where personal data lives; data minimization plus a maintained data map lets you locate and delete a subject's data across systems. Retaining all prompts indefinitely conflicts with storage-limitation and minimization principles. The provider cannot erase data in your own stores. Hashing is pseudonymization, not anonymization, and does not remove GDPR obligations.
referenceText: Data minimization and knowing where personal data is stored support GDPR rights such as erasure.

stem: A regulated enterprise standardized on AWS wants to consume Claude while keeping traffic, IAM, and governance inside their AWS account boundary. Which deployment option best meets this?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, using AWS IAM and Bedrock's regional endpoints
Call the public Anthropic API directly and forward responses into the AWS account
Deploy the Claude model weights on the team's own EC2 instances
Consume Claude via Google Vertex AI and peer it into the AWS VPC
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets an AWS-standardized enterprise invoke Claude with AWS-native IAM, regions, and governance, keeping the integration inside the AWS boundary. Calling the public API moves auth and governance outside AWS. Anthropic does not distribute weights to run on EC2. Vertex AI is the Google Cloud path, not AWS-native.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock provides AWS-native access with IAM-based auth and regional endpoints.

stem: A team running Claude on Google Vertex AI reports that requests to one specific model fail while other models work. Which cause is most consistent with Vertex AI's model access model and should be checked first?
options: The specific Claude model has not been enabled in Vertex AI Model Garden for that project and region
Vertex AI expects an Anthropic API key in the Authorization header, which this request omitted
Vertex AI supports only the Message Batches API, so single synchronous requests always fail
Vertex AI requires a prompt-cache breakpoint on every request, and this one omitted it
rationale: On Vertex AI, individual Claude models must be enabled/granted in Model Garden per project and region; a model not enabled in that region is the classic cause of selective failures. Vertex uses Google Cloud credentials, not an Anthropic API key. Vertex supports synchronous requests, not batches only. Prompt caching is optional, not a prerequisite.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI requires enabling the specific model in Model Garden per project/region and uses Google Cloud auth.

stem: To debug intermittent production issues, an SRE wants to correlate a specific failed Claude API call with Anthropic's logs when opening a support case. Which piece of information from each API response is most useful to capture in observability tooling?
options: The request-id returned with each API response
The stop_reason value on the response
The output token count from the usage object
The temperature configured for the call
rationale: Each API response includes a unique request-id (in headers/SDK), the identifier Anthropic support uses to look up a specific call, so logging it is essential for correlation. stop_reason explains why generation ended but does not identify the request. Token counts and temperature describe content and settings, not a locatable request identity.
referenceText: Capture the request-id from responses to correlate a specific call with provider-side logs for support.

stem: An architect is defining an observability strategy for a Claude-based service to support cost control, quality monitoring, and incident response. Which signals are most valuable to capture per request? (Select 3.)
options: Input and output token usage for cost attribution
End-to-end latency, including time-to-first-token
The request-id and error type for failed calls
The raw API key on each request, stored so failed calls can be replayed with the exact credential
The provider's server-side GPU utilization at request time
rationale: Token usage enables cost attribution and right-sizing, latency/TTFT tracks user experience and SLOs, and request-id plus error type make failures diagnosable and correlatable with provider logs. Storing raw API keys in logs is a serious security violation. Server-side GPU utilization is internal to the provider and not observable by the client, so it cannot be captured per request.
referenceText: Track token usage, latency, and request-id/error metadata; never log secrets like API keys.

stem: An MCP-based agent exposes tools to Claude. Before rolling it to production, the team wants confidence that Claude reliably selects the correct tool and passes valid arguments across realistic scenarios. Which testing practice is most appropriate?
options: Build an evaluation suite of representative user scenarios and assert on the tool calls and arguments the model produces
Invoke each tool once directly against the MCP server and assume the model will call them correctly in production
Confirm only that the MCP server process starts without errors
Rely on each tool having a description, since well-described tools are reliably selected
rationale: Evaluating tool use means running representative scenarios and asserting the model selects the right tool with valid arguments, catching selection and schema errors before release. Invoking tools directly tests the server, not the model's decisions. Confirming the server starts checks liveness, not correctness. Good descriptions help but do not guarantee correct calls; empirical evaluation is still required.
referenceText: Evaluate agent tool-use by asserting on the model's tool selection and arguments across representative scenarios.

stem: A team wants to estimate and control input cost before sending large prompts, and to validate that a prompt fits within a model's context window. Which capability directly supports this without generating a full response?
options: The token counting endpoint, which returns the number of input tokens for a given request
Streaming, which reports token usage only after the full response completes
The Message Batches API, which counts tokens as a side effect of processing
Prompt caching, which prevents prompts from exceeding the context window
rationale: The token counting endpoint returns the input token count for a prospective request without running generation, letting you estimate cost and confirm the prompt fits the context window ahead of time. Streaming reports usage only after generation, which does not help pre-flight. The Batches API still generates responses and is not a lightweight counting tool. Prompt caching reduces cost of repeated prefixes but does not measure or cap context size.
referenceText: The token counting endpoint returns input token counts for a request before generation.

stem: An architect must communicate an upcoming model version migration to non-technical stakeholders and minimize regression risk. Which lifecycle approach is most sound?
options: Run the new model against the existing eval set, share a before/after quality and cost comparison, and pilot behind a flag before full rollout
Swap the model in production immediately and announce it after the fact
Delay any migration indefinitely because change always introduces risk
Let each engineer choose whichever model they prefer per request with no baseline comparison
rationale: Sound lifecycle management evaluates the new model on the established eval set, communicates an evidence-based before/after comparison stakeholders can understand, and pilots behind a flag to contain regressions before full rollout. Silent immediate swaps risk unmeasured regressions and erode stakeholder trust. Indefinite delay forgoes improvements and eventual deprecation forces the issue anyway. Ad hoc per-engineer model choices with no baseline make behavior inconsistent and unmeasurable.
referenceText: Validate model migrations against evals and pilot changes before full rollout; communicate results to stakeholders.

stem: A RAG pipeline sometimes answers confidently from chunks that are only loosely related to the question, producing subtly wrong answers. Retrieval returns the top-k by vector similarity only. Which improvement most directly targets this precision problem?
options: Add a re-ranking step over the initially retrieved candidates and instruct Claude to answer only from the provided context or say it does not know
Increase temperature so the model is more creative with the retrieved chunks
Remove the retrieval step and rely on the model's parametric knowledge
Embed the query with a different random seed on each call
rationale: Re-ranking the initial vector candidates raises precision by promoting the truly relevant passages, and instructing Claude to answer only from context (or abstain) curbs confident answers from loosely related chunks. Higher temperature increases variability and hallucination risk. Removing retrieval abandons grounding entirely and worsens factuality. Embeddings are deterministic for a given input; a 'random seed' has no meaningful effect and does not improve relevance.
referenceText: Improve RAG precision with re-ranking and by grounding the model to answer only from provided context.

stem: An enterprise agent sends a 12,000-token system prompt (compliance policies plus tool definitions) that is byte-for-byte identical on every request, followed by a short user turn that changes each call. The team wants to reduce both cost and time-to-first-token. What is the best way to apply prompt caching?
options: Place the static system prompt and tool definitions before the variable user content and set a cache_control breakpoint at the end of that static block, so later requests read the prefix from cache.
Put the variable user turn first and mark it with cache_control so it is cached and reused across requests.
Lower max_tokens on each request; caching engages automatically once the output is small enough.
Split the 12,000-token system prompt into roughly 60 separate 200-token cache breakpoints to maximize the number of cache hits.
rationale: Prompt caching works on a stable prefix. Marking the end of the identical system/tool block with a cache_control breakpoint lets subsequent requests hit the cache (cache reads are billed at a small fraction of base input tokens) and lowers TTFT, while the changing user turn stays after the breakpoint. Caching the variable content produces a different prefix each time, so it never hits. max_tokens governs output length and has nothing to do with caching, which is not automatic. There is a minimum cacheable length (roughly 1,024 tokens for Sonnet/Opus) and at most 4 cache breakpoints, so 200-token blocks fall below the minimum and exceed the breakpoint limit.
referenceText: Prompt caching lets you cache a stable prefix (system prompt, tools, documents) by placing a cache_control breakpoint on the last identical block; cache reads cost a fraction of base input tokens, there is a per-model minimum cacheable length, and up to 4 breakpoints are allowed.

stem: A team must classify 2 million archived support tickets overnight. The labeled results are consumed by analysts the next morning, no end user is waiting on any individual response, and reducing token cost is a priority. Which approach best fits these constraints?
options: Submit the workload to the Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly a 50% discount on input and output tokens.
Fire 2 million synchronous Messages requests in a tight parallel loop to finish as quickly as possible.
Enable prompt caching with response streaming to minimize per-request latency.
Increase max_tokens and pack all 2 million tickets into a single request's context window.
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant workloads and offers roughly a 50% cost reduction with asynchronous processing that completes within 24 hours, which matches an overnight batch consumed the next morning. Firing synchronous requests optimizes for speed the team does not need, incurs full price, and risks rate-limit pressure. Caching with streaming optimizes latency (not the goal) and does not provide the batch discount. Packing all tickets into one request is impossible: 2 million tickets vastly exceed any context window, and it defeats per-ticket labeling.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a 50% cost discount, with batches completing within 24 hours; ideal for non-time-sensitive bulk workloads.

stem: Before launch, an evaluation on a held-out set shows Haiku scoring 0.94 and Opus scoring 0.95 on a high-volume intent-routing classification, with Haiku at a fraction of the cost and latency. What is the soundest recommendation?
options: Deploy Haiku for the routing classification and reserve larger models for the harder downstream tasks where evals justify the added cost.
Always deploy the most capable model available regardless of eval parity, to be safe.
Deploy Opus, because a 0.01 accuracy gap will compound catastrophically once traffic scales.
Skip the eval entirely and pick the model with the largest parameter count.
rationale: Model right-sizing means letting task-specific evaluation drive model choice. When a smaller model reaches near-parity on a well-defined task, deploying it captures large cost and latency savings, and the capable model is reserved where evals show it is needed. Always deploying the most capable model ignores empirical evidence and overpays. The compounding claim is unfounded; the eval already reflects task performance, and a 0.01 gap does not justify the cost multiple without evidence. Picking by parameter count discards the evidence-based process and treats size as a proxy for task fit.
referenceText: Choose the smallest model that meets your success criteria on representative evaluations; right-sizing balances capability against cost and latency per task.

stem: A legal RAG application must let reviewers verify every factual claim in the answer against the exact source passage, and fabricated references are unacceptable. Which capability best supports verifiable, grounded attribution?
options: Use the Citations feature by supplying source documents in the request so Claude returns claims linked to the specific document locations it actually used.
Append "cite your sources" to the prompt and rely on the model to generate bracketed reference numbers.
Set temperature to 0 so the model becomes incapable of hallucinating citations.
Post-process the output with a regex that attaches the lexically nearest chunk to each sentence.
rationale: The Citations feature has Claude reference the exact locations in the provided source documents that support each generated claim, giving reviewers verifiable, model-grounded attribution rather than free-text references. Prompting for citations relies on the model inventing reference markers, which can be fabricated. Temperature 0 reduces randomness but does not guarantee grounded or accurate citations. Attaching sources by surface similarity after the fact does not reflect what the model actually used and can mislabel claims.
referenceText: The Citations feature grounds responses in supplied source documents and returns citations pointing to the specific passages used, improving verifiability.

stem: A production service intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses during traffic spikes. Which TWO practices together form a resilient retry and fallback strategy? (Select 2.)
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when the response includes one.
Treat every 429 and 529 as fatal and immediately fail the user's request with no retry.
Retry instantly in a tight loop until the call eventually succeeds.
Cap total retries and, once the budget is exhausted, fall back to a degraded response or a smaller-model path.
Retry on a fixed 1-second interval regardless of any retry-after value the response provides.
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient. Exponential backoff with jitter (respecting retry-after) spreads load and avoids synchronized retry storms, and bounding retries with a graceful fallback preserves availability when the backend stays saturated. Treating these codes as fatal is wrong because they are retryable. Tight-loop instant retries amplify load and worsen overload. Fixed-interval retries that ignore retry-after synchronize clients into retry storms and disregard the server's explicit backoff signal.
referenceText: 429 (rate_limit_error) and 529 (overloaded_error) indicate rate limiting and overload; the SDKs retry transient errors with exponential backoff honoring retry-after, and clients should bound retries with fallbacks.

stem: A healthcare product will send clinical notes to Claude to draft visit summaries. Which combination best aligns with HIPAA obligations and the principle of data minimization?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement with the provider, use a HIPAA-enabled organization, and strip identifiers not required for the task before sending the note.
Send complete records including all PHI so the model has maximum context, relying on the model to disregard sensitive fields.
Retain every prompt and completion indefinitely in plaintext to guarantee audit completeness.
Rely solely on TLS in transit, since at-rest encryption and retention controls are unnecessary for LLM API calls.
rationale: HIPAA-ready use of the Claude API requires a signed BAA and a HIPAA-enabled organization, which applies safeguards such as encryption, access controls, and audit logging; data minimization means sending only the PHI the task actually needs and removing the rest. Sending all PHI violates minimization and trusts the model with unnecessary data. Indefinite plaintext retention maximizes breach exposure and contradicts minimization. Relying on TLS alone ignores at-rest protection and retention governance, which are essential controls beyond transport encryption.
referenceText: The Claude API supports HIPAA-ready integrations: with a signed BAA and a HIPAA-enabled organization you can process PHI under safeguards such as encryption, access controls, and audit logging, sending only data necessary for the task.

stem: A team needs to measure the quality of open-ended, free-text summaries at scale, where exact-match grading against a single reference is meaningless. Which evaluation approach is most defensible?
options: Build a held-out eval set graded by an LLM-as-judge against a clear rubric, and validate the judge's scores against a sample of human ratings.
Read a handful of outputs each release and rely on subjective impressions to decide if quality is acceptable.
Use exact string match against one reference summary as the only quality metric.
Have the same model that produced each summary rate its own output from 1 to 10 with no rubric and no human validation.
rationale: For open-ended generation, a curated held-out eval set scored by an LLM-as-judge with an explicit grading rubric scales to many examples, and calibrating the judge against human labels keeps it trustworthy. Reading a handful of outputs is unrepeatable and non-quantitative. Exact match fails because valid summaries can differ entirely from one reference. Uncalibrated model self-scoring lacks a rubric and human validation, making the scores unreliable.
referenceText: For subjective or open-ended tasks, use held-out eval sets with model-graded rubrics (LLM-as-judge) and validate grading against human judgments.

stem: To debug intermittent production failures and uphold latency SLOs on Claude API traffic, which observability practice provides the most value?
options: Log the request-id, model, token usage, and end-to-end latency for every call so failures can be correlated with the provider and latency percentiles tracked.
Log only the final user-visible text to keep storage costs minimal.
Track only average latency, since percentiles mostly add noise.
Rely on a client-side stopwatch for timing and do not capture any request identifiers.
rationale: Capturing the per-request request-id lets you correlate specific failures with the provider's records for support and root-cause analysis, while logging model, token usage, and latency enables cost attribution and percentile (p95/p99) SLO tracking. Logging only user-visible text discards the metadata needed to diagnose failures. Tracking only averages is wrong because SLOs are defined on tail latency; averages hide the p95/p99 spikes users feel. A stopwatch with no request identifiers makes failures hard to trace with the provider.
referenceText: Every API response includes a unique request-id header (also the request_id field in error bodies) used to correlate and debug requests with support; capturing usage and latency enables cost and percentile monitoring.
