stem: A healthcare startup is building a patient-intake summarizer on the Claude API that will process protected health information (PHI). Their compliance officer asks what MUST be in place before any PHI is sent to Claude. Which action is the required first step to use Claude with PHI in a HIPAA-regulated workflow?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the model provider before sending any PHI
Enable prompt caching so PHI is stored in an encrypted cache rather than in prompts
Set max_tokens to a low value so less PHI is retained in outputs
Rely on Claude's built-in safety training to automatically redact PHI from responses
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with any vendor that processes PHI on your behalf; Anthropic offers a BAA to eligible customers, and it must be in place before PHI is transmitted. Prompt caching is a latency/cost feature and does not create a HIPAA-compliant relationship. Lowering max_tokens limits output length, not PHI exposure. Claude's safety training does not guarantee PHI redaction and is not a substitute for the contractual and technical safeguards HIPAA mandates.
referenceText: Anthropic Trust Center / privacy documentation on HIPAA and Business Associate Agreements

stem: An enterprise architect must confirm to their data-protection team whether prompts and completions sent to the Anthropic commercial API are used to train Anthropic's models by default. Which statement accurately reflects Anthropic's standard commercial API data-handling policy?
options: Inputs and outputs from commercial API traffic are not used to train Anthropic's models by default
All API traffic is used for training unless the customer files a manual opt-out ticket per project
Only prompts flagged by the safety classifier are excluded from training data
API data is always used for training but is fully anonymized before use
rationale: Under Anthropic's commercial terms, inputs and outputs from the commercial API are not used to train models by default; customers do not need to opt out. The other options misstate the default: training is not the default, exclusion is not conditional on classifier flags, and the policy is not 'always trained but anonymized.' Architects must communicate the correct default to satisfy data-governance and GDPR data-minimization reviews.
referenceText: Anthropic Commercial Terms of Service and Privacy Center on data usage for training

stem: A EU-based bank is subject to GDPR and wants to minimize personal data exposure in a Claude-powered RAG support assistant. Which design choice BEST embodies the GDPR principle of data minimization?
options: Redact or tokenize personal identifiers from documents before they are chunked, embedded, and sent as context
Increase the context window so the entire customer record is always available to the model
Store the full chat transcript indefinitely to support future model fine-tuning
Send all customer records to Claude and instruct the model in the system prompt to ignore any personal data
rationale: Data minimization means only the minimum necessary personal data should be collected and processed. Redacting or tokenizing identifiers before embedding and retrieval ensures PII is not unnecessarily transmitted or stored in vector indexes. Maximizing context, indefinite retention, and relying on a prompt instruction all increase, rather than minimize, personal-data exposure and processing, contradicting GDPR principles.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on embeddings and building RAG systems; GDPR data-minimization principle

stem: A U.S. federal agency requires that its generative-AI workload run in a FedRAMP-authorized environment. The team already uses AWS GovCloud. What is the most appropriate way to consume Claude while meeting this requirement?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock in an AWS region/environment covered by the required authorization boundary
Call the public Anthropic first-party API directly from GovCloud VPC endpoints
Self-host the Claude model weights inside the agency data center
Use the Message Batches API, since batch processing is inherently FedRAMP compliant
rationale: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock, which lets agencies inherit AWS's compliance authorizations (including GovCloud/FedRAMP boundaries) for the infrastructure layer. The first-party API is a separate environment and does not automatically fall within a customer's AWS authorization boundary. Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting. The Batches API is a throughput/cost feature and confers no compliance authorization by itself.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Claude on Amazon Bedrock

stem: An architect is designing audit logging for a regulated Claude application so that every model interaction can be reviewed during an audit. Which logging approach best supports non-repudiation and traceability without introducing new compliance risk?
options: Log a request ID, timestamp, model version, actor identity, and a hash or redacted reference to inputs/outputs in an append-only store
Log full raw prompts and completions, including any PII, to a mutable application log for maximum detail
Log only errors, since successful requests do not need an audit trail
Rely on the provider's internal logs and keep no application-side records
rationale: Effective audit logging captures who did what, when, and against which model version, in a tamper-evident (append-only) store, while minimizing sensitive-data exposure via hashing/redaction, balancing traceability with data-minimization duties. Logging raw PII to a mutable log creates its own breach and integrity risk. Logging only errors loses the audit trail for successful (and potentially misused) requests. Relying solely on provider logs leaves the customer unable to independently demonstrate compliance.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on API request IDs, model versioning, and observability best practices

stem: A product team wants to launch a consumer-facing Claude feature that lets users generate marketing copy. Legal asks how to ensure the deployment stays within Anthropic's Usage Policy. What is the most appropriate governance control to implement?
options: Combine acceptable-use guardrails in the system prompt with automated moderation and a documented process for reviewing prohibited-use categories
Add a single line to the system prompt saying 'follow all laws' and consider compliance complete
Assume any output Claude produces is automatically policy-compliant because of safety training
Disable all content filtering to avoid over-blocking legitimate marketing requests
rationale: Compliance with the Usage Policy is a shared responsibility: architects should layer prompt-level guardrails, automated moderation/classification, and an operational review process mapped to prohibited-use categories. A single 'follow all laws' instruction is not an enforceable control. Safety training reduces but does not eliminate policy-violating output, so it cannot be assumed compliant. Disabling all filtering removes the very controls needed to prevent misuse.
referenceText: Anthropic Usage Policy (Acceptable Use Policy)

stem: A multinational company must keep processing of customer data within a specific geographic region for data-residency compliance. They deploy Claude via Google Cloud Vertex AI. Which configuration decision most directly satisfies the data-residency requirement?
options: Selecting the Vertex AI region that is within the required jurisdiction when issuing requests to Claude
Setting a system-prompt instruction telling Claude to keep data in-region
Choosing a smaller Claude model, since smaller models process less data
Enabling prompt caching, which keeps all data in the caller's local memory
rationale: On Vertex AI, the region (endpoint/location) you call determines where processing occurs, so selecting an in-jurisdiction region is the concrete control that satisfies data residency. A system-prompt instruction cannot change where computation physically happens. Model size does not affect processing location. Prompt caching stores cached prompt prefixes provider-side, not in the caller's local memory, and does not by itself guarantee residency.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Claude on Google Vertex AI (regional endpoints)

stem: In a RAG pipeline for a legal-research tool, stakeholders are worried that Claude may fabricate case citations, creating regulatory and malpractice risk. Which platform capability most directly reduces this risk by grounding claims in the provided source documents?
options: Use the Citations feature so responses reference the specific source passages that support each claim
Raise the temperature so the model explores more sources before answering
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to write longer answers
Switch to a larger embedding dimension to improve retrieval recall only
rationale: Claude's Citations feature returns references to the exact source passages used, making claims verifiable and reducing unsupported/fabricated assertions, a key hallucination-risk control for regulated use. Higher temperature increases variability and hallucination risk. More max_tokens affects length, not grounding. Larger embedding dimensions may help retrieval but do not, by themselves, tie generated statements to sources the way citations do.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Citations

stem: A high-availability Claude service occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses under peak load. Which resilience pattern is the recommended way to handle these transient conditions?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, respecting the retry-after signal, up to a bounded number of attempts
Immediately retry in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Treat every 429 and 529 as a fatal error and surface it to the end user
Permanently switch all traffic to a different model on the first 429
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; the recommended approach is bounded retries with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring rate-limit/retry signals to avoid thundering-herd amplification. Tight-loop retries worsen overload. Treating transient errors as fatal harms availability unnecessarily. Permanently rerouting all traffic after a single 429 is an overreaction that ignores the transient nature of the signal and can mask capacity planning issues.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: errors, rate limits, and retry handling

stem: An architect is designing a degraded-mode strategy so a customer-support assistant stays available when the primary model is temporarily unavailable. Which fallback design is the most robust from a risk-management standpoint?
options: Cache the last successful response and return it for any request received during an outage
Fail closed and return an error for all requests until the primary model recovers
Define a documented fallback to an alternative Claude model (or provider endpoint) with tested prompts and clearly communicated capability differences
Fall back to a model whose prompt format and safety behavior have not been tested for this use case
rationale: A robust fallback is pre-defined, validated against the same prompts and evals, and its capability/behavior differences are documented so quality and safety are understood in degraded mode. Returning a stale cached response produces answers unrelated to the new request. Failing closed for all traffic sacrifices availability unnecessarily. Falling back to an untested model introduces unknown quality and safety risk, defeating the purpose of a controlled fallback.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: models overview and handling availability with alternative endpoints.

stem: Before deploying a Claude application into a regulated production environment, the risk committee requires evidence that the system was evaluated for safety and quality. Which practices should be part of a responsible pre-deployment evaluation? (Select 2.)
options: Build a representative evaluation set with graded test cases covering both typical and adversarial/edge inputs
Rely on a handful of manual spot checks by the developer on launch day
Define measurable success criteria and acceptance thresholds agreed with stakeholders before launch
Defer to Claude's built-in safety training and skip application-level test cases
Track average latency and cost as the primary launch gate, deferring correctness testing to post-launch monitoring
rationale: A defensible evaluation uses a representative, graded test set that includes adversarial/edge cases plus pre-agreed measurable thresholds so pass/fail is objective and auditable. Ad-hoc spot checks are neither reproducible nor representative. Model-level safety training does not remove the need for application-level evals. Gating only on latency and cost defers the correctness and safety signals that matter most for a regulated launch.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: develop and run evaluations (test and evaluate).

stem: An agentic Claude application uses the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to expose tools that can modify production records and send external emails. From a governance and safety perspective, which control is most important for these high-impact tools?
options: Grant the MCP server broad credentials so tool calls never fail due to missing permissions
Configure the model to auto-approve all tool calls to minimize user-facing latency
Rely on the model's own judgment to refuse unsafe tool invocations, without separate permission scoping
Require human-in-the-loop approval and scoped, least-privilege permissions for destructive or externally visible tool actions
rationale: High-impact tools (data mutation, outbound email) warrant human confirmation plus least-privilege, scoped credentials so an errant or manipulated action cannot cause unbounded harm. Broad credentials enlarge the blast radius. Auto-approving all calls removes the key safety checkpoint. Relying solely on the model's judgment provides no independent control when the model is manipulated (e.g., via prompt injection).
referenceText: Anthropic docs: tool use overview (permissions and human oversight for tools).

stem: An architect maintains a production application pinned to a specific Claude model version. Anthropic announces that this model is being deprecated with a retirement date. What is the most appropriate lifecycle-management response to communicate to stakeholders?
options: Take no action, since deprecated models continue to serve traffic indefinitely
Plan a migration: re-run evaluations against the successor model, schedule cutover before the retirement date, and communicate expected behavior changes
Immediately remove the feature from production on the day the deprecation is announced
Switch to the newest model in production right away without re-running evaluations
rationale: Deprecation requires proactive lifecycle management: validate the successor against your eval suite, plan cutover ahead of the retirement date, and communicate behavioral differences. Deprecated models are eventually retired, so inaction risks an outage. Pulling the feature immediately overreacts to an announcement with a future retirement date. Switching without re-evaluation risks undetected quality or safety regressions.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: model deprecations and lifecycle.

stem: During a vendor security review, a prospective customer's procurement team asks for evidence of Anthropic's security and compliance posture (e.g., SOC 2 reports, certifications). Where should the architect direct them to obtain this documentation?
options: The public model card, which includes the SOC 2 report as an appendix
Anthropic's Trust Center, which hosts compliance reports and certification details under appropriate access controls
The API reference documentation, which lists certifications alongside endpoint specifications
The published Usage Policy, which contains the third-party audit attestations
rationale: Compliance artifacts such as SOC 2 reports and certifications are made available through Anthropic's Trust Center, typically under access controls or NDA. Model cards describe model capabilities and risks, not audit reports. The API reference and Usage Policy are technical and legal documents that do not host audit evidence.
referenceText: Anthropic Trust Center.

stem: A Claude RAG assistant ingests documents from untrusted external sources (uploaded PDFs, web pages). The security team is concerned about indirect prompt injection embedded in retrieved content. Which mitigations meaningfully reduce this risk? (Select 2.)
options: Clearly delimit and label retrieved content as untrusted data, instructing the model to treat it as reference material rather than instructions
Constrain tool permissions so injected instructions cannot trigger high-impact actions without human approval
Raise the temperature so the model is less likely to comply with injected instructions
Rely on the model's safety training to detect and ignore injected instructions in retrieved text
Concatenate retrieved passages directly into the system prompt so they carry the same authority as your instructions
rationale: Indirect prompt injection is mitigated by structurally separating and labeling untrusted content so it is treated as data, and by least-privilege tool scoping plus human approval so any followed injection cannot cause high-impact actions. Temperature governs sampling randomness, not instruction-following susceptibility. No model reliably blocks all injection, so blind trust is unsafe. Placing retrieved text in the system prompt elevates the attacker's content to your own authority level, worsening the problem.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: tool use overview and mitigating prompt injection.

stem: A platform team manages Claude API access for dozens of internal applications. From a security-governance standpoint, which API-key management practice best limits blast radius if one application is compromised?
options: Share a single organization-wide API key across all applications to simplify rotation
Issue separate, scoped API keys per application/workspace and rotate them regularly with monitoring for anomalous usage
Embed a shared API key in each application's client-side code so keys stay with the app
Provision long-lived keys and avoid rotation to prevent deployment churn
rationale: Per-application scoped keys with regular rotation and usage monitoring contain the impact of any single compromise and enable precise revocation. A shared org-wide key means one leak compromises everything. Embedding keys in client-side code exposes them to end users. Never rotating leaves a compromised credential valid indefinitely.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: API overview, workspaces, and access management.

stem: An observability lead is instrumenting a production Claude service to detect quality regressions, cost anomalies, and safety incidents. Which signals are most valuable to capture for governance and operations? (Select 2.)
options: Per-request token usage (input/output, including cache read/write) and cost attribution by feature or tenant
Rates of stop_reason values, refusals, errors, and latency percentiles over time
Only the total number of API calls per month, with no per-request breakdown
A single quality score recorded by the developer at launch and left unchanged
Total storage consumed by application logs, used as a proxy for overall request volume
rationale: Token and cost attribution (including prompt-cache reads/writes) enables cost-anomaly detection and chargeback, while stop_reason distributions, refusal/error rates, and latency percentiles surface quality and safety regressions over time. A monthly call count lacks granularity to attribute or diagnose issues. A one-time launch score is not an ongoing signal. Log storage size is an indirect infrastructure metric, not a governance signal about model behavior.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: handling stop reasons, token counting, and prompt caching metrics.

stem: A moderation team wants defense-in-depth for a public Claude chatbot that could receive harmful requests. Which architecture best reflects layered safety rather than relying on a single control?
options: Rely exclusively on the model to refuse harmful requests, with no input or output checks
Screen inputs only, treating anything that passes input screening as safe to return
Combine input screening, model-level guardrails via system prompt, and output moderation, with logging and escalation for flagged cases
Screen outputs only, passing all inputs directly to the model regardless of content
rationale: Defense-in-depth layers independent controls (input screening, model-level guardrails, output moderation) plus logging and escalation, so no single failure is catastrophic. Relying only on the model, only on input screening, or only on output moderation each leaves a gap a novel or determined attack can exploit.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: content moderation and guardrails.

stem: A team processes 500,000 non-urgent document classifications nightly and wants to reduce cost while keeping the workload governable and auditable. Which approach aligns cost optimization with sound governance for this batch workload?
options: Send all 500,000 requests synchronously at once to finish as quickly as possible
Use the largest available model for every document regardless of task difficulty
Use the Message Batches API for the asynchronous jobs and retain per-batch request IDs and results for auditability
Disable logging during the batch run to reduce storage and speed up processing
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads at reduced cost, and retaining per-request IDs and results preserves auditability. Firing 500k synchronous requests risks rate limits with no cost benefit. Always using the largest model ignores right-sizing and inflates cost. Disabling logging removes the audit trail needed for governance and error investigation.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Message Batches API (batch processing).

stem: A risk officer asks how the organization should handle suspected misuse or abuse discovered in a production Claude application (e.g., users attempting prohibited activity). Which practice best establishes accountable incident handling?
options: Delete all related logs immediately to reduce the organization's liability
Maintain an incident-response process with monitoring, defined escalation paths, retained evidence, and reporting consistent with Anthropic's Usage Policy
Take no action unless Anthropic contacts the organization first
Publicly disclose the affected users' identities to deter future abuse
rationale: Accountable incident handling requires proactive monitoring, defined escalation, evidence retention, and reporting aligned with the Usage Policy so misuse is detected, contained, and documented. Deleting logs destroys evidence and can worsen legal exposure. Waiting for the provider is not a control. Publicly disclosing user identities creates privacy and legal violations rather than a legitimate deterrent.
referenceText: Anthropic Usage Policy and enforcement/reporting expectations.

stem: An intake application forwards free-text customer complaints to Claude for summarization. Legal review finds the complaints frequently contain national ID numbers that are not needed to produce a summary. Which design change best satisfies the data-minimization principle?
options: Send the full text unchanged and open a support ticket asking the provider to delete the identifiers after processing
Encrypt the request payload with a customer-managed key so the identifiers are protected in transit
Redact or strip the identifiers that the summarization task does not require before the text is sent to the API
Raise max_tokens so Claude can echo the identifiers and then omit them from the final summary
rationale: Data minimization means transmitting only the personal data actually needed for the purpose. Removing unneeded identifiers before the text leaves your boundary is the correct control. Post-hoc deletion still means the data was transmitted and processed, so minimization already failed. Encryption in transit protects confidentiality but does not reduce the data collected. Echoing identifiers through the model increases exposure rather than minimizing it.
referenceText: Data minimization requires limiting personal data to what is necessary; strip or redact unneeded identifiers before processing.

stem: A digital-health company intends to send protected health information (PHI) to Claude to draft clinical visit summaries. Which prerequisite must be in place to handle PHI in a HIPAA-aligned manner?
options: Rely on the default commercial terms, since API traffic is already encrypted with TLS
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and enable available data-handling controls, such as zero data retention
Set a custom HTTP header of hipaa=true on every Messages API request
Proceed on the standard tier after obtaining patient consent, since PHI cannot be anonymized
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement with any vendor that processes PHI, combined with appropriate safeguards such as zero data retention where offered. TLS encryption is necessary but not sufficient and creates no contractual relationship. There is no hipaa header that establishes compliance. Patient consent does not substitute for a BAA and appropriate technical/contractual controls.
referenceText: Handling PHI with a vendor requires a signed BAA plus appropriate safeguards; see the provider's compliance/trust program.

stem: A US federal agency must run a Claude-based workload inside a FedRAMP-authorized boundary. Which approach best satisfies that requirement?
options: Call the public Anthropic API directly and layer the agency's own encryption on top
Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock in an environment carrying the required FedRAMP authorization, inheriting the platform's compliance boundary
Self-host the model weights in the agency data center to avoid any cloud dependency
Use the consumer Claude app and restrict access to agency-issued laptops
rationale: Consuming Claude via a cloud platform such as Amazon Bedrock lets the workload inherit that platform's FedRAMP authorization boundary, which is how federal agencies typically meet the requirement. The public API is not the same authorized boundary. Anthropic does not distribute model weights for self-hosting. The consumer app is not designed for FedRAMP-authorized agency workloads.
referenceText: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock, letting workloads run within the cloud platform's compliance boundary.

stem: A regulated deployment must keep an audit trail that links every Claude response back to who requested it and when. Which practice best supports this requirement?
options: Store the full prompt and response only inside the provider's usage dashboard
Enable Claude's built-in per-user audit ledger by passing an audit=true request parameter
Depend on the TLS access logs emitted by your load balancer
Record the API response request_id alongside the authenticated user, timestamp, model, and token usage in your own append-only audit store
rationale: Each API response includes a request_id that you can persist together with the authenticated principal, timestamp, and model/usage metadata to build an auditable trail you control. The provider dashboard is not an audit store whose retention you own. There is no audit=true parameter. Load-balancer TLS logs record connections, not which user produced which model output.
referenceText: API responses carry a request_id you can log with your own user, timestamp, and usage metadata for auditing.

stem: A product team asks who is responsible for ensuring an application built on Claude complies with Anthropic's Usage Policy. Which statement is correct?
options: The provider automatically blocks every policy-violating use, so application-level controls are unnecessary
The Usage Policy applies only to consumer-facing products, not to internal tools
The deploying organization must enforce acceptable use in its own application, implementing safeguards appropriate to its use case
The Usage Policy governs only training data and does not apply to API applications
rationale: The deploying organization is accountable for how it uses the model and must build safeguards suited to its use case in line with the Usage Policy. Provider-side safety measures exist but do not remove the deployer's obligation. The policy applies whether a product is internal or consumer-facing, and it governs applications built on the API, not just training data.
referenceText: Applications built on Claude must comply with the Usage Policy; deployers are responsible for enforcing acceptable use.

stem: A support assistant sends the same 8,000-token policy-and-tone system prompt on every request, and the team wants to cut both cost and time-to-first-token without changing behavior. Which technique is most appropriate?
options: Move the policy text into every user message so it compresses better
Apply prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint on the stable prefix so repeated tokens are served from cache at a reduced rate
Lower max_tokens on each request to shorten processing time
Increase temperature so the model spends fewer tokens deliberating
rationale: Prompt caching marks a stable prefix so its tokens are reused across requests, lowering cost and latency for the repeated content without changing behavior. Relocating the text to user messages does not reduce token processing. Lowering max_tokens caps output length, not the cost of the large repeated prefix. Temperature affects sampling, not token count or caching.
referenceText: Prompt caching reuses a stable prompt prefix across requests, reducing cost and latency for repeated content.

stem: A nightly pipeline must classify 500,000 documents. There is no real-time requirement and cost is the primary constraint. Which approach fits best?
options: Fan out 500,000 concurrent synchronous Messages requests to finish as fast as possible
Send one enormous request containing all 500,000 documents to save on overhead
Submit the work through the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing at a lower per-token cost
Run the job interactively and retry any rate-limited request without backoff
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, non-time-sensitive workloads and offers reduced cost for asynchronous processing. Massive synchronous fan-out risks rate limits and higher cost. A single giant request exceeds context limits and is not how batching works. Interactive retries without backoff worsen throttling.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large asynchronous workloads at reduced cost for non-time-sensitive jobs.

stem: A high-volume ticket-routing feature has a strict latency and cost budget and currently uses a large model. What is the most defensible right-sizing decision?
options: Always use the largest available model because it maximizes accuracy in every case
Pick the cheapest model without evaluation because routing is a simple task
Evaluate a smaller, faster model against the required accuracy bar and adopt it if it meets the target
Alternate randomly between models per request to average out cost and quality
rationale: Right-sizing means selecting the smallest, cheapest model that still clears your measured quality bar, verified with an evaluation. Defaulting to the largest model wastes cost and latency. Choosing the cheapest without evaluation risks unacceptable accuracy. Randomly alternating produces inconsistent quality and is not a principled selection.
referenceText: Choose the model that meets your task's quality bar at the best cost/latency; validate with evaluation.

stem: Under bursty traffic your service intermittently receives HTTP 429 responses from the API. Which client behavior is the correct resilient response?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request eventually succeeds
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when present
Treat 429 as fatal and surface an error to the user without retrying
Permanently lower your concurrency to a single request at a time
rationale: A 429 indicates rate limiting; the resilient pattern is exponential backoff with jitter while respecting any retry-after guidance. Tight-loop retries amplify the overload. Treating 429 as fatal discards a transient, retryable condition. Dropping to single concurrency permanently needlessly cripples throughput instead of adapting to bursts.
referenceText: Rate-limit (429) responses should be retried with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring retry-after.

stem: You are hardening a customer-facing Claude integration against provider-side incidents and latency spikes. Which two patterns most improve resilience? (Select 2.)
options: Consolidate all traffic onto a single region and endpoint so failures are easier to trace
Configure sensible request timeouts and retry transient failures with exponential backoff and jitter
Retry every failed request immediately with no delay, including non-idempotent writes
Define a fallback path, such as an alternate model or a cached/deterministic response, when the primary call fails or times out
Remove all client-side timeouts so long-running requests are never cut short prematurely
rationale: Timeouts plus backoff-with-jitter retries prevent thread exhaustion and thundering-herd retries during transient failures, and a defined fallback keeps the experience degraded-but-working when the primary path is down. Consolidating onto a single dependency creates a single point of failure. Immediate no-backoff retries of non-idempotent writes can duplicate side effects and amplify load. Removing timeouts lets slow calls pile up and exhaust connection pools.
referenceText: Resilient designs use timeouts, backoff-with-jitter retries, and fallback paths for provider errors and latency spikes.

stem: A RAG-based policy assistant must let reviewers verify that each answer is grounded in source documents and reduce the risk of unsupported claims. Which capability most directly addresses this?
options: Raise temperature so the model produces more varied and creative answers
Use the Citations feature so Claude attributes each statement to the specific source passages it relied on
Increase max_tokens so answers are longer and include more supporting detail
Disable retrieval and rely on the model's parametric knowledge for consistency
rationale: Citations tie generated statements to the exact source passages, giving reviewers an auditable grounding and reducing unsupported claims. Higher temperature increases variability and hallucination risk. More tokens make answers longer without establishing grounding. Removing retrieval discards the authoritative sources the assistant is supposed to cite.
referenceText: The Citations feature grounds Claude's statements in provided source documents for verifiable, auditable answers.

stem: In a multi-tenant RAG system, a user retrieved a chunk containing another tenant's confidential data. What is the correct root-cause fix?
options: Reduce the number of retrieved chunks (top_k) so cross-tenant leakage is less likely
Instruct Claude in the system prompt to ignore any data belonging to other tenants
Enforce tenant-scoped access control and metadata filtering at retrieval time so only that tenant's authorized chunks are candidates
Re-embed all documents with a higher-dimensional embedding model
rationale: Cross-tenant leakage is an authorization failure in the retrieval layer; the fix is enforcing tenant isolation via access control and metadata filters so unauthorized chunks are never candidates. Lowering top_k only changes the probability of leakage, not the boundary. A prompt instruction cannot serve as access control once the data has already been surfaced to the model. Different embeddings change relevance ranking but do not enforce isolation.
referenceText: Retrieval in multi-tenant systems must enforce access control and metadata filtering so only authorized chunks are returned.

stem: You are connecting Claude to internal systems through Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Which control most reduces the risk of over-privileged or unintended tool actions?
options: Grant each MCP server broad administrative credentials to avoid permission errors during rollout
Apply least-privilege scoping to each MCP server and require human approval before high-impact actions execute
Expose all available tools to the model so it can always choose the most capable one
Disable logging on MCP calls to reduce overhead in production
rationale: Least-privilege scoping limits the blast radius of any single tool, and human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions guards against unintended or injected commands. Broad admin credentials maximize damage from any error or injection. Exposing every tool enlarges the attack surface. Disabling logging removes the observability needed to detect and investigate misuse.
referenceText: MCP integrations should follow least privilege and gate high-impact tool actions behind human approval.

stem: An agent fetches third-party web pages and passes their content to Claude, which can also call internal tools. A fetched page contains hidden text instructing the model to exfiltrate data. What is the most effective mitigation for this indirect prompt injection?
options: Concatenate the fetched page text directly into the system prompt so the model treats it as authoritative context
Trust the fetched content because your own system retrieved it from a known URL
Deliver third-party content only inside tool_result blocks marked as untrusted, keep it separate from your instructions, and scope tool permissions to least privilege
Raise the model's temperature so it is less likely to follow embedded instructions
rationale: The recommended defense is to treat retrieved/tool content as untrusted data, place it in tool_result blocks distinct from trusted instructions, state an untrusted-content policy, and limit tool access so a successful injection can accomplish little. Trusting content because you fetched it ignores that the source is attacker-controlled. Putting it in the system prompt elevates untrusted text to authoritative instructions. Temperature does not address injection.
referenceText: Put untrusted third-party content in tool_result blocks, keep it separate from instructions, and apply least-privilege tool access.

stem: Before launching a customer-facing assistant, a team wants to systematically catch harmful or policy-violating outputs and prevent regressions as prompts change. Which practice best achieves this?
options: Manually spot-check a handful of outputs once, on launch day
Rely on the model's built-in safety training and skip application-level testing
Wait for user-reported incidents in production, then patch prompts reactively
Build an evaluation suite with representative and adversarial test cases graded against defined criteria, and run it before and after every change
rationale: A repeatable evaluation suite with adversarial cases and explicit grading criteria detects harmful outputs and guards against regressions whenever prompts or models change. One-time spot checks do not cover adversarial inputs or future changes. Relying solely on built-in safety ignores application-specific risks. Purely reactive patching exposes users to harm before it is caught.
referenceText: Develop empirical tests with representative and adversarial cases and grade outputs against defined criteria before and after changes.

stem: A governance-minded team is defining what to monitor for a production Claude service to support SLOs and cost accountability. Which three signals are most relevant? (Select 3.)
options: Per-request token usage and the resulting cost
The provider's published benchmark scores, re-polled on a fixed schedule
Latency, including time-to-first-token for streamed responses
Error and refusal rates over time
Per-request GPU utilization inside the provider's data centers
rationale: Token usage/cost, latency including time-to-first-token, and error/refusal rates are the operational and cost signals that let a team enforce SLOs and control spend. The provider's benchmark scores are static marketing/quality references, not live operational telemetry for your service. Provider-side GPU utilization is neither observable to you nor relevant to your SLOs.
referenceText: Monitor latency (including time-to-first-token), error/refusal rates, and token usage/cost for production governance.

stem: A compliance requirement states that the model provider must not retain the organization's prompts or outputs after a response is returned. Which option correctly addresses this?
options: Add store=false to the request body to disable retention per call
Arrange zero data retention terms with the provider so inputs and outputs are not stored after the response is delivered
Delete your own application logs nightly, which also purges the provider's stored copies
Encrypt the payload in transit, which prevents the provider from storing it
rationale: Provider-side retention is governed by contractual data-handling terms; a zero data retention arrangement ensures inputs/outputs are not stored after processing. There is no store=false request field that controls provider retention. Deleting your own logs has no effect on provider-side storage. Transit encryption protects data in motion but does not determine retention.
referenceText: Provider data retention is governed by contractual terms; zero data retention agreements prevent storage after processing.

stem: An EU-based customer requires that its data be processed within an EU region to meet data-residency obligations while using Claude. What is the appropriate architecture?
options: Call the global public API and add a region=eu header to pin processing to Europe
Store the results in an EU database after processing, which satisfies residency for the whole flow
Deploy Claude through a cloud platform such as Google Vertex AI or Amazon Bedrock in an EU region that satisfies the residency requirement
Rotate requests across all global endpoints so no single non-EU region dominates
rationale: Consuming Claude via a cloud platform lets you select an EU region so inference occurs where residency rules require. There is no region header on the public API that guarantees processing location. Storing outputs in the EU does not control where the inference itself happened. Rotating across global endpoints sends data outside the EU, violating residency.
referenceText: Claude is available through Google Vertex AI and Amazon Bedrock, letting you choose a region to meet data-residency needs.

stem: The provider announces that a model version your production system depends on will be retired on a set date. Which lifecycle-management practice is correct?
options: Pin to the deprecated model version indefinitely so behavior never changes
Track the deprecation notice, evaluate the successor model against your existing eval suite, and migrate before the retirement date
Switch immediately to the newest model without running your evaluations to save time
Wait until the model is fully retired and errors appear in production, then react
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means monitoring deprecation notices, validating the successor against your evaluation suite to catch behavior changes, and migrating ahead of the retirement date. Pinning indefinitely becomes impossible once the model is retired. Switching without evaluation risks silent quality regressions. Waiting for production errors guarantees an outage.
referenceText: Track model deprecation timelines and migrate to successor models with evaluation before the retirement date.

stem: A community platform needs to block clearly disallowed user submissions before they reach a downstream workflow that takes action. Which design is most appropriate?
options: Let the main workflow run and rely on a human to review every action after it is taken
Increase the main model's temperature so unsafe requests are answered less coherently
Add a lightweight input-classification/moderation step, such as a fast Claude-based content filter, ahead of the main workflow
Trust the primary model to refuse everything harmful, so no separate screening is needed
rationale: A dedicated moderation/classification stage in front of the workflow screens inputs and blocks disallowed content before any action is taken, which is the recommended pattern. Post-hoc human review of every action does not prevent the harmful action and does not scale. Temperature is unrelated to safety screening. Relying only on the primary model's refusals provides no defense-in-depth for downstream actions.
referenceText: Use a fast Claude-based classifier as a moderation stage to screen user inputs before downstream processing.

stem: A hospital network is building a clinical-note summarization feature on the Claude API. Prompts will contain protected health information (PHI), and the deployment must satisfy HIPAA. As the solution architect, which two governance controls are essential before PHI is processed? (Select 2.)
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and provision a HIPAA-enabled organization so PHI is processed only under contractual coverage with encryption, access controls, and audit logging, and retained data is not used to train models.
Rely on Claude's built-in safety guardrails to automatically detect and strip all PHI from prompts before inference.
Apply data minimization by sending only the minimum-necessary PHI in each prompt, and enforce role-based access controls with audit logging over every request.
Persist complete prompts and responses containing PHI indefinitely in plaintext application logs to guarantee a full audit trail.
Fine-tune a dedicated Claude model on the network's historical PHI so that patient data never leaves an internal training pipeline.
rationale: HIPAA readiness for a covered entity rests on two architect-controlled pillars. The BAA plus a HIPAA-enabled organization is the contractual and technical mechanism that permits a business associate to handle PHI, applying encryption, access controls, and audit logging across the data lifecycle, with retained data not used for training without express permission (HIPAA readiness is an alternative to zero data retention, not combined with it). Data minimization (the minimum-necessary standard) plus role-based access and audit logging are core HIPAA safeguards that are the customer's responsibility. Relying on safety guardrails to strip PHI is wrong: those features are not a certified de-identification control. Retaining PHI indefinitely in plaintext logs violates minimization and expands breach exposure. Fine-tuning on PHI is not a required control and does not establish the required agreements or safeguards.
referenceText: Anthropic's data-usage documentation describes HIPAA readiness: a signed BAA plus a HIPAA-enabled organization applies encryption, access controls, and audit logging that protect PHI across its lifecycle, as an alternative to ZDR, with retained data not used for training without permission. The minimum-necessary standard and access/audit controls are customer-implemented HIPAA safeguards.

stem: An EU-based SaaS company logs every Claude API prompt and response into an observability platform with a three-year retention window. Some logged prompts contain customer personal data. A customer submits a GDPR right-to-erasure request. Which design change best aligns the logging pipeline with GDPR data-minimization and erasure obligations?
options: Retain all logs for the full three years regardless of erasure requests, because immutable audit trails always take precedence over individual deletion rights
Minimize the personal data captured in logs, tag records with a data-subject identifier, and set retention limits so specific records can be located and deleted when an erasure request arrives
Stop logging prompts and responses entirely so no personal data is ever stored, eliminating any need for retention or deletion controls
Rely on the model provider to erase the customer's data from Claude's memory, since the model retains past prompts and can be instructed to forget them
rationale: GDPR requires data minimization, purpose-limited retention, and the ability to locate and delete a specific data subject's personal data on request; tagging records to a subject identifier with retention limits makes both observability and erasure feasible. The claim that audit trails 'always' override erasure is false, as controllers must balance legal-retention bases against erasure rights. Eliminating all logging is over-engineered and destroys legitimate observability; minimization means collecting only what is necessary, not nothing. The Claude API is stateless and does not 'remember' prior prompts, so erasure must be handled in the customer's own storage systems.
referenceText: Anthropic acts as a data processor under a Data Processing Addendum. The right to erasure, data minimization, and storage-limitation obligations are GDPR principles the data controller must implement in its own logging and retention design, since the Claude API does not retain prompts as model state.

stem: A production application is pinned to a specific Claude model snapshot. Anthropic publishes a deprecation notice for that snapshot with a retirement date six months out. As the architect, what is the most responsible lifecycle action to communicate and execute with stakeholders?
options: Wait until the retirement date, then swap the model ID in production, since behavior is guaranteed identical across snapshots
Immediately hard-pin to the newest model without evaluation, because newer snapshots are always strictly better
Schedule a migration plan that re-runs the regression eval suite against the successor model, communicates a cutover timeline to stakeholders, and completes the swap before the retirement date
Remove the explicit model ID so requests float to whatever the API defaults to, eliminating future migration work
rationale: The correct approach treats deprecation as a planned lifecycle event: validate the successor with the existing eval suite, communicate a timeline, and cut over before retirement. Waiting until the deadline risks an outage and assumes identical behavior, which is not guaranteed. Blindly pinning the newest model without evaluation ignores possible regressions. Dropping the model ID to float on defaults sacrifices reproducibility and can cause silent behavior changes at any time.
referenceText: Anthropic provides deprecation notices and retirement dates so teams can plan migrations; pin explicit model versions and test before migrating.

stem: A finance stakeholder asks why the monthly Claude bill dropped 40% after your team enabled prompt caching on a workload that repeatedly sends a large, static system prompt. Which explanation is accurate?
options: Prompt caching makes all input and output tokens free after the first request
Cache reads are billed at a reduced rate compared with processing the same input tokens uncached, so repeated large static prefixes cost less on subsequent calls
Prompt caching reduces cost by automatically switching the request to a smaller model
Prompt caching only reduces latency and has no effect on token billing
rationale: Cache reads (hits) are billed at a fraction of the normal input-token price, so a large repeated static prefix becomes much cheaper on subsequent calls; there is also a one-time cache-write premium. Caching does not make tokens free, does not change the model, and affects both latency and cost, not latency alone.
referenceText: Cache read tokens are billed at a reduced rate relative to base input tokens; cache writes carry a one-time premium.

stem: A stakeholder wants to process 2 million overnight classification jobs at the lowest cost, and the results are not needed in real time. Which Claude capability should the architect propose, and what expectation must be set?
options: The standard synchronous Messages API, promising sub-second latency per request at the batch discount
The Message Batches API, setting the expectation that results are returned asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at a discounted per-token rate
Prompt caching alone, promising the full batch completes in real time at no token cost
Streaming responses, promising the batch discount applies to every streamed token
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large asynchronous, latency-tolerant workloads and offers a substantial discount, with results typically completing within 24 hours. Synchronous Messages, prompt caching, and streaming do not provide the batch discount and do not fit a not-real-time bulk workload the way batching does.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a reduced cost, with batches completing within 24 hours.

stem: You are presenting a model right-sizing recommendation to stakeholders for a new feature. Which three factors are legitimate inputs to a defensible model-selection decision? (Select 3.)
options: The measured accuracy of each candidate model on a task-specific evaluation set
The per-request latency and cost budget the feature must meet
The complexity of the reasoning the task actually requires
Always selecting the largest available model to eliminate capability risk
The vendor's overall market share, independent of task fit
rationale: Right-sizing balances task-specific eval accuracy, latency/cost constraints, and the intrinsic complexity of the task. Defaulting to the largest model in all cases wastes cost and latency where a smaller model suffices. Choosing by vendor market share ignores your own requirements, data, and measured performance.
referenceText: Choose a model by balancing capability, speed, and cost against the requirements of your specific task, validated with your own evaluations.

stem: Before a product launch, stakeholders ask how you will know the Claude feature is good enough to ship. What is the strongest lifecycle practice to agree on with them first?
options: Ship first and rely on user complaints as the primary evaluation signal
Judge quality informally by having one engineer read a handful of outputs the morning of launch
Assume quality is acceptable because the prompt reads well to the author
Define measurable success criteria and build a representative evaluation set with graded cases before development, then gate the launch on hitting the target
rationale: Agreeing on measurable success criteria and an eval set up front turns 'good enough' into an objective, stakeholder-approved gate. Waiting for complaints, spot-reading a few outputs, or trusting author intuition are unrepresentative and non-repeatable, and expose the launch to undetected regressions.
referenceText: Define success criteria that are measurable and build evaluations that reflect real-world usage before iterating on prompts.

stem: An SRE stakeholder wants production observability for your Claude integration so incidents can be diagnosed quickly. Which three signals are most valuable to capture and monitor? (Select 3.)
options: Latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per model and endpoint
Error and rate-limit response rates over time
Token usage per request broken down by input, output, and cache hits
The full request and response bodies logged verbatim on every call by default
The raw API key included in each log line for request attribution
rationale: Latency percentiles, error/rate-limit rates, and token breakdowns (including cache hits) are the core operational signals for diagnosing latency, reliability, and cost issues. Logging full prompt and response bodies by default is a privacy and cost liability rather than an observability requirement, and logging raw API keys is a serious credential-leak security violation, not a traceability practice.
referenceText: Monitor latency, error rates, and token usage; rate-limit and error headers help diagnose and back off appropriately.

stem: A healthcare stakeholder must confirm the deployment can handle protected health information (PHI) under HIPAA. What is the correct governance step before sending PHI to Claude?
options: Rely on TLS encryption in transit alone, since encryption satisfies all HIPAA obligations
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and confirm the specific service/deployment is in scope before transmitting PHI
Send PHI immediately because Anthropic's models never retain any input data under any configuration
Ask each patient to individually approve the API calls at request time
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed BAA covering the specific in-scope service before PHI is processed by a business associate. Encryption in transit is necessary but not sufficient. A blanket claim about zero retention is not a substitute for a BAA and contractual scoping. Per-request patient approval is not the mechanism HIPAA compliance relies on.
referenceText: Customers handling PHI should execute a Business Associate Agreement and use services within the agreed compliance scope.

stem: A privacy officer requires that a production RAG pipeline honor GDPR data-minimization principles. Which two design choices most directly support that requirement? (Select 2.)
options: Retrieve and pass only the minimum document chunks relevant to the user's query rather than the entire corpus
Redact or exclude personal-data fields that are not needed to answer the query before sending context to the model
Embed and index every document in full so any personal data is always available for future retrieval
Attach the user's complete account history to every request to give the model maximum context
Store raw prompts and retrieved context indefinitely so answers can always be re-derived
rationale: Data minimization means processing only the personal data necessary for the purpose. Retrieving just the relevant chunks and redacting unneeded personal fields both limit exposure. Indexing full documents with unused personal data, attaching entire histories to every request, and retaining raw prompts indefinitely all maximize rather than minimize processed personal data, contradicting GDPR.
referenceText: Send only the context needed to answer the query; minimize the personal data included in prompts and retrieval.

stem: A compliance stakeholder needs an auditable record of who created API keys and changed workspace settings across your organization's Anthropic account. Which capability should the architect point them to?
options: The Admin API, which programmatically exposes organization members, workspaces, and API key management for governance and auditing
The Usage and Cost API, which reports token consumption and spend broken down by workspace over time
The Messages API response metadata, which records the caller identity attached to each request
The Console billing export, which lists configuration changes alongside monthly invoices
rationale: The Admin API is the governance surface for managing and auditing members, workspaces, and API keys at the organization level. The Usage and Cost API reports spend, not administrative actions; Messages responses carry usage and model metadata, not who provisioned keys; and the billing export itemizes cost, not configuration-change history.
referenceText: The Admin API lets organizations programmatically manage workspaces, members, and API keys for governance and auditing.

stem: You are designing a resilient client for the Claude API and must document the retry and failure-handling strategy for stakeholders. Which three practices belong in the design? (Select 3.)
options: Retry transient 429 and 5xx responses using exponential backoff with jitter
Set sensible request timeouts and surface a graceful fallback when a call exceeds them
Honor the retry-after guidance from rate-limit responses rather than retrying in a tight loop
Retry 4xx validation errors with the same request body, since transient network issues usually cause them
Remove client-side timeouts on streaming requests so partial responses are never cut off
rationale: Exponential backoff with jitter, sensible timeouts with graceful fallback, and honoring retry-after are standard resilience patterns for transient failures. Deterministic 4xx validation errors will not succeed on retry with the same payload and waste quota, and removing timeouts lets requests hang indefinitely, harming reliability.
referenceText: Handle 429 and 5xx errors with retries and exponential backoff; respect retry-after and avoid retrying non-retryable 4xx errors.

stem: Your service has a hard SLA and stakeholders want continuity if the primary model is temporarily unavailable or rate-limited. Which fallback design best balances resilience with quality expectations?
options: Define a documented fallback to an alternate model or region, validate it against the eval suite, and communicate any expected quality or latency differences to stakeholders
Fail over automatically to a smaller, cheaper model without re-running evals, since any Claude model preserves output quality
Retry the primary model continuously without backoff until it recovers, holding the request open to avoid ever invoking a fallback
Queue every failed request for manual reprocessing later and return a placeholder to the caller in the meantime
rationale: A validated, documented fallback path with communicated trade-offs preserves availability while managing quality expectations honestly. Failing over without evals assumes quality parity that may not hold; unbounded retries without backoff blow the latency budget and can worsen rate limiting; and manual reprocessing with placeholders breaks the SLA and degrades the user experience.
referenceText: Design for graceful degradation with validated fallbacks; Claude is available across the API, Bedrock, and Vertex AI for redundancy.

stem: A procurement stakeholder asks whether to consume Claude via the Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, or Google Vertex AI. Which two statements about this deployment choice are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Bedrock and Vertex let organizations consume Claude within their existing AWS or Google Cloud accounts, billing, and IAM governance controls
Model IDs and some regional availability differ across the Anthropic API, Bedrock, and Vertex, so integration code and configuration must be platform-aware
Bedrock and Vertex require rewriting the Messages request and response format from scratch because they are incompatible with the Anthropic SDK
Only the first-party Anthropic API can access Claude models; Bedrock and Vertex expose other vendors' models instead
rationale: Bedrock and Vertex let teams use Claude within their existing cloud account, billing, and governance, and model identifiers and regional availability differ by platform, so configuration must be platform-aware. The message format is largely consistent across platforms with SDK support, and both Bedrock and Vertex do expose Claude models, so the last two options are false.
referenceText: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI, using platform-specific model identifiers and regions within your cloud account.

stem: A non-technical stakeholder asks why your team adopted the Model Context Protocol (MCP) instead of writing bespoke integrations for each data source and tool. What is the clearest, accurate justification?
options: MCP is an open standard that lets models connect to tools and data sources through a common interface, reducing per-integration custom glue and improving reuse
MCP is Anthropic's managed connector marketplace that hosts every integration on Anthropic's own servers
MCP is a fine-tuning format that embeds tool schemas directly into the model weights
MCP is a client library that automatically writes the integration code for any API you point it at
rationale: MCP is an open standard providing a common interface between models and external tools and data, cutting bespoke integration work and enabling reuse across clients and servers. It is not a hosted marketplace, not a fine-tuning or weight-embedding format, and does not auto-generate integration code.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard for connecting AI applications to external tools and data through a common interface.

stem: Business stakeholders in a regulated domain worry that RAG answers cannot be trusted because sources are unclear. Which Claude capability most directly addresses this concern?
options: The Citations feature, which grounds responses in provided documents and returns references to the exact source passages, improving verifiability and auditability
Prompt caching, which stores validated source passages so repeated answers remain accurate
Extended thinking, which exposes the reasoning steps that led to each claim in the answer
Structured outputs, which force the model to emit a citation field for every sentence
rationale: Citations grounds responses in supplied documents and returns pointers to the specific source passages, giving stakeholders verifiable, auditable answers. Prompt caching is a cost/latency feature with no attribution; extended thinking surfaces reasoning but not document sources; and structured outputs constrain format without verifying or linking to sources.
referenceText: Citations enables Claude to ground its answers in source documents and provide references to the specific passages used.

stem: Your team edits the production system prompt frequently, and a stakeholder is concerned about undetected quality regressions from these edits. What is the strongest safeguard to institute?
options: Version-control every prompt change and run the regression eval suite in CI before promoting a new prompt to production
Require a second engineer to manually review each prompt diff before it is applied in production
Keep a changelog of prompt edits and spot-check a sample of outputs weekly after they ship
Gate prompt changes behind a feature flag and roll back only if error rates spike after release
rationale: Treating prompts as versioned artifacts gated by an automated eval suite in CI catches regressions before release and provides an auditable change history. Manual diff review does not measure output quality; weekly spot-checks let regressions reach users first; and rolling back only on error-rate spikes misses silent quality regressions that do not raise errors.
referenceText: Iterate on prompts against a held-out evaluation set and track changes so regressions are caught before deployment.

stem: A finance stakeholder wants a recurring, itemized view of Claude spend by workspace to attribute cost to teams. Which approach should the architect recommend?
options: Use the Usage and Cost API to programmatically pull usage and cost data broken down by workspace for reporting
Sum the input and output token fields returned in each Messages API response and price them per team
Provision a separate API key per team and estimate cost from each key's request count
Export the monthly Console invoice, which is itemized by individual user rather than by workspace
rationale: The Usage and Cost API exposes usage and cost data that can be broken down by workspace, enabling accurate chargeback reporting. Aggregating per-response token fields is error-prone and misses pricing tiers; request counts ignore variable token usage; and the invoice is not the right granularity or source for automated per-workspace reporting.
referenceText: The Usage and Cost API provides programmatic access to token usage and cost data, filterable by workspace and model.

stem: A Claude-powered service has a production incident with elevated error rates. What belongs in effective stakeholder incident communication during and after the event? (Select 3.)
options: A timely status update describing user-facing impact and the current mitigation
An estimated time to resolution or next-update time, revised as understanding improves
A post-incident review capturing root cause and preventive actions
A full technical root-cause analysis broadcast to all stakeholders before the incident is mitigated
A committed permanent-fix date issued in the first update, before the cause is understood
rationale: Effective incident communication provides timely impact and mitigation updates, an ETA or next-update commitment, and a blameless post-incident review with preventive actions. Broadcasting a full root-cause analysis before mitigation delays action and can be inaccurate, and committing to a fix date before the cause is known sets expectations that are likely to be missed.
referenceText: Communicate impact, mitigation, and expected next updates during incidents, and follow up with a root-cause review and preventive actions.

stem: During a RAG design review, a stakeholder challenges your chunking strategy. Which justification reflects sound, documentable chunking practice?
options: Chunk sizes and overlap were chosen to preserve semantic coherence and fit retrieval and context constraints, and were validated by measuring retrieval quality on an eval set
Chunk sizes were maximized to fill the context window on every query so no relevant passage is ever omitted
Documents were split on a fixed token count with no overlap so every unit is exactly the same size
Chunk boundaries follow the embedding model's default and were left untuned, since retrieval quality depends only on the model
rationale: Good chunking preserves semantic units, respects retrieval and context-window limits, and is validated empirically with retrieval-quality metrics. Filling the whole context window per query defeats retrieval and inflates cost; fixed-size splits with no overlap sever passages that span boundaries; and leaving boundaries untuned ignores that chunking, not just the embedding model, drives retrieval quality.
referenceText: Chunk documents to preserve semantic meaning and fit context limits, and validate retrieval quality empirically.

stem: A stakeholder asks how much advance warning your team will have before a model you depend on stops working, so they can plan budget and engineering time. What is the accurate lifecycle expectation to communicate?
options: Anthropic publishes deprecation notices with retirement dates in advance, so the team tracks these notices to plan migrations before retirement
Anthropic guarantees a minimum of five years of support for every released model, so no migration is needed within that window
Deprecation is announced only after a model already returns errors, so migration is always reactive
Snapshot model IDs are retired, but the corresponding alias keeps serving the old weights indefinitely
rationale: Anthropic publishes deprecation and retirement dates in advance, and tracking them lets teams plan migrations proactively. There is no blanket multi-year support guarantee, deprecation is announced before models return retirement errors, and aliases do not preserve retired weights indefinitely.
referenceText: Deprecated models have published retirement dates; monitor deprecation notices to plan and complete migrations in advance.

stem: For a high-visibility launch, stakeholders want to limit blast radius if a new Claude feature underperforms in production. Which rollout approach best fits lifecycle risk management?
options: A phased canary rollout that exposes a small traffic percentage first, monitors quality and error metrics against agreed thresholds, and expands only when gates pass
A full cutover on day one paired with a documented rollback plan so any issue can be reverted immediately after launch
A blue-green deployment that shifts all traffic at once but keeps the previous version warm as a standby
An internal dogfooding period with employees only, followed by a direct 100% public launch
rationale: A canary rollout with monitored gates limits blast radius and gives stakeholders objective go/no-go signals before full exposure. A same-day full cutover, even with rollback, exposes all users to a regression before it is caught; blue-green shifts all production traffic at once and shares that risk; and internal dogfooding followed by a full public launch skips graded exposure to real customer traffic.
referenceText: Roll out changes gradually and monitor evaluation and operational metrics before expanding to all traffic.

stem: An architect receives a notice that a Claude model the company relies on in production has entered a deprecation period with a scheduled retirement date. Which action best reflects sound lifecycle management when communicating this to engineering and business stakeholders?
options: Immediately cut over all production traffic to the successor model so the migration is finished well before retirement
Publish a migration plan with the retirement date, the recommended successor model, and a regression-testing window before cutover
Wait until Anthropic returns retirement errors, then swap the model ID in production as a hotfix
Communicate that behavior is frozen during deprecation, so the successor needs no re-evaluation before cutover
rationale: The deprecation period provides a defined notice window and a recommended replacement, so the right response is a communicated migration plan that dates the cutover and reserves time to re-run evaluations against the successor. An immediate unvalidated cutover forfeits the testing window; waiting for retirement errors turns a planned migration into an incident; and claiming no re-evaluation is needed is false because successor models can differ in behavior.
referenceText: Anthropic model deprecations policy and lifecycle stages.

stem: Before rolling a newly released Claude model into a customer-facing product, a product owner asks how to reduce the risk that stakeholders experience unexpected quality changes at launch. Which approach best manages that risk?
options: Route all traffic to the new model at launch and monitor support tickets to catch any regressions quickly
Run the new model against a held-out evaluation set, then canary it to a small traffic percentage before full cutover
Pin the current model indefinitely and decline future upgrades to guarantee stable behavior
Approve the upgrade based on the published model-card benchmark scores rather than application-specific evals
rationale: Managing a model upgrade means validating against your own evaluation suite, which reflects your task, and then exposing a small slice of traffic first so regressions surface before they reach everyone. Full immediate cutover makes support tickets the test harness; pinning forever ignores deprecation timelines and forfeits improvements; and published benchmarks do not capture application-specific behavior, so they cannot replace your own evals as the go/no-go signal.
referenceText: Building and running application-specific evaluations before deployment.

stem: A finance stakeholder wants a monthly forecast of Claude API spend and asks which lever most directly explains cost. Which factor is the primary driver the architect should surface in cost reporting?
options: The number of distinct API keys and workspaces provisioned across teams
The count of input and output tokens processed, priced per model tier
The volume of HTTP 429 rate-limit responses received
The number of concurrent connections held open to the API
rationale: Anthropic bills by input and output tokens at per-model rates, so token volume times model price is the honest basis for a spend forecast. Provisioned keys and workspaces do not themselves incur cost, 429 responses are throttling signals rather than billed usage, and concurrency is not a pricing dimension. Grounding cost conversations in tokens-per-model connects directly to optimization levers like model right-sizing and prompt caching.
referenceText: Token counting and how usage maps to cost.

stem: An architect is justifying a plan to cut Claude inference cost on a large nightly document-processing job without degrading output quality. Which two techniques are legitimate cost levers to include? (Select 2.)
options: Submit the workload through the Message Batches API for its lower per-token pricing
Right-size to a smaller Claude model only after evaluations confirm it meets the quality bar
Lower the temperature parameter, since more deterministic sampling bills fewer output tokens
Cap max_tokens far below the expected output length so responses are truncated and cheaper
Disable automatic retries so transient failures are never re-billed
rationale: The Message Batches API offers reduced pricing for asynchronous, non-time-sensitive workloads like a nightly batch, and right-sizing to a smaller model is legitimate specifically when evals confirm quality holds. Temperature controls sampling randomness, not token count or price; capping max_tokens below the needed length truncates and degrades outputs; and disabling retries harms resilience without meaningfully reducing cost, since successful requests are billed regardless.
referenceText: Message Batches API pricing and asynchronous processing.

stem: A compliance stakeholder asks how the team pins the exact model and API behavior so an audited workload does not silently change between releases. Which practice provides that guarantee?
options: Specifying a dated model snapshot ID together with a fixed anthropic-version header on every request
Using a floating latest alias so the workload always benefits from the newest fixes and safety updates
Setting temperature to 0 so identical prompts always return byte-identical responses
Recording each response in an audit log so the exact output can be reproduced from storage
rationale: Reproducibility for audit means pinning both the specific dated model snapshot and the anthropic-version header, so neither the model weights nor the API contract shifts underneath the workload. A floating alias intentionally changes over time; temperature 0 reduces but does not guarantee identical outputs and does nothing to pin the model version; and logging responses preserves past outputs but does not pin behavior for new inputs, which is the audit concern.
referenceText: API versioning and the anthropic-version header.

stem: During an incident review, stakeholders want the architect to explain how the system should behave when the Claude API returns transient 529 (overloaded) responses under load. Which resilient design should be communicated as the standard?
options: Retry immediately without backoff, since 529 responses clear quickly and speed limits user impact
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, and fall back to a degraded response after a bounded number of attempts
Fail the request on the first 529 and surface the error, treating overload as non-retryable
Extend the request timeout to several minutes so the request waits out the overload without retrying
rationale: Overloaded responses are transient, so exponential backoff with jitter avoids synchronized retry storms, and a bounded attempt count with a graceful fallback keeps the user experience predictable. Immediate tight retries amplify the overload; treating a transient 529 as permanent fails recoverable requests; and simply extending the timeout holds connections open without a retry strategy and worsens latency.
referenceText: API error types, retries, and handling overloaded responses.
