stem: A healthcare customer's stakeholders ask whether the architecture can meet HIPAA obligations when using Claude. Which statement most accurately frames what the architect should communicate?
options: Claude output is inherently HIPAA-compliant, so no additional controls are required
Compliance depends on the deployment: a Business Associate Agreement plus data-handling controls such as minimization and audit logging must be in place
A zero-data-retention configuration alone makes the deployment HIPAA-compliant without further agreements
Sending PHI is acceptable as long as it is encrypted in transit with TLS to the API
rationale: HIPAA compliance is a property of the whole system and contractual posture, not the model alone: it requires a BAA covering PHI plus operational controls like data minimization and audit logging. No model is automatically compliant, a retention setting alone does not satisfy the contractual and administrative safeguards HIPAA requires, and TLS in transit is necessary but far from sufficient on its own.
referenceText: Anthropic compliance, data handling, and enterprise agreements.

stem: Legal stakeholders reviewing a RAG assistant want assurance that end users can trace answers back to source documents. Which Claude capability should the architect point to, and how should its limits be communicated?
options: Enable the Citations feature so responses reference supplied source passages, while noting citations reduce but do not eliminate the need for human review
Instruct the model in the system prompt to append a bibliography, guaranteeing every claim is grounded
Raise max_tokens so the model has room to quote entire documents verbatim in every answer
Rely on the model's training-data recall, since Claude cites the documents it was trained on
rationale: The Citations feature grounds responses in the document passages you supply and returns references to them, the correct traceability mechanism, paired with the honest caveat that it lowers but does not remove the need for human verification. A prompt-appended bibliography does not guarantee grounding and can be fabricated. Inflating max_tokens to quote whole documents is wasteful and off-point. Claude does not cite its training data, so relying on recall is incorrect.
referenceText: Citations feature for grounding responses in provided sources

stem: An architect is defining what should appear on a shared observability dashboard so that operations, product, and finance stakeholders each get the signal they need. Which metrics are appropriate to expose? (Select 3.)
options: End-to-end latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per endpoint
Input and output token consumption trended over time
Rate of API errors and retries by status code
The full plaintext of every prompt and completion for all users
Individual end users' account passwords and API keys used to authenticate each call
rationale: Latency percentiles serve operations and user-experience SLAs, token-consumption trends serve finance and cost forecasting, and error/retry rates serve reliability monitoring, the cross-stakeholder signals a dashboard should carry. Exposing full plaintext prompts and completions violates data-minimization and privacy principles, and surfacing user credentials or API keys is a serious security failure. Both over-exposure options are inappropriate regardless of stakeholder demand.
referenceText: Latency measurement, monitoring, and reducing latency

stem: A stakeholder asks why the architect chose to run a specific regulated workload on Claude via Amazon Bedrock rather than calling the Anthropic API directly. Which justification is most defensible to communicate?
options: Bedrock runs a newer, higher-quality version of Claude that the Anthropic API cannot access
Bedrock lets the workload stay within the customer's existing AWS account, IAM, and compliance boundary, easing governance sign-off
Bedrock removes all token-based pricing, making the workload effectively free
Bedrock guarantees zero latency because inference happens on the customer's own hardware
rationale: Deploying Claude through Bedrock keeps the workload inside the customer's established AWS account, IAM controls, and compliance footprint, the real governance advantage. Bedrock serves the same Claude models, not a superior version; it still charges for token usage; and inference runs in AWS's managed infrastructure, not on customer hardware, so a zero-latency guarantee is false.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock deployment and access

stem: An architect must communicate an SLA-relevant tradeoff to stakeholders who want both the lowest cost and the fastest possible response for an interactive assistant. Which framing correctly represents the levers available?
options: Cost and latency are independent, so both can be minimized simultaneously with no tradeoff
Prompt caching can lower both cost and latency on repeated context, while batch processing lowers cost but increases latency and is unsuitable for interactive use
The Message Batches API is the fastest option and should power all interactive traffic
Choosing a larger model always reduces latency because it needs fewer tokens to answer
rationale: Prompt caching genuinely helps both dimensions when large context is reused across calls, whereas batch processing trades latency for cost and is inappropriate for real-time interaction. It is misleading to claim cost and latency never trade off; the Batches API is asynchronous and higher-latency, so it cannot power interactive traffic; and larger models generally increase latency.
referenceText: Prompt caching for latency and cost reduction

stem: A privacy officer asks the architect how the design honors GDPR data-minimization when sending user records to Claude for summarization. Which practice should the architect present as the control?
options: Send each user's full record so Claude has maximum context, then delete logs weekly
Strip or tokenize fields not required for the summary before the prompt is constructed, sending only the minimum necessary data
Rely on Claude to ignore any personal data it does not need in the prompt
Encrypt the request in transit, which by itself satisfies data minimization
rationale: Data minimization means personal data never leaves your boundary unless needed for the task, so removing or tokenizing unnecessary fields before building the prompt is the correct control. Sending full records maximizes exposure; trusting the model to ignore surplus data does not stop it being transmitted and processed; and transport encryption protects data in transit but is a separate control from minimizing what is sent.
referenceText: Data handling, privacy, and usage policy

stem: The architect is asked to define the go/no-go criterion that a steering committee will use to approve promoting a prompt change from staging to production. Which criterion is the most rigorous to propose?
options: The new prompt looks better in a handful of manual spot checks by the author
The new prompt meets or exceeds baseline scores on a versioned evaluation set with defined pass thresholds
The new prompt produces longer, more detailed responses than the current one
The new prompt was reviewed and approved by the largest number of stakeholders in a meeting
rationale: A defensible promotion gate compares the change against a versioned evaluation set with pre-agreed pass thresholds, giving the committee an objective, reproducible signal. A few author spot checks are low-coverage and biased; longer responses are not a quality measure; and counting stakeholder votes measures consensus, not whether task performance actually improved.
referenceText: Designing evaluations and success criteria for changes

stem: An architect is writing a customer-facing communication about the known limitations of an LLM-powered feature, to set accurate expectations. Which statements are honest and appropriate to include? (Select 2.)
options: The assistant can generate plausible-sounding but incorrect information, so outputs should be verified for high-stakes decisions
Retrieval grounding and citations improve accuracy but do not guarantee every answer is correct
The assistant is factually infallible for any question within its training domain
The assistant will never expose sensitive data because it forgets prompts after each request
Setting temperature to zero makes the assistant's answers guaranteed correct
rationale: Honest communication acknowledges that the model can produce confident errors and that grounding reduces but does not eliminate mistakes. Claiming factual infallibility overstates capability; claiming the model can never expose sensitive data conflates statelessness with a security guarantee; and temperature zero reduces randomness but does not make answers correct.
referenceText: Reducing hallucinations and communicating model limitations

stem: A platform team wants to expose internal tools to Claude in a standardized, reusable way and asks the architect which approach to communicate to integrating teams as the interoperability standard. Which is the best answer?
options: Hard-code each tool's API calls into every application's prompt templates
Adopt the Model Context Protocol to expose tools and data through a standard server interface that multiple clients can reuse
Fine-tune a separate model per tool so no integration layer is needed
Embed tool credentials directly in the system prompt so Claude can call services itself
rationale: The Model Context Protocol is the open standard for exposing tools and data through servers any MCP-compatible client can consume, the reusable interoperability layer to standardize on. Hard-coding calls into each app creates brittle duplication; fine-tuning per tool is costly and provides no connectivity standard; and putting credentials in the system prompt is a serious security anti-pattern.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol overview and connecting tools to Claude

stem: An SRE stakeholder asks how the architecture should protect a user-facing endpoint from occasional multi-second tail latencies on Claude calls. Which pattern should the architect specify?
options: Remove client timeouts so slow requests always eventually complete
Set a bounded request timeout with a fast fallback path, and use streaming to return partial output sooner
Retry the same slow request in parallel five times and take the first response, on every call
Increase max_tokens so the model finishes faster
rationale: A bounded timeout with a defined fallback caps the worst-case user wait, and streaming delivers tokens as produced so perceived latency drops. Removing timeouts lets one slow request hang the experience indefinitely; always firing five hedged requests multiplies cost and load; and raising max_tokens permits longer, slower generations.
referenceText: Reducing latency and streaming responses

stem: A security stakeholder asks the architect to define what must be captured in audit logs for a governed Claude deployment, without violating data-minimization. Which logging design best balances the two?
options: Log full prompt and completion bodies for every request indefinitely to maximize traceability
Log request metadata (timestamp, user/service identity, model ID, token counts, outcome) and reference IDs, with sensitive content access-controlled and retention bounded
Log nothing, since any logging risks exposing regulated data
Log only successful requests, discarding all errors to keep the audit trail clean
rationale: Effective audit logging records who did what, when, against which model, with what outcome and volume, using reference IDs so investigators can trace events while sensitive payloads stay access-controlled and retention bounded. Storing full bodies indefinitely breaches minimization; logging nothing defeats governance; and dropping errors removes exactly the events auditors most need.
referenceText: Governance, audit logging, and data handling practices

stem: A business stakeholder requests a single Claude deployment target that keeps data inside the organization's existing Google Cloud project and IAM controls. Which option should the architect recommend and how should it be described?
options: Claude on Google Vertex AI, described as serving Claude models within the customer's GCP project and IAM boundary
Claude on Google Vertex AI, described as running a Google-built variant of Claude with different capabilities
The direct Anthropic API, described as automatically inheriting the customer's GCP IAM policies
A self-hosted download of Claude weights, described as the only way to stay within GCP
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI serves the same Claude models within the customer's GCP project and IAM boundary, exactly the governance property needed. It is not a Google-built variant; the direct Anthropic API does not inherit GCP IAM policies; and Anthropic does not distribute downloadable Claude weights for self-hosting.
referenceText: Claude on Google Vertex AI deployment and access

stem: During roadmap planning, a product stakeholder wants to build a production feature on a Claude capability that is currently offered as a beta. What should the architect advise to manage lifecycle risk?
options: Treat beta features identically to generally available ones, since betas never change
Flag that beta interfaces can change or be removed, isolate the dependency behind an abstraction, and monitor release notes before committing to hard GA-dependent timelines
Refuse to use any beta capability under any circumstances
Pin to the beta header and assume the behavior is frozen for the life of the product
rationale: Beta capabilities can evolve or be withdrawn, so the mature approach acknowledges that risk, wraps the dependency behind an abstraction to limit blast radius, and tracks release notes rather than binding GA-dependent commitments to it. Treating betas as stable ignores their status; a blanket refusal forfeits value with no nuance; and a beta header does not freeze behavior.
referenceText: API beta features and versioning practices

stem: An architect is establishing a feedback loop so that real production issues flow back into the evaluation suite over the system's lifecycle. Which practice best operationalizes this for stakeholders?
options: Rely on aggregate customer-satisfaction scores and never inspect individual failures
Capture flagged failures and edge cases, add representative examples to the versioned eval set, and re-run evals before each change
Rewrite the system prompt whenever a user complains, without recording the case
Freeze the evaluation set at launch so results remain comparable forever
rationale: A durable lifecycle loop converts real failures into new eval cases so future changes are regression-tested against actual production problems, and re-running before each change keeps quality from drifting. Satisfaction scores alone hide specific defects; ad hoc prompt edits with no recorded case lose the learning; and freezing the eval set blinds the suite to newly discovered failure modes.
referenceText: Iterating on evaluations and success criteria over time

stem: An architect receives notice that a Claude model powering a production workload has been placed on Anthropic's deprecation list with a published retirement date. Deprecated models continue serving requests normally until the retirement date, after which requests to that model return errors. What is the most appropriate lifecycle management action to plan and communicate to stakeholders?
options: Take no action until requests begin failing, because a deprecated model behaves identically to a supported one and Anthropic will extend the date if usage remains high

Establish a migration plan before the retirement date: evaluate the recommended successor model against the existing regression and eval suite, run a phased rollout with monitoring, and complete cutover ahead of the deadline
Pin the anthropic-version request header to an earlier date so the API continues routing to the deprecated model after its retirement date
Cut all production traffic over to the newest available model on the announcement day without re-running evaluations, since newer models are strictly better than the one being retired
rationale: Anthropic's model lifecycle moves a model from active to deprecated (still functional, scheduled for retirement) to retired (requests fail). The responsible action is a proactive, evaluated migration completed before the retirement date and communicated to stakeholders. Option 0 is a reactive anti-pattern and misstates that dates are negotiated by usage. Option 2 is wrong because anthropic-version governs request/response format compatibility, not model availability. Option 3 skips regression testing and relies on an unfounded 'strictly better' absolute, risking quality, latency, or cost regressions.
referenceText: Anthropic model deprecations and lifecycle: models transition through active, deprecated, and retired states; requests to retired models fail, and usage should be migrated to a recommended replacement before the retirement date, with at least 60 days notice for publicly released models.

stem: An architect is planning the migration of a production workload from a deprecated Claude model to its recommended successor and must prepare the stakeholder communication and lifecycle plan. Which practices should be part of that plan? (Select 2.)
options: Present side-by-side evaluation results (quality on the existing regression suite, plus latency and cost) comparing the successor against the current model, so stakeholders can make an informed go/no-go decision
Define and communicate explicit success criteria and a rollback plan for the phased rollout, so traffic can revert to the prior configuration if the successor underperforms
Skip re-running prompt evaluations, because successor models are always backward-compatible and never require prompt or system-message adjustments
Withhold all communication from stakeholders until after cutover is fully complete, to avoid raising concerns during the transition
Immediately remove the deprecated model from monitoring and observability dashboards to reduce dashboard noise during the migration window
rationale: Sound lifecycle management pairs evidence with a safety net. Option 0 gives stakeholders objective, comparable data (quality via the existing eval suite, plus latency and cost) to authorize the change, consistent with testing replacement models well before the retirement date. Option 1 provides measurable success criteria and a rollback path for a resilient phased rollout. Option 2 is wrong: upgrades can introduce breaking changes and often require prompt tuning ('always/never' signals over-broad). Option 3 hides risk and removes informed sign-off. Option 4 is wrong because observability of the outgoing model is exactly what enables comparison and rollback during the window.
referenceText: Migrating between Claude models: migrate all usage to a suitable replacement before the retirement date and thoroughly test replacement models on your tasks well before the deadline; combine with a monitored rollout that has clear success criteria and a rollback plan.

stem: A team runs a customer-support assistant that prepends a 6,000-token product manual and a fixed system prompt to every request, then appends the user's short question. Traffic is bursty but the static preamble never changes within a session. Which change most directly reduces per-request input cost without altering answer quality?
options: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static manual and system prompt so repeated calls read the cached prefix
Move the product manual into the user turn so it is billed as output tokens instead of input tokens
Switch every request to the Message Batches API to obtain the 50% batch discount on interactive traffic
Lower max_tokens so the model is forced to reuse earlier context automatically
rationale: Prompt caching stores a static prefix so subsequent requests within the cache lifetime pay a heavily reduced cache-read rate on those tokens; placing the cache_control breakpoint after the unchanging manual/system content maximizes reuse. Moving the manual to the user turn does not change token pricing (input is input); Message Batches is asynchronous (up to 24h) and unsuitable for interactive support; and lowering max_tokens caps output length, not input-prefix reuse.
referenceText: Prompt caching lets you cache a static prefix; cache reads are billed at a fraction of base input token price, and cache_control marks the end of the cacheable prefix.

stem: An analytics team must classify 400,000 archived support tickets overnight. Results are not needed in real time, and cost is the primary concern. Which approach best fits these requirements?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs within a 24-hour window at a reduced price
Send 400,000 synchronous Messages API calls in a tight loop and rely on automatic retries for any 429 responses
Use prompt caching alone to make the 400,000 synchronous calls cheap enough to run interactively
Increase the organization's rate-limit ceiling and stream every request to reduce latency
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume asynchronous workloads, processing batches within 24 hours at a 50% discount relative to standard synchronous pricing, an exact match for non-urgent bulk classification. Firing hundreds of thousands of synchronous calls stresses rate limits and pays full price; prompt caching reduces repeated-prefix cost but provides no batch economics; and raising limits and streaming optimize latency, which the scenario does not require.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously, returning results within 24 hours at a 50% discount over standard API pricing.

stem: A product owner insists on using the largest available Claude model for a high-throughput input-validation step that only checks whether a string is a valid ISO date. Latency and cost per call are both climbing. What is the most appropriate architectural recommendation?
options: Right-size the task to a smaller, faster model (e.g., a Haiku-tier model) and reserve larger models for steps that require deep reasoning
Keep the largest model but disable streaming to cut token overhead
Route the validation through the Batches API so the largest model becomes cheaper
Increase max_tokens so the model finishes reasoning in a single pass
rationale: Model right-sizing matches task complexity to model capability; a simple, deterministic validation is a textbook case for the fastest, lowest-cost tier, freeing larger models for genuinely hard reasoning. Disabling streaming does not reduce token count or model cost; Batches lowers price but is asynchronous and still uses an oversized model; and raising max_tokens increases potential output cost.
referenceText: Choose the model that fits the task: faster, smaller models handle simple tasks efficiently, while larger models are reserved for complex reasoning.

stem: A RAG pipeline indexes 300-page policy PDFs. Users complain that answers cite the right document but include unrelated neighboring text, and retrieval sometimes misses the relevant passage entirely. Chunks are currently created by splitting on a fixed 4,000-character boundary regardless of structure. What chunking change best addresses both symptoms?
options: Chunk along semantic/structural boundaries (sections, paragraphs) with modest overlap so each chunk is topically coherent and context is not severed mid-idea
Increase the fixed chunk size to 40,000 characters so each chunk contains more of the document
Remove overlap entirely and shrink chunks to one sentence each to maximize retrieval precision
Embed the entire document as a single chunk so retrieval always returns the whole policy
rationale: Structure-aware chunking with overlap keeps each chunk topically coherent (reducing irrelevant bleed-in) and prevents relevant ideas from being split across a hard boundary (reducing misses). Very large fixed chunks reintroduce unrelated text and dilute embeddings; one-sentence chunks lose surrounding context and hurt recall; and a single whole-document chunk defeats retrieval granularity.
referenceText: Effective chunking respects document structure and uses overlap so semantically related content stays together and retrieval returns coherent, relevant passages.

stem: During RAG design review, an engineer plans to call a Claude 'embeddings endpoint' to vectorize documents. What should the architect clarify?
options: The Claude API does not provide a native embeddings endpoint; Anthropic recommends a dedicated embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) for generating vectors
Every Claude model returns an embedding vector in the response alongside generated text
Embeddings can only be produced by enabling the citations feature on the Messages API
Claude generates embeddings automatically whenever prompt caching is enabled
rationale: Anthropic's documentation states Claude does not offer its own embedding model and points users to third-party embedding providers such as Voyage AI. Claude message responses contain generated text and usage data, not embedding vectors; citations ground answers in provided documents but do not produce embeddings; and prompt caching reduces repeated-prefix cost and is unrelated to vector generation.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model; for embeddings, Anthropic recommends providers such as Voyage AI.

stem: A regulated-industry customer requires that every claim in a Claude-generated answer be traceable to a specific passage in the source documents they supply. Which built-in capability most directly satisfies this?
options: Pass the sources as document content blocks and enable the Citations feature so responses reference the exact quoted passages used
Instruct the model in the system prompt to 'always cite sources' and trust the free-text citations it writes inline
Enable prompt caching on the documents so the model attributes each reused passage automatically
Set temperature to zero so the model deterministically reproduces the source text verbatim
rationale: The Citations feature returns structured references tying generated statements to the exact source passages provided as document blocks, giving verifiable traceability. A prompt-only 'always cite' instruction produces unverified free text prone to fabricated references. Prompt caching only affects cost/latency of reused input, not attribution. Zero temperature reduces variability but provides no structured passage-level traceability.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude ground responses in source documents and return references to the specific passages it used.

stem: A team wants an automated, repeatable way to measure whether prompt changes improve answer quality before shipping. They currently eyeball 10 outputs per release. What is the strongest improvement to their evaluation practice?
options: Build a versioned evaluation set with representative inputs and graded criteria, and run it automatically (including model-graded scoring where appropriate) on every prompt change
Replace human review with a single production A/B test and ship whichever variant receives higher traffic
Evaluate only after customers report incidents, to avoid spending effort on cases that already pass
Rely on a self-assessment the model appends to each production response as the quality metric
rationale: A stable, versioned eval set with defined grading criteria (including LLM-as-judge where suitable), run automatically on each change, gives objective, repeatable regression signal before release. Traffic volume is not a quality signal. Waiting for customer incidents is reactive and misses pre-ship regressions. In-line model self-assessment is unreliable and unaudited as a governing metric.
referenceText: Develop empirical, versioned evaluations with representative test cases and grading criteria, and run them systematically to measure changes.

stem: When designing an LLM-as-judge evaluation to grade free-text answers, which practice most improves the reliability of the automated scores?
options: Give the judge a rubric with explicit, discrete criteria and ask it to output a structured verdict with reasoning, rather than a single unanchored 1-10 score
Raise the judge's temperature so it considers a wider range of possible scores before deciding
Have the same prompt under test grade its own outputs to keep the judge aligned with the author's intent
Ask the judge only for a pass/fail bit with no criteria so results are simple to aggregate
rationale: Explicit, discrete rubric criteria plus structured output and reasoning make LLM-graded scores more consistent and interpretable than an unanchored numeric scale. High temperature adds noise, reducing reproducibility. Letting the prompt under test grade itself introduces bias and defeats independent evaluation. A bare pass/fail with no criteria is under-specified and yields inconsistent judgments.
referenceText: Model-based grading works best with clear rubrics and criteria; ask the grader to reason and return structured judgments for consistency.

stem: An SRE team needs to correlate a specific problematic Claude API response with Anthropic's logs when filing a support case. Which element should they capture from every response to enable this?
options: The unique request ID returned with each API response (e.g., the request-id header), logged alongside their own trace context
The final answer text only, since Anthropic can reconstruct the request from the returned wording
The user's raw prompt, stored in plaintext indefinitely for every request
The account API key, embedded in each log line so support can verify the caller's identity
rationale: Each API response includes a unique request ID; logging it with local trace context lets teams reference the exact call in support and observability workflows. Answer text alone is not a reliable correlation key. Storing raw prompts indefinitely is a data-minimization and privacy liability. Logging API keys is a serious security anti-pattern.
referenceText: API responses include a unique request identifier that can be used to reference a specific request when contacting support.

stem: A latency-sensitive chat feature shows a blank screen for several seconds while Claude composes long answers. The team cannot shorten the answers. Which change most improves perceived latency?
options: Stream the response so tokens render incrementally as they are generated, reducing time-to-first-visible-token
Increase max_tokens so the full answer arrives in one larger chunk rather than several
Buffer the complete response server-side and send it once finished to avoid rendering partial text
Move the request to the Message Batches API to obtain faster interactive delivery
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens incrementally, so users see output begin almost immediately, dramatically improving perceived latency for long answers. Raising max_tokens does not make generation start sooner. Buffering the full response server-side prevents any incremental rendering, worsening perceived latency. Batches is asynchronous with up-to-24h turnaround, the opposite of what interactive chat needs.
referenceText: Streaming returns partial results as they are generated, improving perceived responsiveness for long outputs.

stem: A production integration occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses during traffic spikes. What is the recommended resilient handling?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring any retry-after guidance, rather than retrying immediately in a tight loop
Immediately retry the same request in a tight loop until it succeeds
Treat both 429 and 529 as permanent failures and surface an error to the user without retry
Rotate to a different API key on every 429 to work around the rate limit
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient conditions; the documented pattern is exponential backoff with jitter (respecting retry-after) to let load subside while avoiding thundering-herd retries. Tight-loop retries amplify overload. Treating transient errors as permanent needlessly degrades availability. Rotating keys does not change organization-level limits and violates usage policy.
referenceText: For 429 rate-limit and 529 overloaded errors, retry with exponential backoff; these are transient conditions.

stem: An architect is hardening a Claude integration against provider-side disruption for a mission-critical workflow. Which resilience techniques are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Configure sensible client timeouts with bounded retries using exponential backoff for transient errors
Define a fallback path (e.g., a secondary model or degraded response) when the primary call repeatedly fails
Remove all timeouts so requests can run indefinitely until they eventually succeed
Cache and replay a previous successful response as the answer to every future user question
Retry non-idempotent failed calls unconditionally with no backoff between attempts
rationale: Bounded retries with exponential backoff plus explicit timeouts prevent hangs and handle transient faults, while a defined fallback (alternate model or graceful degradation) preserves continuity when the primary path fails. Removing timeouts lets requests hang and exhaust resources. Replaying one cached response for every question returns wrong answers. Unconditional, backoff-free retries amplify load and risk duplicate side effects.
referenceText: Resilient designs use timeouts, bounded exponential-backoff retries for transient errors, and fallback strategies for sustained failures.

stem: A healthcare provider wants to build a patient-facing summarization tool on Claude that will process protected health information (PHI). Which is the correct compliance prerequisite before sending PHI?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and use the API under the terms that support HIPAA-regulated workloads
Rely on the default consumer terms, since TLS encryption in transit alone satisfies HIPAA
Run the PHI through a public evaluation dataset first to validate the tool's accuracy
Ensure PHI is deleted from your own database within 24 hours, which makes the usage HIPAA-eligible
rationale: Handling PHI under HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement with Anthropic and using the service under the corresponding terms; encryption in transit alone does not confer HIPAA compliance. Consumer/default terms are insufficient for PHI. Posting PHI to any public dataset is a breach. Deleting PHI from your own store does not make the provider relationship HIPAA-eligible without a BAA.
referenceText: Customers with a Business Associate Agreement may use the API for workloads involving PHI under HIPAA; a BAA is required.

stem: A GDPR-focused review of a Claude RAG application asks which data-handling practices reduce privacy risk. Which practices are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Apply data minimization—send only the fields necessary for the task and redact PII the task does not require
Maintain audit logs of who queried what and when, with appropriate access controls and retention limits
Send every field in the source records verbatim so the model always has full context regardless of need
Store all raw user prompts indefinitely in plaintext to simplify future debugging
Disable all logging entirely so no personal data is ever recorded anywhere in the system
rationale: Data minimization (sending only what the task needs, redacting unnecessary PII) and controlled audit logging with retention limits are core privacy-by-design practices that reduce exposure while preserving accountability. Sending all fields regardless of need violates minimization. Indefinite plaintext prompt retention creates unnecessary risk. Disabling all logging removes the audit trail needed for compliance and incident response.
referenceText: Follow data minimization and privacy-by-design: collect and transmit only necessary data, and keep auditable, access-controlled logs with defined retention.

stem: A compliance officer asks whether prompts and outputs submitted to the Claude API are used to train Anthropic's models by default for commercial API usage. What is the accurate statement to communicate?
options: By default, Anthropic does not train its models on commercial API inputs and outputs unless the customer explicitly opts in or the content is reported
All commercial API traffic is used for model training unless the customer signs a separate opt-out addendum
Whether traffic is used for training is determined by the temperature parameter set on each request
Enabling prompt caching automatically consents to training on the cached content
rationale: Anthropic's commercial terms state that inputs and outputs from commercial API usage are not used to train models by default, absent explicit opt-in or a trust-and-safety report. There is no addendum required to avoid default training. Temperature is a sampling parameter unrelated to data governance. Prompt caching affects cost/latency and does not constitute training consent.
referenceText: Anthropic does not train its models on inputs or outputs from commercial API customers by default.

stem: An enterprise standardized on AWS wants to run Claude within their existing VPC, IAM, and regional data-residency controls. Which deployment option best fits, and what regional consideration applies?
options: Use Claude on Amazon Bedrock, invoking models via Bedrock APIs/IAM; confirm the target model is available in the chosen AWS Region or use cross-region inference where offered
Use the first-party Anthropic API and route its traffic through AWS PrivateLink, which makes it equivalent to a Bedrock deployment
Deploy Claude on Bedrock and assume every model is available in every AWS Region under identical model IDs
Use Google Vertex AI, since it integrates natively with AWS IAM roles and VPC networking
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets AWS customers invoke Claude through Bedrock's APIs under their IAM and networking controls; model availability varies by Region, so architects must verify availability or use cross-region inference. Routing first-party API traffic through PrivateLink does not make it a Bedrock deployment or change its governance model. Model availability is not uniform across Regions. Vertex AI is Google Cloud, not AWS, and does not use AWS IAM/VPC.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is accessed via Bedrock APIs and IAM; model availability differs by AWS Region, and cross-region inference may be used.

stem: A Google Cloud customer wants to call Claude using their existing GCP project, service-account authentication, and regional endpoints. Which statement correctly describes using Claude on Vertex AI?
options: Requests are authenticated with Google Cloud credentials and target a specified GCP region/endpoint, with the model referenced by its Vertex model identifier
Vertex AI requires an Anthropic first-party API key passed in the x-api-key header instead of Google credentials
Claude on Vertex AI can only be invoked from on-premises hardware, not from GCP-hosted services
Model IDs on Vertex AI are identical strings to the first-party Anthropic API, with no platform-specific naming
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication (e.g., service-account/ADC credentials) and region-scoped endpoints, referencing models by their Vertex identifiers. It does not use Anthropic's x-api-key auth. It is designed for GCP-hosted and other clients, not exclusively on-prem. Platform model identifiers differ from first-party model strings, so IDs are not identical.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI authenticates with Google Cloud credentials, uses regional endpoints, and references models by their Vertex model identifiers.

stem: A team is explaining the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to stakeholders. Which statements about MCP are accurate? (Select 3.)
options: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context (tools, resources, prompts) to LLMs via a client-server architecture
MCP servers expose capabilities such as tools (model-invokable actions) and resources (readable data) that clients can discover
An MCP host connects to MCP servers through MCP clients to access those capabilities
MCP removes the need for any authentication or authorization when a server exposes sensitive tools
MCP is a proprietary, Anthropic-only wire format that cannot be implemented by third parties
rationale: MCP is an open, client-server protocol standardizing how context—tools, resources, and prompts—is supplied to models; servers expose discoverable capabilities and hosts connect via clients. It does not remove the need for auth/authorization—sensitive tools still require access controls. It is an open standard implementable by anyone, not an Anthropic-only proprietary format.
referenceText: MCP is an open protocol using a client-server architecture where servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLM applications.

stem: An MCP server will expose a tool that can issue refunds against real customer accounts. From a governance and safety standpoint, what is the most important design control for the host application?
options: Require human-in-the-loop approval (and scoped authorization) before executing high-impact tool calls, and surface the tool's action clearly to the user
Label the tool as read-only in its description so the model does not treat it as dangerous
Grant the MCP client unrestricted access to all connected servers so the workflow never blocks
Rely on a system-prompt instruction telling the model to 'be careful' when issuing refunds
rationale: For consequential, state-changing tools, MCP guidance emphasizes human approval and properly scoped authorization before execution, with transparency about the action—a defense that does not depend on model behavior alone. Falsely labeling a mutating tool read-only misrepresents it and adds no real control. Granting unrestricted client access expands the blast radius. A 'be careful' instruction is not an enforceable safeguard against irreversible actions.
referenceText: Hosts should keep humans in the loop and require explicit authorization before invoking tools that take consequential actions.

stem: A platform team is presenting a Claude feature's rollout plan to executives and legal. Which lifecycle practice best supports safe operation and clear stakeholder communication after launch?
options: Define ownership, monitoring/eval dashboards, a model-version upgrade/deprecation plan, and a rollback path, and review them with stakeholders on a set cadence
Freeze the pinned model version permanently so the integration never needs revalidation
Communicate with stakeholders only when an outage occurs, to avoid burdening them with routine updates
Delegate post-launch decisions to the model by having it self-report its own health status
rationale: Sustainable operation needs clear ownership, ongoing monitoring/evaluation, a plan for model upgrades and deprecations, and a rollback path—reviewed with stakeholders regularly, which also keeps communication proactive. Freezing a version forever ignores deprecations and eventually breaks. Communicating only during outages is reactive and erodes trust. Letting the model self-report health lacks independent verification.
referenceText: Manage the lifecycle with monitoring, planned model upgrades and deprecation handling, rollback options, and regular stakeholder review.

stem: An interactive customer-support assistant sends the same 40,000-token product manual and policy set as a static prefix on every turn of a live conversation, followed by the short, changing user message. Engineers report high per-request cost and slow time-to-first-token, but responses must still stream back within an interactive latency budget (batch/async processing is not acceptable). Which change best reduces both cost and latency for the repeated prefix while preserving interactive responses?
options: Enable prompt caching so the large static prefix is written to cache once and read at a reduced rate on subsequent turns within the cache lifetime
Move the workload to the Message Batches API to obtain the batch discount on every request
Lower the max_tokens setting on each request so the model processes fewer tokens overall
Switch permanently to the smallest available Claude model regardless of answer-quality requirements
rationale: Prompt caching is designed for a large, static, repeated prefix: the first call writes the prefix to cache and later calls within the cache TTL read those tokens at a large discount and with reduced prefill latency, while the changing suffix is processed normally—cutting both cost and time-to-first-token without leaving the interactive streaming model. The Message Batches API gives a discount but processes asynchronously (up to 24h), violating the interactive requirement. Lowering max_tokens only caps output length; the 40k-token prefix is still re-processed each turn. Forcing the smallest model ignores quality needs and still re-processes the full prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching: cache a large static prefix once and read it at reduced cost and latency on subsequent requests within the cache lifetime; contrasted with the Message Batches API for asynchronous, throughput-oriented workloads.
